11.notes:
Psalms - Chapter 11
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
A psalm of trust — when counseled to flee like a bird, David refuses: 'In the LORD put I my trust.' The LORD is in His holy temple testing the righteous and wicked; He loves righteousness.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Multiple authors — primarily David (73 psalms attributed),
plus Asaph (12), Sons of Korah (11), Solomon (2), Moses (1),
Heman (1), Ethan (1), and anonymous. The Psalter was compiled over
approximately 1000 years and served as Israel's hymnal and prayer
book. The book is divided into five 'books' (1-41, 42-72, 73-89,
90-106, 107-150), paralleling the five books of Moses. Key themes:
worship, lament, praise, trust, kingship, creation, wisdom,
Messianic prophecy, and the full range of human emotion brought
before God.

Classification: Trust/Confidence Psalm
Attributed Author: David — "To the chief Musician, A Psalm of
David"
Key Themes: Refusing fear-based flight, God's sovereign
observation from heaven, testing of the righteous, judgment on the
wicked, beholding God's face

Historical Context: This short, powerful psalm likely dates from
David's time fleeing Saul. Well-meaning advisors urge him to flee
"like a bird to your mountain" — to abandon his post and run.
David's response is not recklessness but theology: the LORD is in
His temple, His throne is in heaven, His eyes see everything.
Why flee when the sovereign God is watching? The psalm addresses
the temptation to abandon faith-based action for fear-based
escape. The question "If the foundations be destroyed, what can
the righteous do?" (v.3) is one of Scripture's most profound
questions about societal collapse.

Structure:
- Rejection of Fearful Advice (vv.1-3)
- God's Sovereign Observation (vv.4-5)
- Judgment on the Wicked (v.6)
- The Reward of the Upright (v.7)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.1              | 1 Samuel    | 23      | 14                 | David in the wilderness, trusting God rather than fleeing permanently
v.3              | Isaiah      | 19      | 10                 | When foundations are destroyed, only God remains the foundation (Isa 28:16)
v.4              | Habakkuk    | 2       | 20                 | "The LORD is in his holy temple: let all the earth keep silence." Same truth — God is sovereign
v.5              | 1 Peter     | 1       | 7                  | "The trial of your faith, being much more precious than gold." God tests the righteous through fire
v.6              | Genesis     | 19      | 24                 | "The LORD rained upon Sodom and upon Gomorrah brimstone and fire." The historical precedent for v.6's imagery
v.7              | Matthew     | 5       | 8                  | "Blessed are the pure in heart: for they shall see God." NT fulfillment of beholding His face
v.7              | Revelation  | 22      | 4                  | "And they shall see his face." The ultimate fulfillment

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.2              | KJV         | "privily shoot" / ESV: "shoot in the dark" — Hebrew "b'mo ophel" = in darkness/gloom. The wicked attack secretly, from concealment. ESV is more literal.
v.5              | KJV         | "The LORD trieth the righteous" / ESV: "The LORD tests the righteous" — Hebrew "bachan" = test/examine/prove. Both translations convey the same idea: God purifies the righteous through testing, while His soul hates (strong rejection) the violent wicked.
v.6              | KJV         | "rain snares, fire and brimstone" / ESV: "rain coals on the wicked; fire and sulfur" — Hebrew "pachim" is debated — "snares" (KJV) or "coals" (ESV). The Sodom-and- Gomorrah imagery is unmistakable either way.
v.7              | KJV         | "his countenance doth behold the upright" / ESV: "the upright shall behold his face" — These are OPPOSITE subjects. KJV: God looks at the upright (divine attention). ESV: The upright look at God (beatific vision). Hebrew can support both — "yashar yechezu panemo." ESV's reading connects to the great biblical hope of seeing God face-to-face.

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. Don't let fear-counsel override faith-conviction (v.1). Others may advise
   flight when God calls you to stand. Test all advice — even well-meaning
   advice — against your trust in God.

2. When foundations collapse, look up (vv.3-4). The answer to "what can the
   righteous do?" is not panic but perspective. God's throne is undisturbed
   by earthly chaos. Heavenly reality remains stable when earthly reality
   crumbles.

3. Testing is not punishment (v.5). God tests the righteous — not to destroy
   but to refine. The same God who hates the violent tests the faithful.
   Distinguish between divine discipline (refining) and divine judgment
   (punishing).

4. The wicked receive their "cup" (v.6). The cup metaphor appears throughout
   Scripture for divine judgment (Jer 25:15; Rev 14:10). The wicked's
   portion is not arbitrary — it is the logical consequence of loving
   violence.

5. The ultimate reward is God's face (v.7). Not prosperity, not ease, not
   vindication before enemies — but seeing God. The highest good is not what
   God gives but who God IS. The upright will behold Him.

RESOURCES
------------------------------------
The Bible Project — Psalms Overview:
  https://bibleproject.com/explore/video/psalms/

David Guzik — Psalms 11 Written Commentary:
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/psalms-11/

BibleProject Podcast:
  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

Translations referenced: KJV, ESV, ASV, NET, WEB
Commentary sources: Enduring Word (enduringword.com), Charles
Spurgeon (Public Domain)

12.notes:
Psalms - Chapter 12
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
A lament over deceitful speech — the faithful have vanished, flattering lips and double hearts abound, but the words of the LORD are pure words, like silver refined seven times.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Multiple authors — primarily David (73 psalms attributed),
plus Asaph (12), Sons of Korah (11), Solomon (2), Moses (1),
Heman (1), Ethan (1), and anonymous. The Psalter was compiled over
approximately 1000 years and served as Israel's hymnal and prayer
book. The book is divided into five 'books' (1-41, 42-72, 73-89,
90-106, 107-150), paralleling the five books of Moses. Key themes:
worship, lament, praise, trust, kingship, creation, wisdom,
Messianic prophecy, and the full range of human emotion brought
before God.

Classification: Communal Lament
Attributed Author: David — "To the chief Musician upon Sheminith,
A Psalm of David"
Key Themes: The disappearance of godly people, corrupt speech
(flattery, lies, boasting), God's pure words contrasted with human
deception, divine intervention for the oppressed

Historical Context: David laments a society where faithful people
seem to have vanished and deceitful speech dominates. "Sheminith"
(the eighth) again indicates a low-pitched musical setting. The
central contrast is between human words (vv.2-4: flattery, lies,
boasting) and God's words (v.6: pure as silver refined seven
times). The psalm's great contribution is v.6 — one of the
strongest statements of Scripture's purity in all the Bible.
God's response (v.5) comes as a direct divine oracle mid-psalm:
"Now will I arise." This is rare — God speaking directly within
a psalm.

Structure:
- Cry for Help — Faithful Have Vanished (v.1)
- The Problem: Corrupt Speech (vv.2-4)
- God's Direct Response (v.5)
- The Purity of God's Words (v.6)
- Trust in God's Preservation (vv.7-8)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.1              | 1 Kings     | 19      | 10                 | Elijah: "I, even I only, am left." Same sense of isolation in faithfulness
v.1              | Micah       | 7       | 2                  | "The good man is perished out of the earth." Micah echoes David's lament
v.2              | Psalm       | 28      | 3                  | "Which speak peace to their neighbours, but mischief is in their hearts." Same duplicity
v.5              | Exodus      | 3       | 7-8                | "I have surely seen the affliction of my people... and I am come down to deliver them." God arises for the oppressed
v.6              | Psalm       | 19      | 7-9                | "The law of the LORD is perfect." Extended praise of Scripture's purity
v.6              | Proverbs    | 30      | 5                  | "Every word of God is pure." Same affirmation
v.6              | Psalm       | 119     | 140                | "Thy word is very pure: therefore thy servant loveth it." Love flowing from purity
v.8              | Proverbs    | 29      | 2                  | "When the wicked beareth rule, the people mourn." Same principle of corrupt leadership

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.1              | KJV         | "the godly man ceaseth" / ESV: "the godly one is gone" — Hebrew "gamar" = completed, ceased, ended. The faithful have been used up, exhausted, disappeared. Both translations convey the desolation.
v.2              | KJV         | "with a double heart do they speak" / ESV: "with a double heart they speak" — Literally "with a heart and a heart" (lev va-lev). Two-faced, two-hearted — one presented, one concealed. The original "double-minded."
v.5              | KJV         | "from him that puffeth at him" / ESV: "for which he longs" — Significantly different. KJV: God protects FROM the one who contemptuously "puffs" at the poor. ESV: God gives the safety the poor LONGS FOR. The Hebrew is ambiguous; both interpretations have merit.
v.7              | KJV         | "Thou shalt keep them" / ESV: "You, O LORD, will keep them; you will guard us" — The pronoun "them" is debated: does it refer to God's WORDS (v.6) or God's PEOPLE? KJV allows both; ESV switches to "us" suggesting people. This verse is significant in discussions of biblical preservation.

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. Faithfulness can feel lonely (v.1). The sense that godly people have
   disappeared is a recurring human experience. But remember: God told
   Elijah there were still 7,000 who had not bowed to Baal. You may feel
   alone — you are not.

2. Human words are corrupt; God's words are pure (vv.2-4 vs. v.6). The
   contrast is absolute. In a world of flattery, deception, and boasting,
   only God's Word can be fully trusted. Seven-times- refined silver has no
   impurity. Build your life on words that have been tested and found
   perfect.

3. The sighing of the needy moves God (v.5). Not eloquent prayer, not
   powerful advocacy — SIGHING. The inarticulate groan of the oppressed
   reaches God's ear and provokes Him to rise. If you can only sigh, that is
   enough.

4. When vile people are exalted, wickedness multiplies (v.8). Culture flows
   from leadership. The character of those in power sets the moral tone for
   everyone. Pray for — and pursue — righteous leadership.

5. God preserves what is His (v.7). Whether this refers to His words or His
   people (or both), the promise is preservation. What belongs to God does
   not perish. His truth endures; His people endure.

13.notes:
Psalms - Chapter 13
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
A brief lament — David cries 'How long, O LORD?' four times, feeling forgotten and overwhelmed by his enemy, yet ends trusting in God's steadfast love and singing because God has dealt bountifully.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Multiple authors — primarily David (73 psalms attributed),
plus Asaph (12), Sons of Korah (11), Solomon (2), Moses (1),
Heman (1), Ethan (1), and anonymous. The Psalter was compiled over
approximately 1000 years and served as Israel's hymnal and prayer
book. The book is divided into five 'books' (1-41, 42-72, 73-89,
90-106, 107-150), paralleling the five books of Moses. Key themes:
worship, lament, praise, trust, kingship, creation, wisdom,
Messianic prophecy, and the full range of human emotion brought
before God.

Classification: Individual Lament (Model Lament)
Attributed Author: David — "To the chief Musician, A Psalm of
David"
Key Themes: God's apparent forgetfulness, "how long?" repeated,
the journey from despair to praise, trust in steadfast love,
the decision to sing

Historical Context: Psalm 13 is considered the "model lament" —
the shortest and most structurally perfect example of the lament
genre. In just six verses it contains all the elements: complaint
(vv.1-2), petition (vv.3-4), and confidence/praise (vv.5-6). The
fourfold "how long?" (vv.1-2) expresses mounting frustration with
God's silence. No specific historical occasion is identified, but
the pattern fits David's years fleeing Saul — prolonged suffering
with no visible end. The beauty of the psalm is its movement:
it begins in abandonment and ends in singing, with no explanation
of what changed between vv.4 and 5 except the decision to trust.

Structure:
- Complaint: Four "How Long?" Questions (vv.1-2)
- Petition: Three Requests (vv.3-4)
- Praise: Trust and Singing (vv.5-6)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.1              | Psalm       | 10      | 1                  | "Why standest thou afar off, O LORD?" Same sense of divine distance
v.1              | Psalm       | 44      | 24                 | "Wherefore hidest thou thy face?" Communal version of the same question
v.3              | Psalm       | 6       | 5                  | "In death there is no remembrance of thee." Same argument: keep me alive for Your glory
v.5              | Psalm       | 52      | 8                  | "I trust in the mercy of God for ever and ever." Same anchor: God's mercy
v.5              | Lamentations | 3       | 21-23              | "This I recall to my mind, therefore have I hope. It is of the LORD's mercies that we are not consumed." Same pivot from despair to hope via remembered mercy
v.6              | Psalm       | 116     | 7                  | "The LORD hath dealt bountifully with thee." Same grateful memory of past provision

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.1              | KJV         | "How long wilt thou forget me" / ESV: "Will you forget me forever?" — Both capture the anguish. The Hebrew construction emphasizes duration — the suffering feels eternal. "Forever?" is the question suffering always asks.
v.3              | KJV         | "lighten mine eyes" / ESV: "light up my eyes" — Hebrew "ha'ira eynay" = make my eyes shine/be bright. Dim eyes indicate approaching death (1 Sam 14:27-29). David asks for vitality — life-light in his eyes — lest death overtake him.
v.5              | KJV         | "thy mercy" / ESV: "your steadfast love" — Hebrew "chesed" = covenant faithfulness, steadfast love. ESV's translation preserves the covenantal dimension — this is not general kindness but committed, unbreakable love.
v.6              | KJV         | "he hath dealt bountifully" / ESV: "he has dealt bountifully" — Same. Hebrew "gamal" = dealt fully, repaid with good. Past-tense confidence: God HAS BEEN good, and that record is the basis for future trust.

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. "How long?" is the prayer of endurance (vv.1-2). The question implies
   duration — you have already been waiting. Long-term suffering produces
   this prayer. It is legitimate, biblical, and heard by God. Bring your
   "how long?" to Him.

2. The psalm moves from complaint to praise with NO external change
   (vv.4→5). Nothing happened between verses 4 and 5. The enemies didn't
   leave. God didn't audibly answer. David simply CHOSE to trust. This
   teaches that the turn from despair to faith is often a choice, not a
   circumstantial change.

3. Past faithfulness grounds present trust (v.6). "He HATH dealt
   bountifully" — David remembers. When the present is dark, recall the
   past. God's track record is evidence. If He was faithful then, He is
   faithful now.

4. Six verses — complete emotional journey. From the bottom of despair to
   singing praise in six verses. This psalm gives permission to feel
   everything AND to choose worship. The two are not contradictory. You can
   hurt AND trust simultaneously.

5. The brevity is the power. This psalm does not explain, theologize, or
   defend. It simply models the complete cycle of faith under pressure. Use
   it as a template: name your complaint, bring your request, choose trust,
   decide to sing.

3. They have all turned aside; together they have become corrupt; there is none who does good, not even one.
4. Have they no knowledge, all the evildoers who eat up my people as they eat bread and do not call upon the LORD?
5. There they are in great terror, for God is with the generation of the righteous.

14.notes:
Psalms - Chapter 14
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
A wisdom psalm on universal depravity — 'The fool hath said in his heart, There is no God'; all have turned aside, none does good; yet the LORD is refuge for the poor, and salvation will come from Zion.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Multiple authors — primarily David (73 psalms attributed),
plus Asaph (12), Sons of Korah (11), Solomon (2), Moses (1),
Heman (1), Ethan (1), and anonymous. The Psalter was compiled over
approximately 1000 years and served as Israel's hymnal and prayer
book. The book is divided into five 'books' (1-41, 42-72, 73-89,
90-106, 107-150), paralleling the five books of Moses. Key themes:
worship, lament, praise, trust, kingship, creation, wisdom,
Messianic prophecy, and the full range of human emotion brought
before God.

Classification: Wisdom/Prophetic Lament
Attributed Author: David — "To the chief Musician, A Psalm of
David"
Key Themes: Practical atheism ("no God"), universal human
corruption, God's search for the righteous, none found good, God
as refuge for the poor, longing for Zion's salvation

Historical Context: Psalm 14 is nearly identical to Psalm 53 (an
Elohistic variant in Book II). Paul quotes vv.1-3 extensively in
Romans 3:10-12 to establish universal human sinfulness — "there is
none righteous, no, not one." The "fool" (nabal) is not
intellectually deficient but morally corrupt — the same word used
for Nabal in 1 Samuel 25. This psalm presents the divine
perspective: God looks down from heaven searching for anyone who
understands or seeks Him — and finds no one. The picture is
devastating: universal departure from God, universal corruption,
universal moral failure. Only divine salvation (v.7) can remedy
human depravity.

Structure:
- The Fool's Declaration and Its Consequences (v.1)
- God's Search — None Found Righteous (vv.2-3)
- Indictment of Oppressors (vv.4-6)
- Longing for God's Salvation (v.7)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.1              | Psalm       | 53      | 1                  | Nearly identical psalm (Elohistic version)
v.1              | 1 Samuel    | 25      | 25                 | Nabal: "for as his name is, so is he; Nabal is his name, and folly is with him." The moral "fool."
v.1-3            | Romans      | 3       | 10-12              | Paul quotes this passage directly: "There is none righteous, no, not one."
v.2              | Genesis     | 11      | 5                  | "The LORD came down to see." Same divine investigation imagery
v.2              | 2 Chronicles | 16      | 9                  | "The eyes of the LORD run to and fro throughout the whole earth." God searching for faithful
v.4              | Micah       | 3       | 3                  | Leaders who "eat the flesh of my people." Same devouring-the-poor imagery
v.6              | Psalm       | 9       | 9                  | "The LORD also will be a refuge for the oppressed." God as refuge for the poor
v.7              | Romans      | 11      | 26                 | "There shall come out of Sion the Deliverer." NT fulfillment of David's longing

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.1              | KJV         | "The fool" / ESV: "The fool" — Hebrew "nabal" = morally senseless, brutish, godless in practice. Not intellectual limitation but chosen moral corruption. A "nabal" rejects divine authority.
v.3              | KJV         | "become filthy" / ESV: "become corrupt" — Hebrew "ne'elachu" = gone sour, become rotten (like spoiled food). The image is of moral putrefaction — humanity has "gone off" like spoiled milk.
v.5              | KJV         | "There were they in great fear" / ESV: "There they are in great terror" — Sudden, unexpected fear. The wicked thought they were safe — then God's presence among the righteous terrifies them. The shift from complacency to terror is abrupt.
v.7              | KJV         | "when the LORD bringeth back the captivity" / ESV: "when the LORD restores the fortunes" — Hebrew "shuvt shevut" can mean "restore captivity" (return from exile) or "restore fortunes" (reverse circumstances). Both point to comprehensive divine restoration.

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. Practical atheism is more dangerous than intellectual atheism (v.1). Most
   "atheism" is not philosophical but functional — living AS IF God does not
   exist. The fool denies God not from evidence but from desire — he wants
   no accountability.

2. Human corruption is universal (vv.2-3). Paul's use of this passage in
   Romans 3 is devastating: "There is none that doeth good, no, not one."
   This is not pessimism — it is diagnosis. Without grace, the human
   condition is uniformly corrupt. This makes salvation entirely of God.

3. The oppression of God's people is personal to God (v.4). Those who "eat
   up my people as they eat bread" — casual, daily consumption of the
   vulnerable — will face sudden terror (v.5) when they realize God is with
   those they oppress.

4. Salvation must come from outside humanity (v.7). Since none is righteous,
   salvation cannot arise from human effort. It must "come out of Zion" —
   from God Himself. This is the gospel in embryonic form: divine rescue for
   a humanity incapable of self- rescue.

5. The psalm begins with human depravity and ends with divine hope
   (v.1→v.7). The trajectory is crucial: honest diagnosis THEN hopeful
   longing. You cannot appreciate salvation until you accept the diagnosis.
   But the diagnosis is never the last word.

------------------------------------

David Guzik — Psalms 14 Written Commentary:
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/psalms-14/

BibleProject Podcast:
  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

15.KJV:
1. LORD, who shall abide in thy tabernacle? who shall dwell in thy holy hill?
2. He that walketh uprightly, and worketh righteousness, and speaketh the truth in his heart.
3. He that backbiteth not with his tongue, nor doeth evil to his neighbour, nor taketh up a reproach against his neighbour.
4. In whose eyes a vile person is contemned; but he honoureth them that fear the LORD. He that sweareth to his own hurt, and changeth not.
5. He that putteth not out his money to usury, nor taketh reward against the innocent. He that doeth these things shall never be moved.
15.NET:
1. A psalm of David. Lord, who may be a guest in your home? Who may live on your holy hill?
2. Whoever lives a blameless life, does what is right, and speaks honestly.
3. He does not slander, or do harm to others, or insult his neighbor.
4. He despises a reprobate, but honors the Lord’s loyal followers. He makes firm commitments and does not renege on his promise.
5. He does not charge interest when he lends his money. He does not take bribes to testify against the innocent. The one who lives like this will never be shaken.
15.notes:
Psalms - Chapter 15
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
An entrance liturgy — 'LORD, who shall abide in thy tabernacle?' The answer describes the person of integrity: truthful speech, no slander, keeps oaths even when it hurts, and does not take bribes.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Multiple authors — primarily David (73 psalms attributed),
plus Asaph (12), Sons of Korah (11), Solomon (2), Moses (1),
Heman (1), Ethan (1), and anonymous. The Psalter was compiled over
approximately 1000 years and served as Israel's hymnal and prayer
book. The book is divided into five 'books' (1-41, 42-72, 73-89,
90-106, 107-150), paralleling the five books of Moses. Key themes:
worship, lament, praise, trust, kingship, creation, wisdom,
Messianic prophecy, and the full range of human emotion brought
before God.

Classification: Torah/Entrance Liturgy Psalm
Attributed Author: David — "A Psalm of David"
Key Themes: Requirements for dwelling with God, ethical character,
truthful speech, integrity in relationships, financial justice,
keeping vows

Historical Context: Psalm 15 is an "entrance liturgy" — likely
used at the gates of the tabernacle/temple when worshippers asked
who may enter God's presence. The question (v.1) receives a
ten-point ethical answer (vv.2-5) rather than a ritual one.
Notably, NONE of the qualifications are ceremonial — they are all
moral and relational. This is remarkable: access to God is
determined by character, not ritual compliance. This may have been
composed when David brought the Ark to Jerusalem (2 Sam 6) and
established tabernacle worship there. Compare Isaiah 33:14-16 and
Psalm 24:3-5 for parallel entrance liturgies.

Structure:
- The Question: Who May Dwell With God? (v.1)
- The Answer: Ten Ethical Qualifications (vv.2-5a)
- The Promise: Unshakable Security (v.5b)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.1              | Psalm       | 24      | 3-4                | "Who shall ascend into the hill of the LORD?" Same entrance-liturgy question with parallel answer
v.1              | Isaiah      | 33      | 14-16              | "Who among us shall dwell with the devouring fire?" Same question in prophetic context
v.2              | Micah       | 6       | 8                  | "What doth the LORD require of thee? To do justly, love mercy, walk humbly." Same ethical summary
v.3              | James       | 3       | 5-8                | "The tongue is a fire." Speech control as character evidence
v.4              | Matthew     | 5       | 37                 | "Let your yes be yes and your no be no." Jesus' ethic of keeping vows
v.4              | Joshua      | 9       | 18-19              | Israel kept its oath to the Gibeonites despite being deceived. Swearing to one's hurt and not changing
v.5              | Exodus      | 22      | 25                 | "If thou lend money to any of my people that is poor... thou shalt not be to him as an usurer." OT prohibition on exploitative lending

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.1              | KJV         | "abide in thy tabernacle" / ESV: "sojourn in your tent" — Hebrew "gur" = sojourn/dwell as a guest. "Ohel" = tent. The imagery is of being a guest in God's own dwelling. Who is welcome in God's house?
v.3              | KJV         | "backbiteth not" / ESV: "does not slander" — Hebrew "ragal" literally = to go about as a spy/foot-traveler, hence "going about gossiping." Both translations capture the sense of spreading harmful speech about others.
v.4              | KJV         | "a vile person is contemned" / ESV: "a vile person is despised" — The righteous person makes moral distinctions. This is not social snobbery but moral discernment — refusing to honor what God rejects while honoring what God values (those who fear the LORD).
v.5              | KJV         | "putteth not out his money to usury" / ESV: "does not put out his money at interest" — Hebrew "neshek" = interest/ usury. In the OT context, lending at interest to fellow Israelites (especially the poor) was prohibited. This is about exploitation of the vulnerable, not all financial transactions.

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. Access to God is ethical, not merely ceremonial (v.1). God does not ask
   "Did you perform the right ritual?" but "What is your character?" Worship
   without integrity is empty. Character IS the qualification for God's
   presence.

2. Truth must be internal before it is external (v.2). "Speaketh the truth
   IN HIS HEART" — not just truth on the lips but truth in the mind.
   Self-deception disqualifies as much as lying to others. Are you honest
   with yourself?

3. Keep your word even when it costs (v.4). This is perhaps the most
   practical and painful requirement. A promise made in optimism must be
   honored in adversity. Your word is your bond even when "the deal" turns
   against you.

4. Financial ethics matter to God (v.5). How you use money reveals
   character. Exploiting the vulnerable through lending or taking bribes
   against the innocent — these are worship disqualifiers. Financial
   integrity is spiritual integrity.

5. Character produces stability (v.5b). "Shall never be moved" is the reward
   of ethical living. The person of integrity has a foundation that
   circumstances cannot shake. Compromise creates instability; integrity
   creates permanence.

RESOURCES
------------------------------------
The Bible Project — Psalms Overview:
  https://bibleproject.com/explore/video/psalms/

David Guzik — Psalms 15 Written Commentary:
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/psalms-15/

BibleProject Podcast:
  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

1. Preserve me, O God: for in thee do I put my trust.

16.notes:
Psalms - Chapter 16
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
A psalm of trust and Messianic prophecy — David declares God as his portion, the boundary lines have fallen in pleasant places; 'thou wilt not leave my soul in hell' — quoted by Peter at Pentecost as fulfilled in Christ's resurrection.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Multiple authors — primarily David (73 psalms attributed),
plus Asaph (12), Sons of Korah (11), Solomon (2), Moses (1),
Heman (1), Ethan (1), and anonymous. The Psalter was compiled over
approximately 1000 years and served as Israel's hymnal and prayer
book. The book is divided into five 'books' (1-41, 42-72, 73-89,
90-106, 107-150), paralleling the five books of Moses. Key themes:
worship, lament, praise, trust, kingship, creation, wisdom,
Messianic prophecy, and the full range of human emotion brought
before God.

Classification: Trust Psalm / Messianic Psalm
Attributed Author: David — "Michtam of David"
Key Themes: God as refuge and portion, satisfaction in God alone,
rejection of idolatry, counsel and guidance, resurrection hope,
fullness of joy in God's presence

Historical Context: "Michtam" is an uncertain term — possibly
"golden psalm" or "inscription/engraved poem." This is one of the
most significant Messianic psalms — Peter quotes vv.8-11 in his
Pentecost sermon (Acts 2:25-28) to prove Christ's resurrection,
and Paul cites it in Acts 13:35. Peter's argument: David died and
his body DID see corruption; therefore David spoke prophetically
about another — the Messiah — whose body would NOT remain in the
grave. The psalm is profoundly personal — David expressing
complete satisfaction in God as his inheritance, portion, and
source of joy. It contains no complaint, no enemies, no crisis —
only deep contentment in God.

Structure:
- Prayer for Preservation and Declaration of Trust (vv.1-2)
- Delight in the Saints, Rejection of Idols (vv.3-4)
- God as Inheritance and Portion (vv.5-6)
- God as Counselor and Presence (vv.7-8)
- Resurrection Hope and Eternal Joy (vv.9-11)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.5              | Numbers     | 18      | 20                 | "I am thy part and thine inheritance." God as Levitical inheritance — David claims the same privilege
v.5              | Lamentations | 3       | 24                 | "The LORD is my portion, saith my soul; therefore will I hope in him." Same inheritance theology
v.8              | Acts        | 2       | 25-28              | Peter quotes vv.8-11 to prove Christ's resurrection from the dead
v.10             | Acts        | 2       | 29-31              | Peter's argument: David died and decayed; he spoke of Christ who did not
v.10             | Acts        | 13      | 35-37              | Paul uses the same verse for the same argument in Antioch
v.11             | Psalm       | 17      | 15                 | "I shall be satisfied, when I awake, with thy likeness." Parallel afterlife hope
v.11             | Revelation  | 21      | 3-4                | "God himself shall be with them." The eternal presence that is fullness of joy

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.2              | KJV         | "my goodness extendeth not to thee" / ESV: "I have no good apart from you" — Significantly different. KJV: my good does not reach/benefit God. ESV: I have no good outside of God. The Hebrew is notoriously difficult ("tovati bal aleyka"). ESV's reading makes better theological sense in context.
v.7              | KJV         | "my reins also instruct me" / ESV: "my heart instructs me" — Hebrew "kilyot" = kidneys, considered the seat of emotions/conscience in Hebrew thought. KJV "reins" (kidneys) is literal but archaic; ESV "heart" modernizes the metaphor.
v.9              | KJV         | "my glory rejoiceth" / ESV: "my whole being rejoices" — Hebrew "kevodi" = my glory/honor (possibly "my liver" or "my inner self"). KJV is literal; ESV interprets as the whole person. Some see "glory" as a reference to the soul/spirit as the glorious part of a person.
v.10             | KJV         | "leave my soul in hell" / ESV: "abandon my soul to Sheol" — Hebrew "Sheol" = the realm of the dead (not NT "hell" as place of punishment). ESV preserves the Hebrew concept. The promise: God will not leave His Holy One in the state/place of death. "Corruption" (shachath) = physical decay of the body.
v.11             | KJV         | "pleasures for evermore" / ESV: "pleasures forevermore" — Hebrew "ne'imot netsach" = delights/sweetnesses of perpetuity. The pleasures at God's right hand are not temporary — they are eternal. No fading, no diminishing, no end.

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. God Himself is the inheritance (v.5). Not His gifts, not His blessings,
   not His services — God HIMSELF. When He is your portion, every other loss
   becomes survivable. The question is not "What has God given me?" but "Do
   I have GOD?"

2. Contentment is a choice and a practice (v.6). "The lines have fallen for
   me in pleasant places" — David chose to see his allotment as beautiful.
   Contentment is not passive acceptance but active recognition of goodness
   already present.

3. Keep the LORD always before you (v.8). This is a spiritual discipline —
   "I have SET the LORD." It is deliberate, continuous, and produces
   unshakable stability. When God is your focal point, you cannot be
   disoriented.

4. Physical death is not the end (v.10). The resurrection hope is not merely
   NT theology — it is rooted in the Psalms. David believed (and the Spirit
   confirmed through Peter) that death would not have the final word for
   God's Holy One.

5. Fullness of joy is in PRESENCE, not in presents (v.11). The ultimate
   pleasure is not heaven's streets of gold but heaven's face-to-face
   encounter with God. Joy is relational, not material. It is found "in thy
   presence" — nowhere else provides FULLNESS.

RESOURCES
------------------------------------

  https://bibleproject.com/explore/video/psalms/

David Guzik — Psalms 16 Written Commentary:
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/psalms-16/

BibleProject Podcast:
  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

17.notes:
Psalms - Chapter 17
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
An individual lament — David appeals to God's justice, professing his integrity under testing, and prays for deliverance from the wicked; he will be satisfied when he awakes with God's likeness.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Multiple authors — primarily David (73 psalms attributed),
plus Asaph (12), Sons of Korah (11), Solomon (2), Moses (1),
Heman (1), Ethan (1), and anonymous. The Psalter was compiled over
approximately 1000 years and served as Israel's hymnal and prayer
book. The book is divided into five 'books' (1-41, 42-72, 73-89,
90-106, 107-150), paralleling the five books of Moses. Key themes:
worship, lament, praise, trust, kingship, creation, wisdom,
Messianic prophecy, and the full range of human emotion brought
before God.

Classification: Individual Lament / Prayer for Vindication
Attributed Author: David — "A Prayer of David"
Key Themes: Innocence before God, divine testing and approval,
enemies like lions, apple of God's eye, shadow of God's wings,
satisfaction in God's presence vs. worldly satisfaction

Historical Context: Labeled simply "A Prayer" — one of only five
psalms so designated (17, 86, 90, 102, 142). David protests his
innocence (vv.1-5) in language similar to Psalm 7, appeals for
protection using intimate imagery (apple of the eye, shadow of
wings), and contrasts two ultimate satisfactions: the worldly
person satisfied with "this life" (v.14) vs. David satisfied with
God's face at resurrection (v.15). The enemies are described as
predatory lions (v.12), suggesting a period when David was hunted.
The final verse (v.15) is one of the most remarkable statements of
afterlife hope in the Psalter.

Structure:
- Appeal Based on Innocence (vv.1-5)
- Petition for Protection (vv.6-9)
- Description of Enemies (vv.10-12)
- Prayer for Deliverance (vv.13-14)
- David's Ultimate Satisfaction (v.15)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.8              | Deuteronomy | 32      | 10                 | "He kept him as the apple of his eye." God's care for Israel in the wilderness
v.8              | Ruth        | 2       | 12                 | "Under whose wings thou art come to trust." Same protective wing imagery
v.8              | Matthew     | 23      | 37                 | "As a hen gathereth her chickens under her wings." Jesus uses the same image of protective love
v.14             | Luke        | 12      | 19-20              | "Soul, thou hast much goods... Thou fool." The rich man whose portion was only in this life
v.15             | 1 John      | 3       | 2                  | "We shall be like him; for we shall see him as he is." NT fulfillment: seeing God, becoming like Him
v.15             | Psalm       | 16      | 11                 | "In thy presence is fulness of joy." Parallel hope of God's presence as ultimate satisfaction
v.15             | 2 Corinthians | 3       | 18                 | "Changed into the same image from glory to glory." Transformation into God's likeness

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.1              | KJV         | "Hear the right, O LORD" / ESV: "Hear a just cause, O LORD" — Hebrew "tsedeq" = righteousness/just cause. David presents his case as legally sound — he has a just cause to bring before the divine court.
v.3              | KJV         | "thou hast proved mine heart; thou hast visited me in the night" / ESV: "You have tried my heart, you have visited me by night" — God's testing happens "in the night" — in hidden moments when no one else sees. David passes the nighttime test.
v.10             | KJV         | "They are inclosed in their own fat" / ESV: "They close their hearts to pity" — Hebrew "chelbamo sagru" = "they have closed their fat." KJV reads anatomically (fat closes around them = self-indulgent, insensitive). ESV interprets: they have shut their compassion. The image is a hardened, insensitive heart.
v.15             | KJV         | "when I awake" / ESV: "when I awake" — This could mean awakening from sleep (seeing God in the morning) or awakening from death (resurrection). The contrast with v.14 (whose portion is THIS life) strongly supports the resurrection reading: David's satisfaction comes BEYOND this life.

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. You are the apple of God's eye (v.8). The most instinctively protected
   part of the body is the pupil. God guards you with that same reflexive,
   immediate, total protection. You are not peripheral to Him — you are
   central.

2. Two satisfactions, two destinies (vv.14-15). The worldly person is full
   NOW — children, wealth, inheritance. David's satisfaction comes LATER —
   at awakening, in God's likeness. Which satisfaction are you pursuing? One
   ends at death; the other begins at resurrection.

3. God tests in the night (v.3). Your true character is revealed when no one
   is watching — "in the night seasons." What you do in private, what you
   think when alone, how you behave unsupervised — these are the divine
   tests.

4. "I shall be satisfied with thy likeness" (v.15). The ultimate human
   satisfaction is not comfort, achievement, or pleasure but LIKENESS TO
   GOD. We were made in His image; we will be restored to it fully. That
   restoration is what finally satisfies the human soul.

5. The shadow of His wings (v.8). In danger, run TO God, not FROM danger.
   The chick does not fight the hawk — it runs to its mother's wings. God's
   wings are both refuge and warmth. They protect AND comfort
   simultaneously.

------------------------------------

  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/psalms-17/

  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

18.ESV:
1. I love you, O LORD, my strength.
2. The LORD is my rock and my fortress and my deliverer, my God, my rock, in whom I take refuge, my shield, and the horn of my salvation, my stronghold.
3. I call upon the LORD, who is worthy to be praised, and I am saved from my enemies.
4. The cords of death encompassed me; the torrents of destruction assailed me;
5. the cords of Sheol entangled me; the snares of death confronted me.
6. In my distress I called upon the LORD; to my God I cried for help. From his temple he heard my voice, and my cry to him reached his ears.
7. Then the earth reeled and rocked; the foundations also of the mountains trembled and quaked, because he was angry.
8. Smoke went up from his nostrils, and devouring fire from his mouth; glowing coals flamed forth from him.
9. He bowed the heavens and came down; thick darkness was under his feet.
10. He rode on a cherub and flew; he came swiftly on the wings of the wind.
11. He made darkness his covering, his canopy around him, thick clouds dark with water.
12. Out of the brightness before him hailstones and coals of fire broke through his clouds.
13. The LORD also thundered in the heavens, and the Most High uttered his voice, hailstones and coals of fire.
14. And he sent out his arrows and scattered them; he flashed forth lightnings and routed them.
15. Then the channels of the sea were seen, and the foundations of the world were laid bare at your rebuke, O LORD, at the blast of the breath of your nostrils.
16. He sent from on high, he took me; he drew me out of many waters.
17. He rescued me from my strong enemy and from those who hated me, for they were too mighty for me.
18. They confronted me in the day of my calamity, but the LORD was my support.
19. He brought me out into a broad place; he rescued me, because he delighted in me.
20. The LORD dealt with me according to my righteousness; according to the cleanness of my hands he rewarded me.
21. For I have kept the ways of the LORD, and have not wickedly departed from my God.
22. For all his rules were before me, and his statutes I did not put away from me.
23. I was blameless before him, and I kept myself from my guilt.
24. So the LORD has rewarded me according to my righteousness, according to the cleanness of my hands in his sight.
25. With the merciful you show yourself merciful; with the blameless man you show yourself blameless;
26. with the purified you show yourself pure; and with the crooked you make yourself seem tortuous.
27. For you save a humble people, but the haughty eyes you bring down.
28. For it is you who light my lamp; the LORD my God lightens my darkness.
29. For by you I can run against a troop, and by my God I can leap over a wall.
30. This God—his way is perfect; the word of the LORD proves true; he is a shield for all those who take refuge in him.
31. For who is God, but the LORD? And who is a rock, except our God?—
32. the God who equipped me with strength and made my way blameless.
33. He made my feet like the feet of a deer and set me secure on the heights.
34. He trains my hands for war, so that my arms can bend a bow of bronze.
35. You have given me the shield of your salvation, and your right hand supported me, and your gentleness made me great.
36. You gave a wide place for my steps under me, and my feet did not slip.
37. I pursued my enemies and overtook them, and did not turn back till they were consumed.
38. I thrust them through, so that they were not able to rise; they fell under my feet.
39. For you equipped me with strength for the battle; you made those who rise against me sink under me.
40. You made my enemies turn their backs to me, and those who hated me I destroyed.
41. They cried for help, but there was none to save; they cried to the LORD, but he did not answer them.
42. I beat them fine as dust before the wind; I cast them out like the mire of the streets.

45. Foreigners lost heart and came trembling out of their fortresses.
46. The LORD lives, and blessed be my rock, and exalted be the God of my salvation—

48. who rescued me from my enemies; yes, you exalted me above those who rose against me; you delivered me from the man of violence.
49. For this I will praise you, O LORD, among the nations, and sing to your name.
50. Great salvation he brings to his king, and shows steadfast love to his anointed, to David and his offspring forever.

49. So I will give you thanks before the nations, O Lord. I will sing praises to you.
50. He gives his king magnificent victories; he is faithful to his chosen ruler, to David and his descendants forever.”

18.notes:
Psalms - Chapter 18
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
A royal thanksgiving psalm — David's grand celebration of God's deliverance from all enemies (parallels 2 Samuel 22); God descended in theophany, drew him from many waters, and made him head of nations.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Multiple authors — primarily David (73 psalms attributed),
plus Asaph (12), Sons of Korah (11), Solomon (2), Moses (1),
Heman (1), Ethan (1), and anonymous. The Psalter was compiled over
approximately 1000 years and served as Israel's hymnal and prayer
book. The book is divided into five 'books' (1-41, 42-72, 73-89,
90-106, 107-150), paralleling the five books of Moses. Key themes:
worship, lament, praise, trust, kingship, creation, wisdom,
Messianic prophecy, and the full range of human emotion brought
before God.

Classification: Royal Thanksgiving / Theophany Psalm
Attributed Author: David — "To the chief Musician, A Psalm of
David, the servant of the LORD, who spake the words of this song
in the day that the LORD delivered him from the hand of all his
enemies, and from the hand of Saul"
Key Themes: Comprehensive deliverance, theophany (God appearing
in storm), God as rock/fortress/deliverer, divine warrior,
gentleness that makes great, Messianic kingship

Historical Context: This is the longest psalm in Book I and one of
the most dramatic. It also appears as 2 Samuel 22 (with minor
variations), placed near the end of David's life as a summary of
God's faithfulness throughout. The superscription dates it to after
David's deliverance from Saul and all enemies — a career-spanning
thanksgiving. The theophany section (vv.7-15) is among the most
spectacular divine-warrior passages in Scripture — God bends the
heavens, rides on a cherub, shakes the earth, and floods the sky
with fire to rescue one man. Paul quotes v.49 in Romans 15:9 as
prophecy of Gentile worship.

Structure:
- Declaration of Love and Trust (vv.1-3)
- The Crisis (vv.4-6)
- The Theophany — God Arrives (vv.7-15)
- The Rescue (vv.16-19)
- David's Righteousness Rewarded (vv.20-24)
- God's Character Matches the Worshipper (vv.25-29)
- God Equips for Battle (vv.30-42)
- God Exalts David Among Nations (vv.43-45)
- Final Praise (vv.46-50)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.2              | 2 Samuel    | 22      | 2-3                | Parallel passage with minor variations
v.7-15           | Exodus      | 19      | 16-18              | Sinai theophany parallels: thunder, smoke, earthquake, fire
v.16             | Exodus      | 2       | 10                 | Moses "drawn out of water." Same rescue imagery
v.19             | Psalm       | 31      | 8                  | "Thou hast set my feet in a large room." Same broad-place deliverance
v.25             | Matthew     | 5       | 7                  | "Blessed are the merciful: for they shall obtain mercy." Same reciprocal principle
v.30             | Proverbs    | 30      | 5                  | "Every word of God is pure." Same tested- word theology
v.35             | 2 Corinthians | 10      | 1                  | "The meekness and gentleness of Christ." God's gentleness embodied
v.49             | Romans      | 15      | 9                  | Paul quotes this verse: "I will confess to thee among the Gentiles." Prophecy of Gentile praise

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.1              | KJV         | "I will love thee" / ESV: "I love you" — Hebrew "racham" = deep love, compassion (from "rechem" = womb). This is tender, visceral love — rare as a human-to-God word. David loves God with gut-level affection.
v.4              | KJV         | "sorrows of death" / ESV: "cords of death" — Hebrew "chevley" can mean "cords/ropes" or "pangs/birth pains." ESV reads as binding cords; KJV as anguish/pangs. The imagery works either way: death was either binding David or causing unbearable pain.
v.26             | KJV         | "with the froward thou wilt shew thyself froward" / ESV: "with the crooked you make yourself seem tortuous" — Hebrew "iqqesh" = twisted, crooked. The principle: God mirrors back what He receives. To the merciful, He shows mercy; to the twisted, He appears twisted (incomprehensible, opposing).
v.35             | KJV         | "thy gentleness hath made me great" / ESV: "your gentleness made me great" — Hebrew "anvat'ka" = your condescension, humility, gentleness. Some translate "your help" or "your answering." The idea: God stooped down to David's level, and that condescension elevated David.

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. God delights to deliver (v.19). He does not rescue reluctantly. "He
   delivered me because he DELIGHTED in me." You are not a burden to God —
   you are His joy. His rescue flows from pleasure, not obligation.

2. God's gentleness makes greatness (v.35). The world assumes power makes
   great. David testifies that GENTLENESS made him great. God's tender,
   condescending care — stooping to a shepherd boy — produced a king.
   Receive His gentleness; extend it to others.

3. No single metaphor captures God (v.2). Rock, fortress, deliverer,
   strength, shield, horn, tower — seven images in one verse. Whatever you
   need God to be, He already is. Your situation names the aspect of God you
   need most.

4. God matches your posture (vv.25-26). With the merciful He is merciful;
   with the pure, pure; with the twisted, impenetrable. This is not divine
   inconsistency but divine appropriateness. How you approach God shapes
   what you experience of God.

5. The theophany reminds us: God MOVES (vv.7-15). He is not static in
   heaven. He bends the heavens, rides the wind, shakes the earth — all to
   reach ONE person in distress. The universe is not too large for God to
   intervene personally in your life.

RESOURCES
------------------------------------
The Bible Project — Psalms Overview:
  https://bibleproject.com/explore/video/psalms/

David Guzik — Psalms 18 Written Commentary:
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/psalms-18/

BibleProject Podcast:
  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

========================================
Study Notes generated for personal Bible study use.
Translations referenced: KJV, ESV, ASV, NET, WEB
Commentary sources: Enduring Word (enduringword.com), Charles
Spurgeon (Public Domain)

19.ESV:
1. The heavens declare the glory of God, and the sky above proclaims his handiwork.
2. Day to day pours out speech, and night to night reveals knowledge.
3. There is no speech, nor are there words, whose voice is not heard.
4. Their voice goes out through all the earth, and their words to the end of the world. In them he has set a tent for the sun,
5. which comes out like a bridegroom leaving his chamber, and, like a strong man, runs its course with joy.
6. Its rising is from the end of the heavens, and its circuit to the end of them, and there is nothing hidden from its heat.
7. The law of the LORD is perfect, reviving the soul; the testimony of the LORD is sure, making wise the simple;
8. the precepts of the LORD are right, rejoicing the heart; the commandment of the LORD is pure, enlightening the eyes;
9. the fear of the LORD is clean, enduring forever; the rules of the LORD are true, and righteous altogether.
10. More to be desired are they than gold, even much fine gold; sweeter also than honey and drippings of the honeycomb.
11. Moreover, by them is your servant warned; in keeping them there is great reward.
12. Who can discern his errors? Declare me innocent from hidden faults.
13. Keep back your servant also from presumptuous sins; let them not have dominion over me! Then I shall be blameless, and innocent of great transgression.
14. Let the words of my mouth and the meditation of my heart be acceptable in your sight, O LORD, my rock and my redeemer.
19.KJV:
1. The heavens declare the glory of God; and the firmament sheweth his handywork.
2. Day unto day uttereth speech, and night unto night sheweth knowledge.
3. There is no speech nor language, where their voice is not heard.
4. Their line is gone out through all the earth, and their words to the end of the world. In them hath he set a tabernacle for the sun,
5. Which is as a bridegroom coming out of his chamber, and rejoiceth as a strong man to run a race.
6. His going forth is from the end of the heaven, and his circuit unto the ends of it: and there is nothing hid from the heat thereof.
7. The law of the LORD is perfect, converting the soul: the testimony of the LORD is sure, making wise the simple.
8. The statutes of the LORD are right, rejoicing the heart: the commandment of the LORD is pure, enlightening the eyes.
9. The fear of the LORD is clean, enduring for ever: the judgments of the LORD are true and righteous altogether.
10. More to be desired are they than gold, yea, than much fine gold: sweeter also than honey and the honeycomb.

19.notes:
Psalms - Chapter 19
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
A creation and Torah psalm — 'The heavens declare the glory of God' (general revelation), then the law of the LORD is perfect, restoring the soul (special revelation); David ends asking to be cleansed from hidden faults.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Multiple authors — primarily David (73 psalms attributed),
plus Asaph (12), Sons of Korah (11), Solomon (2), Moses (1),
Heman (1), Ethan (1), and anonymous. The Psalter was compiled over
approximately 1000 years and served as Israel's hymnal and prayer
book. The book is divided into five 'books' (1-41, 42-72, 73-89,
90-106, 107-150), paralleling the five books of Moses. Key themes:
worship, lament, praise, trust, kingship, creation, wisdom,
Messianic prophecy, and the full range of human emotion brought
before God.

Classification: Hymn of Praise / Torah Psalm
Attributed Author: David — "To the chief Musician, A Psalm of
David"
Key Themes: Two books of revelation (creation and Scripture), the
heavens' wordless testimony, the perfection and value of God's
law, hidden faults, presumptuous sins, acceptable worship

Historical Context: C.S. Lewis called this "the greatest poem in
the Psalter and one of the greatest lyrics in the world." The
psalm moves from general revelation (creation, vv.1-6) to special
revelation (Torah, vv.7-11) to personal response (prayer, vv.12-
14). The two halves use different names for God: "El" (God the
Creator) in vv.1-6 and "Yahweh" (LORD, the covenant God) in vv.7-
14 — reflecting the two modes of revelation. Paul quotes v.4 in
Romans 10:18 to argue that God's message has reached all peoples.
The six descriptions of God's Word in vv.7-9 (law, testimony,
statutes, commandment, fear, judgments) with six qualities and six
effects form one of the most structured and beautiful passages in
Scripture.

Structure:
- God's Glory in Creation (vv.1-6)
- God's Word — Its Character and Value (vv.7-11)
- Personal Response — Prayer for Purity (vv.12-14)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.1              | Romans      | 1       | 19-20              | "The invisible things of him from the creation of the world are clearly seen." Paul's theology of general revelation
v.4              | Romans      | 10      | 18                 | Paul quotes this verse: "Their sound went into all the earth." Applied to the gospel's spread
v.7              | Psalm       | 119     | 1-176              | The fullest expansion of Torah-love theology
v.7              | Psalm       | 12      | 6                  | "The words of the LORD are pure words." Same purity affirmation
v.10             | Psalm       | 119     | 72                 | "The law of thy mouth is better unto me than thousands of gold and silver." Same gold comparison
v.12             | 1 Corinthians | 4       | 4                  | "I know nothing by myself; yet am I not hereby justified." Limited self-knowledge
v.13             | Numbers     | 15      | 30                 | "The soul that doeth ought presumptuously." Deliberate vs. inadvertent sin
v.14             | Philippians | 4       | 8                  | "Whatsoever things are true... think on these things." Heart meditation as spiritual discipline

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.3              | KJV         | "There is no speech nor language, where their voice is not heard" / ESV: "There is no speech, nor are there words, whose voice is not heard" — The Hebrew is ambiguous: either "their voice is not heard" (they speak without audible words) or "there is no place where their voice is not heard" (universal reach). Both are true — creation's testimony is wordless AND universal.
v.7              | KJV         | "converting the soul" / ESV: "reviving the soul" — Hebrew "meshivat nephesh" = turning back/restoring the soul. ESV's "reviving" captures restoration; KJV's "converting" suggests transformation. Both are valid — God's Word both restores and transforms.
v.9              | KJV         | "the judgments of the LORD are true and righteous altogether" / ESV: "the rules of the LORD are true, and righteous altogether" — Hebrew "mishpatim" = judgments, ordinances, decisions, rules. "Altogether" = all of them collectively, without exception.
v.14             | KJV         | "my strength" / ESV: "my rock" — Hebrew "tsuri" = my rock. ESV is literal; KJV interprets the metaphor. The psalm ends with "my rock and my redeemer" — connecting back to both creation (God as foundation/rock) and redemption (God as redeemer/kinsman).

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. Creation speaks to everyone (vv.1-4). You do not need a Bible to know God
   exists — the heavens preach a wordless sermon heard universally. General
   revelation leaves all without excuse. But it points toward the fuller
   revelation in Scripture.

2. God's Word does specific things (vv.7-9). It revives, makes wise,
   rejoices the heart, enlightens, endures, and proves true. Each
   description is both a promise and an invitation. If your soul needs
   reviving, the law of the LORD is the instrument.

3. Scripture is more valuable than gold and sweeter than honey (v.10). This
   is not hyperbole — it is experienced reality for those who truly engage
   with it. If God's Word feels less valuable than gold or less sweet than
   honey, the problem is not the Word but the palate.

4. You have sins you cannot see (v.12). "Secret faults" — hidden even from
   yourself. Self-knowledge is always partial. Therefore: ask God to search
   you (Ps 139:23-24) and cleanse what you cannot identify. Humility
   acknowledges unknown sin.

5. Guard against presumptuous sins (v.13). There are two kinds of sin:
   inadvertent (hidden) and deliberate (presumptuous). The first needs
   cleansing; the second needs prevention. Ask God to KEEP you from willful
   rebellion before it gains dominion.

RESOURCES
------------------------------------
The Bible Project — Psalms Overview:
  https://bibleproject.com/explore/video/psalms/

David Guzik — Psalms 19 Written Commentary:
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/psalms-19/

BibleProject Podcast:
  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

20.notes:
Psalms - Chapter 20
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
A royal psalm — a pre-battle prayer for the king: may the LORD answer you in the day of trouble, grant your heart's desire, and give victory; 'some trust in chariots, and some in horses: but we will remember the name of the LORD.'

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Multiple authors — primarily David (73 psalms attributed),
plus Asaph (12), Sons of Korah (11), Solomon (2), Moses (1),
Heman (1), Ethan (1), and anonymous. The Psalter was compiled over
approximately 1000 years and served as Israel's hymnal and prayer
book. The book is divided into five 'books' (1-41, 42-72, 73-89,
90-106, 107-150), paralleling the five books of Moses. Key themes:
worship, lament, praise, trust, kingship, creation, wisdom,
Messianic prophecy, and the full range of human emotion brought
before God.

Classification: Royal/Community Prayer Psalm
Attributed Author: David — "To the chief Musician, A Psalm of
David"
Key Themes: Community praying for the king, trust in God's name
vs. military power, pre-battle liturgy, confidence in God's
anointed, salvation through divine intervention

Historical Context: Psalm 20 is a pre-battle liturgy — the people
pray for their king before he goes to war. It pairs with Psalm 21
(post-battle thanksgiving). The congregation addresses the king
(vv.1-5), then a single voice (prophet or king) declares
confidence (v.6), and the people affirm collective trust (vv.7-8).
The psalm's theology is explicitly anti-militaristic: "Some trust
in chariots, and some in horses: but we will remember the name of
the LORD" (v.7). Military success comes not from superior
equipment but from divine favor. This is the theology that sent
David against Goliath with stones instead of armor.

Structure:
- Congregational Prayer for the King (vv.1-5)
- Declaration of Confidence (v.6)
- Trust in God's Name vs. Military Power (vv.7-8)
- Final Prayer (v.9)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.1              | Psalm       | 46      | 1                  | "God is our refuge and strength, a very present help in trouble." Same "day of trouble" refuge
v.2              | 2 Chronicles | 20      | 17                 | "Stand ye still, and see the salvation of the LORD." Help from the sanctuary
v.7              | Isaiah      | 31      | 1                  | "Woe to them that go down to Egypt for help; and stay on horses." Same anti-militaristic theology
v.7              | 1 Samuel    | 17      | 45                 | "I come to thee in the name of the LORD of hosts." David vs. Goliath — the Name vs. weapons
v.7              | Psalm       | 33      | 16-17              | "There is no king saved by the multitude of an host: a horse is a vain thing for safety." Same principle
v.9              | Psalm       | 118     | 25                 | "Save now, I beseech thee, O LORD." Similar cry for divine salvation

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.1              | KJV         | "The LORD hear thee" / ESV: "May the LORD answer you" — Both are jussive (wishes/prayers). ESV adds "may" to clarify the prayerful/wishful mood. The people are praying FOR their king, not describing what has happened.
v.3              | KJV         | "accept thy burnt sacrifice" / ESV: "regard with favor your burnt sacrifices" — Hebrew "yedash'neha" = regard as fat/rich, accept favorably. Pre-battle sacrifice was standard practice; the prayer asks God to accept it.
v.7              | KJV         | "we will remember the name" / ESV: "we trust in the name" — Hebrew "nazkir" = we will mention, invoke, remember, bring to mind. It implies both intellectual remembering and verbal invocation — calling on God's name as a battle cry.
v.9              | KJV         | "Save, LORD: let the king hear us when we call" / ESV: "O LORD, save the king! May he answer us when we call" — Punctuation creates different readings. KJV: Save us, LORD; let the king be responsive. ESV: Save the king; may God answer us. The Hebrew can be divided either way; ESV's reading parallels the psalm's structure better.

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. Pray for your leaders before the battle (vv.1-5). The congregation's role
   is intercession. Before the king faces danger, the people pray. Apply
   this to spiritual leadership, community leaders, and anyone facing a
   battle — pray before, not only during.

2. "The name of the LORD our God" is sufficient (v.7). Chariots =
   technology, resources, systems. Horses = speed, power, advantage. These
   are not wrong to have — but wrong to TRUST. The question is not "Do you
   have resources?" but "Where is your trust placed?"

3. The outcome proves the trust (v.8). Those who trust human power
   eventually collapse; those who trust God's name eventually stand. The
   timeline may vary, but the principle holds. Place your trust where the
   outcome confirms it.

4. Communal faith strengthens individual faith. This is a COLLECTIVE psalm —
   "we will remember," "we are risen." Faith is not only personal — it is
   corporate. Surround yourself with people who trust God's name rather than
   human chariots.

5. Credit God publicly for victories (v.5). "In the name of our God we will
   set up our banners" — when victory comes, fly God's flag. Public
   attribution prevents the pride that claims "my chariot delivered me."

RESOURCES
------------------------------------
The Bible Project — Psalms Overview:
  https://bibleproject.com/explore/video/psalms/

David Guzik — Psalms 20 Written Commentary:
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/psalms-20/

BibleProject Podcast:
  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

Translations referenced: KJV, ESV, ASV, NET, WEB
Commentary sources: Enduring Word (enduringword.com), Charles
Spurgeon (Public Domain)

21.notes:
Psalms - Chapter 21
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
A royal psalm — thanksgiving for the king's victory and God's blessings upon him; the king trusted in the LORD, and through the Most High's steadfast love he shall not be moved.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Multiple authors — primarily David (73 psalms attributed),
plus Asaph (12), Sons of Korah (11), Solomon (2), Moses (1),
Heman (1), Ethan (1), and anonymous. The Psalter was compiled over
approximately 1000 years and served as Israel's hymnal and prayer
book. The book is divided into five 'books' (1-41, 42-72, 73-89,
90-106, 107-150), paralleling the five books of Moses. Key themes:
worship, lament, praise, trust, kingship, creation, wisdom,
Messianic prophecy, and the full range of human emotion brought
before God.

Classification: Royal Thanksgiving Psalm
Attributed Author: David — "To the chief Musician, A Psalm of
David"
Key Themes: King's joy in divine strength, answered prayer,
blessings received, trust in God's steadfast love, judgment on
enemies, divine exaltation

Historical Context: Psalm 21 is the post-battle companion to
Psalm 20 (pre-battle prayer). What the people prayed for in Psalm
20, they celebrate in Psalm 21. The king has been victorious, and
the congregation acknowledges that the victory came from God, not
military might. The language is both Davidic and Messianic — "life"
and "length of days forever and ever" (v.4) exceeds what any
earthly king receives and points to the eternal Messianic King.
The structure mirrors Psalm 20: the people speak about the king
(vv.1-7), address God about judgment on enemies (vv.8-12), and
close with praise (v.13).

Structure:
- The King's Blessings Celebrated (vv.1-7)
- God's Judgment on Enemies (vv.8-12)
- Closing Praise (v.13)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.1              | Psalm       | 20      | 5                  | "We will rejoice in thy salvation." Psalm 20 anticipated; Psalm 21 fulfills
v.3              | Psalm       | 20      | 4                  | "Grant thee according to thine own heart." Prayer answered in Psalm 21:2
v.4              | 2 Samuel    | 7       | 16                 | "Thy throne shall be established for ever." Davidic covenant — eternal kingdom
v.6              | Psalm       | 16      | 11                 | "In thy presence is fulness of joy." Same countenance-joy theology
v.7              | Psalm       | 16      | 8                  | "I shall not be moved." Same stability from God's presence
v.9              | Malachi     | 4       | 1                  | "The day cometh, that shall burn as an oven." Same fiery oven judgment imagery
v.13             | Psalm       | 57      | 5                  | "Be thou exalted, O God, above the heavens." Same closing exaltation

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.3              | KJV         | "thou preventest him with the blessings of goodness" / ESV: "you meet him with rich blessings" — KJV "prevent" is archaic English meaning "go before/precede" (Latin praevenio). ESV clarifies: God MEETS the king — His blessings arrive before the king even asks.
v.4              | KJV         | ESV both: "length of days forever and ever" — This exceeds any earthly king's lifespan. Messianic interpreters see this pointing to Christ's eternal reign. Davidic interpreters see hyperbolic court language or dynastic perpetuity.
v.9              | KJV         | "a fiery oven" / ESV: "a blazing oven" — The image of judgment as a furnace. Those who opposed God's king are consumed like fuel in an oven. Intense, thorough, complete destruction.

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. Answered prayer deserves public celebration (vv.1-5). What you prayed for
   in private (Psalm 20), celebrate publicly when granted (Psalm 21). Do not
   forget to return thanks for prayers answered.

2. God's blessings often precede our asking (v.3). He "meets" us with
   blessings — arrives ahead of us. Grace is not always reactive to our
   requests; it is often proactive, preparing goodness before we know to
   ask.

3. The king's joy is in God's strength, not his own (v.1). True leaders
   rejoice not in their own power but in God's. If your joy is in your own
   strength, every loss diminishes you. If your joy is in GOD'S strength,
   you are never diminished.

4. Trust + steadfast love = stability (v.7). The equation for being unmoved:
   the king trusts; God is faithful. Your trust meeting God's chesed
   produces permanence nothing can shake.

5. All glory returns to God (v.13). The psalm's final word is worship — not
   of the victorious king but of the God who gave the victory. Every human
   triumph finds its proper ending in divine praise.
