22.notes:
Psalms - Chapter 22
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
A Messianic lament — 'My God, my God, why hast thou forsaken me?' describes suffering in extraordinary detail (pierced hands and feet, casting lots for garments), yet turns to worldwide praise as all nations worship the LORD.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Multiple authors — primarily David (73 psalms attributed),
plus Asaph (12), Sons of Korah (11), Solomon (2), Moses (1),
Heman (1), Ethan (1), and anonymous. The Psalter was compiled over
approximately 1000 years and served as Israel's hymnal and prayer
book. The book is divided into five 'books' (1-41, 42-72, 73-89,
90-106, 107-150), paralleling the five books of Moses. Key themes:
worship, lament, praise, trust, kingship, creation, wisdom,
Messianic prophecy, and the full range of human emotion brought
before God.

Classification: Individual Lament / Messianic Psalm (supreme)
Attributed Author: David — "To the chief Musician upon Aijeleth
Shahar, A Psalm of David" ("Aijeleth Shahar" = "The Doe of the
Dawn")
Key Themes: Divine abandonment, extreme suffering, mockery, physical
torment describing crucifixion, pierced hands and feet, garments
divided, the pivot from lament to worldwide praise, universal
kingdom

Historical Context: Psalm 22 is the supreme Messianic psalm —
quoted by Jesus on the cross ("My God, my God, why hast thou
forsaken me?" — Matt 27:46) and fulfilled in detail by His
crucifixion. Written approximately 1000 years before Christ and
centuries before crucifixion was invented as an execution method,
it describes: pierced hands and feet (v.16), bones out of joint
(v.14), garments divided and lots cast (v.18), surrounded by
mockers using the exact words of Matthew 27:43 (v.8), thirst
(v.15), and visible bones (v.17). The psalm's structure moves
from deepest desolation (vv.1-21) to highest praise (vv.22-31),
paralleling Christ's journey from cross to resurrection. The final
phrase "he hath done this" (v.31) parallels Jesus' "It is finished"
(John 19:30).

Structure:
- The Cry of Forsakenness (vv.1-2)
- God's Past Faithfulness (vv.3-5)
- The Sufferer's Degradation (vv.6-8)
- Trust From Birth (vv.9-11)
- Enemies Surround (vv.12-13)
- Physical Suffering Described (vv.14-18)
- Final Plea for Deliverance (vv.19-21)
- The Pivot — Praise Declared (vv.22-26)
- Universal Worship Proclaimed (vv.27-31)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.1              | Matthew     | 27      | 46                 | Jesus quotes this verse from the cross
v.7-8            | Matthew     | 27      | 39-43              | Mockers at the cross shake heads and say "He trusted in God; let him deliver him." Nearly word-for-word fulfillment
v.14             | John        | 19      | 34                 | "Blood and water" from Jesus' side — consistent with pericardial effusion ("heart like wax melted")
v.15             | John        | 19      | 28                 | "I thirst." Fulfilled from this psalm
v.16             | John        | 20      | 25-27              | Thomas sees the nail prints in Jesus' hands. Physical evidence of piercing
v.18             | John        | 19      | 23-24              | John explicitly quotes Psalm 22:18 as fulfilled at the cross
v.22             | Hebrews     | 2       | 12                 | Quoted as Christ's words to His brothers (the church) after resurrection
v.27             | Revelation  | 7       | 9                  | "A great multitude, which no man could number, of all nations." Universal worship fulfilled
v.31             | John        | 19      | 30                 | "It is finished." The parallel declaration of accomplished work

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.1              | KJV         | "from the words of my roaring" / ESV: "from the words of my groaning" — Hebrew "she'agati" = my roaring/crying out. Not quiet prayer but visceral, loud anguish. On the cross, Jesus CRIED OUT with a loud voice (Mark 15:34).
v.16             | KJV         | "they pierced my hands and my feet" / ESV: "they have pierced my hands and feet" — The Hebrew textual issue: Masoretic text reads "ka'ari" (like a lion — my hands and feet), which is grammatically difficult. The LXX, Dead Sea Scrolls fragment, and Syriac versions read "pierced." Most scholars accept "pierced" as the original reading.
v.21             | KJV         | "thou hast heard me from the horns of the unicorns" / ESV: "You have rescued me from the horns of the wild oxen!" — Hebrew "re'emim" = wild oxen (now extinct aurochs), not unicorns. ESV corrects KJV's rendering. More importantly, this is the PIVOT — "thou hast HEARD me" — deliverance has come. Everything after this is praise.
v.31             | KJV         | "he hath done this" / ESV: "he has done it" — The Hebrew is simply "asah" (he has done [it]). The parallel to "tetelestai" (It is finished) in John 19:30 is remarkable. Both declarations mark the completion of God's redemptive work through suffering.

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. Jesus entered the depths of forsakenness for you (v.1). The sinless Son
   experienced what no human has — full divine abandonment — so that you
   never would. His "Why?" was answered on Easter morning. If you feel
   forsaken, know that Christ endured this so that you would never be truly
   abandoned.

2. Past faithfulness anchors present suffering (vv.3-5). Even in agony, the
   sufferer recalls: "Our fathers trusted... thou didst deliver them." When
   you cannot feel God's presence, remember His track record. He has been
   faithful before; He will be again.

3. Suffering does not negate God's holiness (v.3). "BUT thou art holy." In
   the middle of feeling forsaken, the sufferer acknowledges: God is still
   holy. Suffering does not mean God has failed or is unjust. Hold both
   truths: I feel abandoned AND God is holy.

4. The psalm MOVES from suffering to worship (vv.21→22). The pivot is the
   key. Suffering is real — 21 verses of it. But it is not final. Praise
   erupts from the very one who suffered most. The cross leads to the empty
   tomb. Friday leads to Sunday.

5. "He has done it" (v.31). The final word is accomplishment, not suffering.
   What God intended through the Messiah's agony is COMPLETE. Salvation is
   not in progress — it is done. Rest in the finished work.

------------------------------------
The Bible Project — Psalms Overview:
  https://bibleproject.com/explore/video/psalms/

David Guzik — Psalms 22 Written Commentary:
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/psalms-22/

BibleProject Podcast:
  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

23.ESV:
1. The LORD is my shepherd; I shall not want.
2. He makes me lie down in green pastures. He leads me beside still waters.
3. He restores my soul. He leads me in paths of righteousness for his name’s sake.
4. Even though I walk through the valley of the shadow of death, I will fear no evil, for you are with me; your rod and your staff, they comfort me.
5. You prepare a table before me in the presence of my enemies; you anoint my head with oil; my cup overflows.
6. Surely goodness and mercy shall follow me all the days of my life, and I shall dwell in the house of the LORD forever.

23.notes:
Psalms - Chapter 23
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
A psalm of trust — the most beloved psalm; 'The LORD is my shepherd, I shall not want.' Through green pastures, still waters, the valley of the shadow of death, and the prepared table — goodness and mercy follow all life's days.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Multiple authors — primarily David (73 psalms attributed),
plus Asaph (12), Sons of Korah (11), Solomon (2), Moses (1),
Heman (1), Ethan (1), and anonymous. The Psalter was compiled over
approximately 1000 years and served as Israel's hymnal and prayer
book. The book is divided into five 'books' (1-41, 42-72, 73-89,
90-106, 107-150), paralleling the five books of Moses. Key themes:
worship, lament, praise, trust, kingship, creation, wisdom,
Messianic prophecy, and the full range of human emotion brought
before God.

Classification: Trust/Confidence Psalm
Attributed Author: David — "A Psalm of David"
Key Themes: God as Shepherd, complete provision, rest and
restoration, fearlessness in danger, abundance in God's presence,
lifelong goodness and mercy, eternal dwelling with God

Historical Context: The most beloved psalm in Scripture. David
writes from personal experience as a shepherd — he knows what a
good shepherd does because he WAS one (1 Sam 16:11; 17:34-36).
The psalm forms a trilogy with Psalms 22-24: Psalm 22 is the Good
Shepherd giving His life (John 10:11), Psalm 23 is the Great
Shepherd caring for His sheep (Heb 13:20), and Psalm 24 is the
Chief Shepherd returning in glory (1 Pet 5:4). The psalm uses two
metaphors: God as Shepherd (vv.1-4) and God as Host (vv.5-6).
Note the pronoun shift: vv.1-3 speak ABOUT God ("He"), vv.4-6
speak TO God ("Thou/You") — intimacy deepens in the valley.

Structure:
- The Shepherd Provides (vv.1-3)
- The Shepherd Protects in Darkness (v.4)
- The Host Prepares a Feast (v.5)
- Lifelong and Eternal Blessing (v.6)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.1              | John        | 10      | 11                 | "I am the good shepherd: the good shepherd giveth his life for the sheep." Christ fulfills this psalm
v.1              | Isaiah      | 40      | 11                 | "He shall feed his flock like a shepherd." Messianic shepherd prophecy
v.2              | Revelation  | 7       | 17                 | "The Lamb... shall lead them unto living fountains of waters." Eternal fulfillment of still waters
v.3              | Psalm       | 19      | 7                  | "The law of the LORD is perfect, converting the soul." Same "restoring/converting" language
v.4              | Isaiah      | 43      | 2                  | "When thou passest through the waters, I will be with thee." Same "through" + "with" promise
v.5              | Psalm       | 104     | 15                 | "Oil to make his face to shine." Anointing as blessing and honor
v.6              | Psalm       | 27      | 4                  | "That I may dwell in the house of the LORD all the days of my life." Same ultimate desire
v.6              | John        | 14      | 2                  | "In my Father's house are many mansions." Eternal dwelling place

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.1              | KJV         | ESV identical: "The LORD is my shepherd; I shall not want." — The translations agree perfectly. Hebrew "lo echsar" = I will not lack. The negative is absolute — no deficiency, no gap, no unmet need.
v.2              | KJV         | "He maketh me to lie down" / ESV: "He makes me lie down" — The shepherd causes rest. Sheep will not lie down unless free from fear, friction, flies, and hunger (Phillip Keller). God removes what prevents rest.
v.4              | KJV         | "the valley of the shadow of death" / ESV: "the valley of the shadow of death" — Hebrew "tsalmaveth" — traditionally parsed as "shadow of death" (tsel + maveth). Some modern scholars read it as "deep darkness" (a single word). Both translations retain the traditional rendering. The image is of deepest possible darkness — whether death or not.
v.5              | KJV         | "thou anointest my head with oil; my cup runneth over" / ESV: "you anoint my head with oil; my cup overflows" — Anointing = honor, healing, celebration. The overflowing cup = abundance beyond need. God does not merely meet minimum requirements — He provides extravagantly.
v.6              | KJV         | "goodness and mercy shall follow me" / ESV: "goodness and mercy shall follow me" — Hebrew "radaph" is the word for pursuing/chasing (often used of enemies pursuing). God's goodness and mercy PURSUE you with the same intensity an enemy would. You cannot outrun grace.

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. "MY shepherd" — personal, possessive (v.1). Not "a shepherd" or "the
   shepherd" but MY shepherd. The psalm's power is in the personal pronoun.
   He is not shepherd in general — He is YOUR shepherd specifically.

2. Rest is provided, not achieved (v.2). "He MAKES me lie down." Rest is
   something the shepherd produces by creating the right conditions. If you
   cannot rest, ask what conditions the Shepherd needs to address — and let
   Him.

3. The valley is THROUGH, not permanent (v.4). You walk THROUGH it — it has
   an entrance and an exit. No valley lasts forever. And the shadow of death
   is just that — a SHADOW. Shadows cannot harm; only the reality casts
   shadows. Christ has defeated death; what remains is only its shadow.

4. God feasts you before your enemies (v.5). He does not wait until the
   conflict is over. His provision is demonstrative — visible to those who
   oppose you. Your abundance in their presence is testimony to His
   faithfulness.

5. Goodness and mercy PURSUE you (v.6). You are not chasing God's blessings
   — they are chasing YOU. The same relentless energy an enemy would use to
   hunt you is the energy goodness and mercy use to find you. You cannot
   escape grace.

RESOURCES
------------------------------------
The Bible Project — Psalms Overview:
  https://bibleproject.com/explore/video/psalms/

David Guzik — Psalms 23 Written Commentary:
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/psalms-23/

BibleProject Podcast:
  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

========================================

Translations referenced: KJV, ESV, ASV, NET, WEB
Commentary sources: Enduring Word (enduringword.com), Charles
Spurgeon (Public Domain)

24.notes:
Psalms - Chapter 24
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
A processional/enthronement psalm — 'The earth is the LORD's'; who may ascend His holy hill? The one with clean hands and a pure heart. 'Lift up your heads, O ye gates — the King of glory shall come in!'

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Multiple authors — primarily David (73 psalms attributed),
plus Asaph (12), Sons of Korah (11), Solomon (2), Moses (1),
Heman (1), Ethan (1), and anonymous. The Psalter was compiled over
approximately 1000 years and served as Israel's hymnal and prayer
book. The book is divided into five 'books' (1-41, 42-72, 73-89,
90-106, 107-150), paralleling the five books of Moses. Key themes:
worship, lament, praise, trust, kingship, creation, wisdom,
Messianic prophecy, and the full range of human emotion brought
before God.

Classification: Entrance Liturgy / Hymn of Praise / Messianic
Attributed Author: David — "A Psalm of David"
Key Themes: God's universal ownership, entrance requirements for
worship, the King of Glory entering His gates, God as warrior-king,
the LORD of hosts

Historical Context: Psalm 24 completes the trilogy of Psalms 22-24
(Cross, Shepherd, Crown). It may have been composed for the Ark's
procession into Jerusalem (2 Sam 6:12-15) — the dramatic moment
when the Ark approached the gates of Zion. The psalm has a
responsive/antiphonal structure suggesting liturgical use: one
group asks "Who shall ascend?" (v.3); another answers (vv.4-5); a
processional voice cries "Lift up your heads, O gates!" (v.7);
gatekeepers respond "Who is this King of glory?" (v.8); and the
answer thunders: "The LORD strong and mighty!" This is also
prophetically applied to Christ's ascension and His future return
in glory.

Structure:
- God's Ownership of All Creation (vv.1-2)
- Who May Approach God? (vv.3-6)
- The King of Glory Enters (vv.7-10)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.1              | 1 Corinthians | 10      | 26                 | Paul quotes this: "The earth is the Lord's, and the fulness thereof." Christian freedom in eating
v.1              | Exodus      | 19      | 5                  | "All the earth is mine." Same ownership claim at Sinai
v.3-4            | Psalm       | 15      | 1-5                | Parallel entrance liturgy with expanded ethical requirements
v.3-4            | Isaiah      | 33      | 14-16              | "Who among us shall dwell with the devouring fire?" Third entrance liturgy
v.7              | 2 Samuel    | 6       | 12-15              | The Ark's entry into Jerusalem — the likely historical occasion
v.7              | Acts        | 1       | 9-11               | Christ's ascension — entering heaven's gates as the King of glory
v.10             | Isaiah      | 6       | 3                  | "The whole earth is full of his glory." The LORD of hosts revealed
v.10             | Revelation  | 19      | 11-16              | Christ returns as King of kings — the ultimate "King of glory" entrance

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.2              | KJV         | "founded it upon the seas" / ESV: "founded it upon the seas" — Ancient cosmology: the earth was understood as established over primordial waters. The theological point is not cosmology but sovereignty — God established the world over chaos and maintains it.
v.4              | KJV         | "lifted up his soul unto vanity" / ESV: "does not lift up his soul to what is false" — Hebrew "shav'" = emptiness, falsehood, worthlessness. ESV clarifies: the pure-hearted person does not orient their life toward what is empty/false (idols, lies, worthless things).
v.6              | KJV         | "that seek thy face, O Jacob" / ESV: "who seek the face of the God of Jacob" — The Hebrew is unusual. KJV reads as addressing Jacob/Israel. ESV (following some manuscripts) reads "the God of Jacob." ESV makes more theological sense — they seek GOD'S face, not Jacob's.
v.7              | KJV         | "everlasting doors" / ESV: "ancient doors" — Hebrew "pithchey olam" = doors of eternity/antiquity. Both translations work — these gates have stood since ancient times and will endure forever. They must expand to receive the infinitely glorious King.

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. Everything belongs to God (v.1). You own nothing ultimately. Your
   possessions, your body, your time, your relationships — all belong to the
   Creator. This is not restrictive but liberating: if He owns it all, He is
   responsible for it all.

2. Access to God requires integrity inside AND out (v.4). Clean hands
   (behavior) without a pure heart (motive) is hypocrisy. A pure heart
   without clean hands is self-deception. God requires both. Begin with the
   heart; the hands will follow.

3. The King of glory requires expanded gates (v.7). He is too great for the
   doors as they are. Our conceptions of God must be continually expanded —
   "lift up your heads." Our categories are always too small for the God who
   comes.

4. Three applications of the King's entry: (1) Historical — the Ark entering
   Jerusalem. (2) Heavenly — Christ ascending after resurrection. (3) Future
   — Christ returning in glory. In each case, the King comes to reclaim what
   is His.

5. "Who is this King of glory?" (vv.8, 10). The question is answered with
   God's most martial title: LORD of hosts, mighty in battle. The King who
   comes is not a passive figurehead but a warrior who has won the decisive
   battle. He enters as conqueror.

RESOURCES
------------------------------------
The Bible Project — Psalms Overview:
  https://bibleproject.com/explore/video/psalms/

David Guzik — Psalms 24 Written Commentary:
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/psalms-24/

BibleProject Podcast:
  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

========================================

Translations referenced: KJV, ESV, ASV, NET, WEB
Commentary sources: Enduring Word (enduringword.com), Charles
Spurgeon (Public Domain)

25.notes:
Psalms - Chapter 25
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
An acrostic lament and prayer — David asks God to teach him His paths, remember mercy not youthful sins, and guard his soul; 'the secret of the LORD is with them that fear him.'

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Multiple authors — primarily David (73 psalms attributed),
plus Asaph (12), Sons of Korah (11), Solomon (2), Moses (1),
Heman (1), Ethan (1), and anonymous. The Psalter was compiled over
approximately 1000 years and served as Israel's hymnal and prayer
book. The book is divided into five 'books' (1-41, 42-72, 73-89,
90-106, 107-150), paralleling the five books of Moses. Key themes:
worship, lament, praise, trust, kingship, creation, wisdom,
Messianic prophecy, and the full range of human emotion brought
before God.

Classification: Individual Lament / Acrostic Prayer
Attributed Author: David — "A Psalm of David"
Key Themes: Guidance, forgiveness of sins (youth and present),
waiting on God, divine instruction, the "secret" of the LORD,
covenant faithfulness, loneliness and affliction

Historical Context: An acrostic psalm (each verse begins with a
successive Hebrew letter, though imperfect). David combines three
strands: requests for guidance (vv.4-5, 8-9, 12), confession of
sin (vv.7, 11, 18), and appeals for protection from enemies
(vv.2-3, 19-20). The unusual combination suggests a period when
David felt both guilty and endangered — perhaps after sin brought
consequences that enemies exploited. V.14 — "The secret of the
LORD" — introduces the concept of divine intimacy available to
those who fear God: He shares His counsel with the reverent.

Structure:
- Trust and Petition (vv.1-7)
- God's Character as Teacher (vv.8-10)
- Confession and Plea (vv.11-15)
- Distress and Deliverance (vv.16-22)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.4              | Psalm       | 86      | 11                 | "Teach me thy way, O LORD; I will walk in thy truth." Same prayer for guidance
v.7              | Isaiah      | 43      | 25                 | "I, even I, am he that blotteth out thy transgressions for mine own sake." God forgets sin for His own name
v.9              | Matthew     | 5       | 5                  | "Blessed are the meek: for they shall inherit the earth." Echoes v.13
v.10             | Psalm       | 103     | 17-18              | "The mercy of the LORD is from everlasting... to such as keep his covenant." Covenant conditionality
v.14             | John        | 15      | 15                 | "I have called you friends; for all things that I have heard of my Father I have made known unto you." Jesus shares the Father's counsel with His own
v.14             | Amos        | 3       | 7                  | "Surely the Lord GOD will do nothing, but he revealeth his secret unto his servants." Same "secret/sod" concept
v.22             | Psalm       | 130     | 8                  | "He shall redeem Israel from all his iniquities." Same national redemption prayer

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.6              | KJV         | "thy tender mercies and thy lovingkindnesses" / ESV: "your mercy... and your steadfast love" — Hebrew "rachamim" (compassions, tender mercies) and "chasadim" (loyal loves, covenant faithfulnesses). Two distinct Hebrew words for two aspects of God's grace.
v.9              | KJV         | "The meek will he guide in judgment" / ESV: "He leads the humble in what is right" — Hebrew "anavim" = humble, afflicted, meek. God guides those who are teachable — not the proud or self-sufficient.
v.14             | KJV         | "The secret of the LORD" / ESV: "The friendship of the LORD" — Hebrew "sod" = secret counsel, intimate friendship circle, confidential assembly. ESV's "friendship" captures the relational dimension; KJV's "secret" captures the exclusivity. Both are present in the Hebrew.
v.16             | KJV         | "I am desolate and afflicted" / ESV: "I am lonely and afflicted" — Hebrew "yachid" = alone, solitary, only one. David feels isolated — not just attacked but ALONE in his suffering. ESV's "lonely" is poignant.

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. You can ask God to forget specific things (v.7). "Remember NOT the sins
   of my youth" — God can choose not to hold past sins against you. His
   memory is selective by grace. Your past does not define your future when
   God forgets.

2. God teaches sinners, not just saints (v.8). "He instructs SINNERS in the
   way" — you do not need to be perfect to receive guidance. God teaches
   those who are still failing. Come as you are — He meets sinners with
   instruction.

3. The "secret" of the LORD belongs to the reverent (v.14). Not the
   brilliant, not the religious, not the powerful — but those who FEAR Him.
   Intimacy with God is not earned by performance but received through
   reverence. He shares His counsel with those who stand in awe.

4. Great sin requires great forgiveness (v.11). David does not minimize: "my
   iniquity is GREAT." And the basis for pardon is not the sin's smallness
   but God's name. The greater the sin, the greater the glory when God
   forgives it. Do not hide the magnitude — confess it fully.

5. Loneliness is brought to God (v.16). "I am lonely and afflicted" — David
   does not hide isolation. He brings it to God as a prayer. Loneliness is
   not a sign of spiritual failure — it is a human experience God invites
   you to bring before Him.

------------------------------------

  https://bibleproject.com/explore/video/psalms/

David Guzik — Psalms 25 Written Commentary:
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/psalms-25/

BibleProject Podcast:
  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

26.notes:
Psalms - Chapter 26
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
A psalm of integrity — David asks God to examine and test him, professing he has walked in faithfulness, avoided the wicked, and loves the house where God's glory dwells.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Multiple authors — primarily David (73 psalms attributed),
plus Asaph (12), Sons of Korah (11), Solomon (2), Moses (1),
Heman (1), Ethan (1), and anonymous. The Psalter was compiled over
approximately 1000 years and served as Israel's hymnal and prayer
book. The book is divided into five 'books' (1-41, 42-72, 73-89,
90-106, 107-150), paralleling the five books of Moses. Key themes:
worship, lament, praise, trust, kingship, creation, wisdom,
Messianic prophecy, and the full range of human emotion brought
before God.

Classification: Protestation of Innocence / Individual Lament
Attributed Author: David — "A Psalm of David"
Key Themes: Integrity, self-examination before God, separation
from evildoers, love for God's house, worship at the altar,
standing on level ground

Historical Context: Like Psalms 7 and 17, David protests his
innocence and invites divine examination. He has been accused or
associated with wrongdoers and asks God to vindicate his integrity.
The psalm emphasizes David's CHOICES: who he does and does not
associate with (vv.4-5), his love for the temple (v.8), and his
determination to walk in integrity (v.11). It connects closely to
Psalm 15's entrance requirements — David claims to meet those
qualifications. The "washing hands" (v.6) likely refers to the
ritual washing before approaching the altar, but metaphorically
to moral preparation for worship.

Structure:
- Appeal for Vindication (vv.1-3)
- Separation from Evil Associates (vv.4-5)
- Approach to God's Altar (vv.6-8)
- Prayer Not to Share Sinners' Fate (vv.9-10)
- Renewed Commitment to Integrity (vv.11-12)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.1              | Psalm       | 7       | 8                  | "Judge me, O LORD, according to my righteousness." Same appeal for vindication
v.2              | Psalm       | 139     | 23-24              | "Search me, O God, and know my heart." Same invitation for divine examination
v.4              | Psalm       | 1       | 1                  | "Blessed is the man that walketh not in the counsel of the ungodly." Same separation principle
v.6              | Psalm       | 73      | 13                 | "I have cleansed my heart in vain, and washed my hands in innocency." Asaph echoes David's words in a crisis of faith
v.8              | Psalm       | 27      | 4                  | "One thing have I desired of the LORD... to behold the beauty of the LORD." Same temple-love
v.8              | Psalm       | 84      | 1-2                | "How amiable are thy tabernacles!" Same longing for God's house

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.1              | KJV         | "Judge me" / ESV: "Vindicate me" — Hebrew "shafat" = judge. ESV clarifies the intent: David is not asking for punishment but for favorable judgment — vindication. He wants God's verdict to clear his name.
v.4              | KJV         | "vain persons... dissemblers" / ESV: "men of falsehood... hypocrites" — KJV "dissemblers" = those who conceal their true nature. ESV "hypocrites" is the modern equivalent. David avoids the company of pretenders.
v.6              | KJV         | "I will wash mine hands in innocency" / ESV: "I wash my hands in innocence" — Allusion to Deut 21:6-7 (ritual handwashing declaring innocence of bloodshed) and priestly washing before altar service (Exod 30:19-21).
v.9              | KJV         | "Gather not my soul with sinners" / ESV: "Do not sweep my soul away with sinners" — David asks not to share the fate of the wicked. His association with integrity should separate his destiny from theirs.

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. Invite God's examination (v.2). If you are innocent, welcome scrutiny. If
   you cannot invite God to test your heart, that itself is diagnostic.
   Confidence before God is not arrogance — it is the fruit of integrity.

2. Who you sit with matters (vv.4-5). David is defined partly by who he
   refuses to associate with. Not isolation but discernment. You become like
   your company. Choose your companions with the same care you choose your
   commitments.

3. Love God's house (v.8). Not dutiful attendance but genuine affection.
   When you truly love the place where God is worshipped, attendance is not
   obligation but delight. Examine whether your worship attendance flows
   from love or duty.

4. Worship requires preparation (v.6). "Washing hands" before approaching
   the altar — worship is not casual. There is something to be PUT RIGHT
   before we come to God. Not perfection, but intentional preparation.

5. Stand on level ground (v.12). After examination, after separation from
   evil, after worship — David stands on stable ground. Integrity produces
   stability. When your life is honest, your footing is sure.

RESOURCES
------------------------------------
The Bible Project — Psalms Overview:
  https://bibleproject.com/explore/video/psalms/

David Guzik — Psalms 26 Written Commentary:
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/psalms-26/

BibleProject Podcast:
  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

Spurgeon (Public Domain)

27.ESV:
1. The LORD is my light and my salvation; whom shall I fear? The LORD is the stronghold of my life; of whom shall I be afraid?
2. When evildoers assail me to eat up my flesh, my adversaries and foes, it is they who stumble and fall.
3. Though an army encamp against me, my heart shall not fear; though war arise against me, yet I will be confident.
4. One thing have I asked of the LORD, that will I seek after: that I may dwell in the house of the LORD all the days of my life, to gaze upon the beauty of the LORD and to inquire in his temple.
5. For he will hide me in his shelter in the day of trouble; he will conceal me under the cover of his tent; he will lift me high upon a rock.
6. And now my head shall be lifted up above my enemies all around me, and I will offer in his tent sacrifices with shouts of joy; I will sing and make melody to the LORD.
7. Hear, O LORD, when I cry aloud; be gracious to me and answer me!
8. You have said, “Seek my face.” My heart says to you, “Your face, LORD, do I seek.”
9. Hide not your face from me. Turn not your servant away in anger, O you who have been my help. Cast me not off; forsake me not, O God of my salvation!
10. For my father and my mother have forsaken me, but the LORD will take me in.
11. Teach me your way, O LORD, and lead me on a level path because of my enemies.
12. Give me not up to the will of my adversaries; for false witnesses have risen against me, and they breathe out violence.
13. I believe that I shall look upon the goodness of the LORD in the land of the living!
14. Wait for the LORD; be strong, and let your heart take courage; wait for the LORD!

1. By David. The Lord is my light and my salvation. I fear no one. The Lord protects my life. I am afraid of no one.
2. When evil men attack me to devour my flesh, when my adversaries and enemies attack me, they stumble and fall.
3. Even when an army is deployed against me, I do not fear. Even when war is imminent, I remain confident.

10. Even if my father and mother abandoned me, the Lord would take me in.
11. Teach me how you want me to live, Lord; lead me along a level path because of those who wait to ambush me.

6. Now my head will be lifted up above my enemies around me; I will offer sacrifices of joy in his tent. I will sing, yes, I will sing praises to Yahweh.
7. Hear, Yahweh, when I cry with my voice. Have mercy also on me, and answer me.
8. When you said, “Seek my face,” my heart said to you, “I will seek your face, Yahweh.”
9. Don’t hide your face from me. Don’t put your servant away in anger. You have been my help. Don’t abandon me, neither forsake me, God of my salvation.
10. When my father and my mother forsake me, then Yahweh will take me up.
11. Teach me your way, Yahweh. Lead me in a straight path, because of my enemies.
12. Don’t deliver me over to the desire of my adversaries, for false witnesses have risen up against me, such as breathe out cruelty.

27.notes:
Psalms - Chapter 27
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
A psalm of trust and longing — 'The LORD is my light and my salvation; whom shall I fear?' David's one desire is to dwell in God's house; though father and mother forsake him, the LORD will take him up. 'Wait on the LORD.'

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Multiple authors — primarily David (73 psalms attributed),
plus Asaph (12), Sons of Korah (11), Solomon (2), Moses (1),
Heman (1), Ethan (1), and anonymous. The Psalter was compiled over
approximately 1000 years and served as Israel's hymnal and prayer
book. The book is divided into five 'books' (1-41, 42-72, 73-89,
90-106, 107-150), paralleling the five books of Moses. Key themes:
worship, lament, praise, trust, kingship, creation, wisdom,
Messianic prophecy, and the full range of human emotion brought
before God.

Classification: Trust/Confidence Psalm with Lament
Attributed Author: David — "A Psalm of David"
Key Themes: God as light and salvation, fearlessness, one supreme
desire (God's presence), divine shelter, seeking God's face,
abandonment by parents but not God, waiting on the LORD

Historical Context: Psalm 27 has two dramatically different halves:
vv.1-6 are radiant confidence; vv.7-14 are urgent pleading. Some
scholars suggest two psalms combined, but the contrast teaches that
confidence and plea coexist in real faith. David faces enemies,
false witnesses, and even parental abandonment (v.10), yet his
central desire remains singular: to dwell in God's presence and
behold His beauty (v.4). This "one thing" theology reduces life
to its essential priority. The psalm ends with the memorable
command to "wait on the LORD" — repeated twice for emphasis.

Structure:
- Bold Confidence: Light and Salvation (vv.1-6)
- Urgent Plea: Hear and Answer (vv.7-12)
- Concluding Faith: Wait on the LORD (vv.13-14)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.1              | John        | 8       | 12                 | "I am the light of the world." Christ as the fulfillment of God as "light."
v.1              | Micah       | 7       | 8                  | "When I sit in darkness, the LORD shall be a light unto me." Same light-in-darkness theology
v.4              | Luke        | 10      | 42                 | "One thing is needful." Mary chose the same "one thing" — presence with Jesus
v.4              | Philippians | 3       | 13                 | "This one thing I do." Paul's singular focus echoes David's
v.8              | 2 Chronicles | 7       | 14                 | "If my people... shall seek my face." God's invitation to face-seeking
v.10             | Isaiah      | 49      | 15                 | "Can a woman forget her sucking child?... yet will I not forget thee." God surpasses even maternal faithfulness
v.14             | Isaiah      | 40      | 31                 | "They that wait upon the LORD shall renew their strength." Same waiting-and-strength theology

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.1              | KJV         | "the strength of my life" / ESV: "the stronghold of my life" — Hebrew "ma'oz" = stronghold, fortress, refuge. ESV's "stronghold" is more precise than KJV's abstract "strength."
v.4              | KJV         | "to behold the beauty of the LORD" / ESV: "to gaze upon the beauty of the LORD" — Hebrew "no'am" = pleasantness, beauty, graciousness. David wants to GAZE (not glance) at God's loveliness. Sustained contemplation, not brief acknowledgment.
v.10             | KJV         | "the LORD will take me up" / ESV: "the LORD will take me in" — Hebrew "ya'aspheni" = will gather me, receive me. The image is of adoption — God gathers the orphaned/ abandoned into His own family.
v.13             | KJV         | places "I had fainted, unless" in italics (not in Hebrew). The Hebrew literally reads: "[Surely] I believe I will see the goodness of the LORD in the land of the living." The KJV adds the implied clause for sense. ESV: "I believe that I shall look upon the goodness of the LORD" — simple confidence statement.

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. If God is your light, fear is irrational (v.1). The logic is complete: if
   the LORD handles darkness, danger, and attack — what is left to fear?
   Fear persists when we forget who God is. Remind yourself of His identity,
   and fear loses its basis.

2. Reduce life to "one thing" (v.4). David had many needs, many enemies,
   many responsibilities — but ONE desire dominated: God's presence.
   Simplify. What is the one thing? Everything else flows from or is
   secondary to this.

3. God adopts the abandoned (v.10). Even if every human relationship fails —
   parents, spouse, friends — God receives you. You cannot be orphaned by
   God. He gathers what others scatter.

4. Believe you will see goodness IN THIS LIFE (v.13). Not only in heaven.
   David expected to see God's goodness "in the land of the living" — this
   side of eternity. Hope is not only eschatological — God is good NOW.

5. Wait — and then wait again (v.14). Repeated: "wait on the LORD... wait, I
   say, on the LORD." The repetition acknowledges that waiting is hard
   enough to need reinforcement. Be strong. Take courage. And wait.

28.notes:
Psalms - Chapter 28
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
A lament turning to praise — David cries out lest God's silence mean death, asks not to be dragged away with the wicked, then suddenly rejoices because the LORD has heard and become his strength and shield.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Multiple authors — primarily David (73 psalms attributed),
plus Asaph (12), Sons of Korah (11), Solomon (2), Moses (1),
Heman (1), Ethan (1), and anonymous. The Psalter was compiled over
approximately 1000 years and served as Israel's hymnal and prayer
book. The book is divided into five 'books' (1-41, 42-72, 73-89,
90-106, 107-150), paralleling the five books of Moses. Key themes:
worship, lament, praise, trust, kingship, creation, wisdom,
Messianic prophecy, and the full range of human emotion brought
before God.

Classification: Individual Lament / Thanksgiving
Attributed Author: David — "A Psalm of David"
Key Themes: Fear of God's silence, separation from the wicked's
fate, God as rock and shield, answered prayer producing praise,
shepherd-king theology

Historical Context: David fears that if God remains silent, he
will die ("become like them that go down into the pit"). He prays
toward the "holy oracle" (debir = the inner sanctuary, Holy of
Holies) — the symbolic location of God's presence and speech. The
psalm's structure shows the pivot from desperate plea (vv.1-5) to
joyful thanksgiving (vv.6-9). The key wicked characteristic: they
"speak peace to their neighbours, but mischief is in their hearts"
(v.3) — peaceful words concealing violent intentions. V.9's prayer
to "feed them" and "lift them up forever" echoes Psalm 23's
shepherd imagery — David as king asks God to shepherd His people.

Structure:
- Plea for God's Voice (vv.1-2)
- Separation from the Wicked (vv.3-5)
- Thanksgiving for Answered Prayer (vv.6-7)
- Prayer for God's People (vv.8-9)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.1              | Psalm       | 143     | 7                  | "Hear me speedily, O LORD: my spirit faileth: hide not thy face from me, lest I be like unto them that go down into the pit." Same fear of divine silence
v.2              | 1 Kings     | 8       | 28-30              | Solomon's prayer toward the temple's inner sanctuary. Same directional prayer
v.3              | Psalm       | 12      | 2                  | "They speak vanity every one with his neighbour: with flattering lips." Same duplicitous speech
v.7              | Psalm       | 18      | 2                  | "The LORD is my rock, and my fortress." Same compound divine titles
v.9              | Isaiah      | 40      | 11                 | "He shall feed his flock like a shepherd: he shall gather the lambs." Shepherd language for God
v.9              | Psalm       | 23      | 1                  | "The LORD is my shepherd." Same shepherd theology applied to all God's people

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.2              | KJV         | "toward thy holy oracle" / ESV: "toward your most holy sanctuary" — Hebrew "debir" = the innermost room of the temple (Holy of Holies). The "oracle" because God spoke from between the cherubim. KJV captures the speech-dimension; ESV captures the spatial dimension.
v.5              | KJV         | "he shall destroy them, and not build them up" / ESV: "he will tear them down and build them up no more" — The imagery is architectural: God tears down what the wicked build, and refuses to rebuild them. Permanent deconstruction.
v.9              | KJV         | "feed them also, and lift them up for ever" / ESV: "Be their shepherd and carry them forever" — ESV makes the shepherd metaphor explicit. "Carry" (nasa = lift, bear) — God carries His people like a shepherd carries lambs.

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. God's silence feels like death (v.1). When God seems quiet, the spiritual
   danger is real — not physical death but the death of hope. In silence,
   keep crying out. Persistence in prayer during divine silence is the test
   of faith.

2. Beware peaceful words with mischievous hearts (v.3). The most dangerous
   enemies speak kindly while plotting harm. Discernment requires looking
   beyond words to patterns, fruit, and consistency.

3. Trust produces the sequence: help → joy → praise (v.7). The chain begins
   with trust, not with favorable circumstances. Trust is the first domino.
   Set it in motion and the sequence follows.

4. Pray beyond yourself (v.9). David moves from personal crisis to communal
   intercession. True spiritual maturity expands outward — from "save ME" to
   "save YOUR PEOPLE." Let your personal deliverance produce intercession
   for others.

5. God carries His people forever (v.9). Not temporarily, not conditionally
   — forever. The Shepherd does not set down His lambs. He carries them
   through every wilderness.

RESOURCES
------------------------------------
The Bible Project — Psalms Overview:
  https://bibleproject.com/explore/video/psalms/

David Guzik — Psalms 28 Written Commentary:
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/psalms-28/

BibleProject Podcast:
  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

29.notes:
Psalms - Chapter 29
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
A hymn of praise — 'the voice of the LORD' thunders seven times over the waters, breaking cedars, shaking the wilderness, stripping forests; the LORD sits enthroned over the flood and gives His people strength and peace.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Multiple authors — primarily David (73 psalms attributed),
plus Asaph (12), Sons of Korah (11), Solomon (2), Moses (1),
Heman (1), Ethan (1), and anonymous. The Psalter was compiled over
approximately 1000 years and served as Israel's hymnal and prayer
book. The book is divided into five 'books' (1-41, 42-72, 73-89,
90-106, 107-150), paralleling the five books of Moses. Key themes:
worship, lament, praise, trust, kingship, creation, wisdom,
Messianic prophecy, and the full range of human emotion brought
before God.

Classification: Hymn of Praise / Theophany / Nature Psalm
Attributed Author: David — "A Psalm of David"
Key Themes: The voice of the LORD (sevenfold), God's glory in the
storm, creation responding to God, God enthroned over chaos, peace
for God's people

Historical Context: Called "The Psalm of the Thunderstorm" — David
watches a violent storm move across Lebanon from the Mediterranean
and sees God's voice in every element: thunder, lightning,
earthquake, wind. "The voice of the LORD" appears seven times
(vv.3-9), representing completeness. Some scholars see polemic
against Canaanite Baal worship — Baal was the storm god, but
David declares YAHWEH as the true God of the storm. The psalm
begins with heavenly worship (v.1), moves through earthly
manifestation (vv.3-9), and ends with God enthroned in sovereign
peace (vv.10-11). Used in Jewish liturgy for Pentecost.

Structure:
- Call to Heavenly Worship (vv.1-2)
- The Sevenfold Voice of the LORD (vv.3-9)
- God Enthroned — Peace for His People (vv.10-11)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.1              | Psalm       | 103     | 20                 | "Bless the LORD, ye his angels." Same call for heavenly beings to worship
v.3              | Psalm       | 18      | 13                 | "The LORD also thundered in the heavens." Same storm-theophany
v.5              | Isaiah      | 2       | 13                 | "Upon all the cedars of Lebanon." Cedars as symbols of pride that God breaks
v.10             | ?           | ?       | ?                  | Genesis 6-9 — "The flood" — God's sovereignty over the primordial deluge.
v.10             | Psalm       | 93      | 1-4                | "The LORD reigneth... mightier than the noise of many waters." Same sovereignty over chaos
v.11             | Numbers     | 6       | 26                 | "The LORD... give thee peace." The Aaronic blessing's peace promise
v.11             | Isaiah      | 26      | 3                  | "Thou wilt keep him in perfect peace." Same divine peace theology

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.1              | KJV         | "O ye mighty" / ESV: "O heavenly beings" — Hebrew "bene elim" = sons of God/sons of the mighty. ESV interprets as angelic beings; KJV as powerful ones. Context suggests the heavenly court — angels called to worship.
v.6              | KJV         | "like a young unicorn" / ESV: "like a young wild ox" — Hebrew "re'em" = wild ox (aurochs). KJV follows the LXX "monokeros" (one-horned). The aurochs was a massive wild bovine, now extinct. The image: mighty Lebanon SKIPS like a calf before God's voice.
v.9              | KJV         | "maketh the hinds to calve, and discovereth the forests" / ESV: "makes the deer give birth and strips the forests bare" — The storm's power is so great it triggers premature labor in deer and strips leaves from entire forests. God's voice affects all living creation.
v.10             | KJV         | "the flood" / ESV: "the flood" — Hebrew "mabbul" is used only here and in Genesis 6-9. This is THE flood — the great primordial chaos. God sat enthroned then; He sits enthroned now. No storm threatens His sovereignty.

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. God's voice is in the storm (vv.3-9). When life is stormy — thunder,
   chaos, destruction — listen for God's voice IN it. He is not absent from
   the storm; He speaks THROUGH it. The storm does not threaten Him — it
   serves Him.

2. God sits above the flood (v.10). Whatever "flood" threatens you —
   financial, relational, health — God is seated above it as King. He is not
   pacing in anxiety. He is enthroned in sovereignty. Your flood is under
   His throne.

3. The God of power gives peace (v.11). The same God whose voice breaks
   cedars gives PEACE to His people. His terrifying power serves your
   tranquility. The storm-God is your peace-giver.

4. Seven voices = complete revelation. God reveals Himself fully through His
   world. Creation is not silent — it is a sevenfold sermon about God's
   power, majesty, and glory. Pay attention.

5. Worship begins in heaven before it reaches earth (vv.1-2). The call goes
   first to "heavenly beings" — worship is already happening in heaven. When
   we worship, we JOIN something already in progress, not start something
   new.

RESOURCES
------------------------------------
The Bible Project — Psalms Overview:
  https://bibleproject.com/explore/video/psalms/

David Guzik — Psalms 29 Written Commentary:
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/psalms-29/

BibleProject Podcast:
  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

30.NET:
1. A psalm, a song used at the dedication of the temple; by David. I will praise you, O Lord, for you lifted me up and did not allow my enemies to gloat over me.
2. O Lord my God, I cried out to you and you healed me.
3. O Lord, you pulled me up from Sheol; you rescued me from among those descending into the grave.
4. Sing to the Lord, you faithful followers of his; give thanks to his holy name.
5. For his anger lasts only a brief moment, and his good favor restores one’s life. One may experience sorrow during the night, but joy arrives in the morning.
6. In my self-confidence I said, “I will never be shaken.”
7. O Lord, in your good favor you made me secure. Then you rejected me and I was terrified.
8. To you, O Lord, I cried out; I begged the Lord for mercy:
9. “What profit is there in taking my life, in my descending into the Pit? Can the dust of the grave praise you? Can it declare your loyalty?
10. Hear, O Lord, and have mercy on me. O Lord, deliver me.”
11. Then you turned my lament into dancing; you removed my sackcloth and covered me with joy.
12. So now my heart will sing to you and not be silent; O Lord my God, I will always give thanks to you.
30.WEB:
1. I will extol you, Yahweh, for you have raised me up, and have not made my foes to rejoice over me.
2. Yahweh my God, I cried to you, and you have healed me.
3. Yahweh, you have brought up my soul from Sheol. You have kept me alive, that I should not go down to the pit.
4. Sing praise to Yahweh, you saints of his. Give thanks to his holy name.
5. For his anger is but for a moment. His favor is for a lifetime. Weeping may stay for the night, but joy comes in the morning.
6. As for me, I said in my prosperity, “I shall never be moved.”
7. You, Yahweh, when you favored me, made my mountain stand strong; but when you hid your face, I was troubled.
8. I cried to you, Yahweh. I made supplication to the Lord:
9. “What profit is there in my destruction, if I go down to the pit? Shall the dust praise you? Shall it declare your truth?
10. Hear, Yahweh, and have mercy on me. Yahweh, be my helper.”
11. You have turned my mourning into dancing for me. You have removed my sackcloth, and clothed me with gladness,
12. To the end that my heart may sing praise to you, and not be silent. Yahweh my God, I will give thanks to you forever!
30.notes:
Psalms - Chapter 30
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
A thanksgiving psalm — David praises God for healing and rescue from the pit; weeping may endure for a night, but joy comes in the morning; God turned his mourning into dancing.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Multiple authors — primarily David (73 psalms attributed),
plus Asaph (12), Sons of Korah (11), Solomon (2), Moses (1),
Heman (1), Ethan (1), and anonymous. The Psalter was compiled over
approximately 1000 years and served as Israel's hymnal and prayer
book. The book is divided into five 'books' (1-41, 42-72, 73-89,
90-106, 107-150), paralleling the five books of Moses. Key themes:
worship, lament, praise, trust, kingship, creation, wisdom,
Messianic prophecy, and the full range of human emotion brought
before God.

Classification: Individual Thanksgiving
Attributed Author: David — "A Psalm and Song at the dedication of
the house of David"
Key Themes: Healing and restoration, mourning turned to dancing,
the brevity of God's anger vs. the permanence of His favor,
complacency shattered, joy in the morning

Historical Context: The superscription connects this to "the
dedication of the house" — possibly David's palace (2 Sam 5:11)
or more likely used later for the temple dedication. It describes
recovery from a near-death experience (severe illness or mortal
danger). David confesses complacency in prosperity (v.6: "I shall
never be moved") that was shattered when God "hid his face" (v.7).
This humbling drove David back to desperate prayer, which God
answered with healing and restoration. The psalm's great
contribution is v.5: "Weeping may endure for a night, but joy
cometh in the morning" — one of the Bible's most beloved promises.

Structure:
- Praise for Deliverance (vv.1-3)
- Call to Communal Praise (vv.4-5)
- Confession of Complacency (vv.6-7)
- The Prayer in Crisis (vv.8-10)
- Mourning Turned to Dancing (vv.11-12)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.3              | Psalm       | 86      | 13                 | "Thou hast delivered my soul from the lowest hell." Same rescue from death/Sheol
v.5              | Isaiah      | 54      | 7-8                | "For a small moment have I forsaken thee; but with great mercies will I gather thee." Same moment- vs-eternity proportion
v.5              | 2 Corinthians | 4       | 17                 | "Our light affliction, which is but for a moment, worketh for us a far more exceeding and eternal weight of glory." NT echo
v.6              | Deuteronomy | 8       | 12-14              | "When thou hast eaten and art full... then thine heart be lifted up, and thou forget the LORD." Prosperity-induced forgetfulness
v.9              | Psalm       | 6       | 5                  | "In death there is no remembrance of thee." Same argument for life-preservation
v.11             | Jeremiah    | 31      | 13                 | "I will turn their mourning into joy." Prophetic fulfillment of the same reversal
v.11             | Revelation  | 21      | 4                  | "God shall wipe away all tears." The ultimate mourning-to-joy transformation

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.1              | KJV         | "thou hast lifted me up" / ESV: "you have drawn me up" — Hebrew "dillitani" = drew up (as drawing water from a well). David was in the depths and God drew him upward. The image is rescue from a pit or cistern.
v.5              | KJV         | "in his favour is life" / ESV: "his favor is for a lifetime" — Hebrew "b'ratsono chayyim." KJV reads: His favor IS life itself. ESV reads: His favor lasts a lifetime. Both are valid. KJV is more theologically profound — God's favor is not merely long-lasting but IS life itself.
v.9              | KJV         | "What profit is there in my blood" / ESV: "What profit is there in my death" — David argues with God: what do You gain from my death? The dead cannot praise You. Keep me alive for YOUR glory. A bold but legitimate prayer logic.
v.11             | KJV         | "girded me with gladness" / ESV: "clothed me with gladness" — Hebrew "azartani" = belted, girded, wrapped. Like changing garments — sackcloth removed, gladness put on like a festive robe. Joy as clothing you wear visibly.

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. Joy comes in the morning (v.5). Whatever night you are in, it has a
   morning. Weeping is temporary — it "endures" for a night but does not
   take up permanent residence. Hold on through the darkness; dawn is
   coming.

2. Prosperity can produce dangerous complacency (v.6). "I shall never be
   moved" — said in ease, disproved by crisis. When life is good, remember
   it is GOD'S FAVOR making your mountain strong (v.7), not your own
   stability. Ease without gratitude breeds false security.

3. God hides His face to restore dependence (v.7). When God "hid his face,"
   David was troubled — and that trouble drove him back to prayer (vv.8-10).
   Sometimes God withdraws felt presence to restore desperate dependence.
   The withdrawal is purposeful.

4. Mourning is TURNED into dancing (v.11). Not replaced, not followed —
   TURNED. The very thing that was mourning becomes dancing. God transforms
   suffering itself into celebration. Your darkest season becomes your
   greatest testimony.

5. The purpose of deliverance is endless praise (v.12). "That my glory may
   sing praise to thee, and not be silent." God rescues so that praise flows
   — forever. You were saved to sing. Do not be silent about what He has
   done.

RESOURCES
------------------------------------
The Bible Project — Psalms Overview:
  https://bibleproject.com/explore/video/psalms/

David Guzik — Psalms 30 Written Commentary:
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/psalms-30/

BibleProject Podcast:
  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

========================================
Study Notes generated for personal Bible study use.
Translations referenced: KJV, ESV, ASV, NET, WEB
Commentary sources: Enduring Word (enduringword.com), Charles
Spurgeon (Public Domain)

31.notes:
Psalms - Chapter 31
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
A lament and trust psalm — 'Into thine hand I commit my spirit' (Jesus' final words from the cross); David is in distress but trusts God's steadfast love and encourages all who wait for the LORD.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Multiple authors — primarily David (73 psalms attributed),
plus Asaph (12), Sons of Korah (11), Solomon (2), Moses (1),
Heman (1), Ethan (1), and anonymous. The Psalter was compiled over
approximately 1000 years and served as Israel's hymnal and prayer
book. The book is divided into five 'books' (1-41, 42-72, 73-89,
90-106, 107-150), paralleling the five books of Moses. Key themes:
worship, lament, praise, trust, kingship, creation, wisdom,
Messianic prophecy, and the full range of human emotion brought
before God.

Classification: Individual Lament and Trust Psalm
Attributed Author: David ("To the chief Musician, A Psalm of David")
Key Themes: Trust in distress, committing one's spirit to God,
God's goodness stored up for His people, God as a strong city
of refuge

Historical Context: Psalm 31 is a deeply personal psalm of David
written during a time of intense persecution and distress. While
the specific occasion is uncertain, many scholars associate it with
David's flight from Saul or possibly the rebellion of Absalom. The
psalm moves dramatically between desperate lament and confident
trust, reflecting David's raw honesty before God in crisis.

This psalm holds immense theological significance because verse 5
("Into thine hand I commit my spirit") was quoted by Jesus Christ
on the cross as His final words (Luke 23:46). Stephen also echoed
this sentiment at his martyrdom (Acts 7:59). The phrase represents
the ultimate act of trust — placing one's very life into God's
hands when all earthly hope has failed.

The psalm's structure reveals a pattern common in lament psalms:
it begins with a declaration of trust, moves into descriptions of
distress, returns to confident faith, and ends with exhortation
to others. Verse 21's mention of a "strong city" (KJV) or
"besieged city" (ESV) may refer to David's experience at Keilah
(1 Samuel 23) or another specific crisis.

Structure:
- Declaration of Trust and Plea for Deliverance (vv. 1-8)
- Description of Distress and Suffering (vv. 9-13)
- Renewed Confidence in God (vv. 14-18)
- Praise for God's Goodness (vv. 19-22)
- Exhortation to the Saints (vv. 23-24)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.1              | Psalm       | 71      | 1                  | 3: Psalm 71 opens with nearly identical language, suggesting either literary dependence or common liturgical phrasing for prayers of refuge
v.5              | Luke        | 23      | 46                 | : Jesus quotes this verse as His final words on the cross, committing His spirit to the Father in the ultimate act of trust in death
v.5              | Acts        | 7       | 59                 | : Stephen echoes this at his martyrdom ("Lord Jesus, receive my spirit"), extending the pattern of committing one's spirit to God in death
v.11-12          | Psalm       | 88      | 8,18               | : Similar language about friends fleeing and being forgotten like the dead, showing this experience of social abandonment is common in deep suffering
v.13             | Jeremiah    | 20      | 10                 | : Jeremiah uses the same phrase "fear on every side" (magor missabib), connecting this psalm to the experience of persecuted prophets
v.15             | Psalm       | 139     | 16                 | : "My times are in thy hand" connects to God's exhaustive knowledge of all our days before one comes to be
v.19             | Isaiah      | 64      | 4                  | ; 1 Corinthians 2:9: The concept of God storing up unseen goodness for those who love Him is expanded in the New Testament's vision of prepared glory
v.23-24          | Psalm       | 27      | 14                 | : Nearly identical exhortation to wait and be courageous, a repeated refrain throughout the Psalter

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.5              | KJV         | "commit" (KJV) / "commit" (ESV): Both translate Hebrew 'apqid (from paqad — to entrust, deposit). The word carries the sense of placing something valuable in another's custody for safekeeping, like depositing treasure with a trusted guardian.
v.6              | KJV         | "I have hated them that regard lying vanities" (KJV) / "I hate those who pay regard to worthless idols" (ESV): The KJV follows the Qere reading (first person), while the Hebrew Ketiv could be read differently. "Lying vanities" (hevelei shav) refers to false gods or empty deceptions. ESV's "worthless idols" makes the reference to idolatry more explicit.
v.10             | KJV         | "mine iniquity" (KJV) / "my iniquity" (ESV): Some Hebrew manuscripts read "my affliction" (oni) rather than "my iniquity" (avoni). The difference is a single Hebrew letter. This affects whether David's suffering is attributed to his own sin or to external persecution.
v.17             | KJV         | "silent in the grave" (KJV) / "go silently to Sheol" (ESV): KJV translates Sheol as "the grave," while ESV retains the Hebrew term. Sheol is the realm of the dead in Hebrew thought — not precisely equivalent to the later concept of hell but the shadowy abode of departed spirits.
v.20             | KJV         | "the pride of man" (KJV) / "the plots of men" (ESV): Hebrew rokes can mean either "pride/arrogance" or "plots/ conspiracies." The ESV interpretation connects to the context of enemies scheming, while KJV emphasizes the general hostility of proud men.

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. Committing our spirit to God is not only for death — it is a daily
   practice of surrendering our lives, plans, anxieties, and uncertainties
   into His faithful hands. What areas of your life have you not yet
   "committed" to the Lord?

2. "My times are in thy hand" (v.15) addresses our deepest fears about the
   future. If God holds all our times — our joys, our sufferings, our length
   of days — then nothing catches Him off guard. How does this truth change
   your response to uncertainty?

3. David experienced social abandonment — friends fleeing, being forgotten
   like a dead man (vv.11-12). Have you experienced isolation in suffering?
   David's response was not bitterness but renewed trust: "But I trusted in
   thee" (v.14). The word "but" marks the turning point from despair to
   faith.

4. God's goodness is "laid up" (v.19) — stored and reserved for those who
   fear Him. Much of God's blessing is unseen in the present moment. Are you
   willing to trust in stored-up goodness you cannot yet perceive, or do you
   demand immediate evidence?

5. The psalm ends with community (vv.23-24). Personal trust in God leads to
   encouraging others. After finding God faithful in your own trials, how
   are you strengthening others who are still in their season of waiting?

RESOURCES
------------------------------------
The Bible Project — Psalms Overview:
  https://bibleproject.com/explore/video/psalms/

David Guzik — Psalms 31 Written Commentary:
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/psalms-31/

BibleProject Podcast:
  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

Translations referenced: KJV, ESV, ASV, NET, WEB
Commentary sources: Enduring Word (enduringword.com), Charles
Spurgeon (Public Domain)

32.ESV:
1. Blessed is the one whose transgression is forgiven, whose sin is covered.
2. Blessed is the man against whom the LORD counts no iniquity, and in whose spirit there is no deceit.
3. For when I kept silent, my bones wasted away through my groaning all day long.
4. For day and night your hand was heavy upon me; my strength was dried up as by the heat of summer. Selah
5. I acknowledged my sin to you, and I did not cover my iniquity; I said, “I will confess my transgressions to the LORD,” and you forgave the iniquity of my sin. Selah
6. Therefore let everyone who is godly offer prayer to you at a time when you may be found; surely in the rush of great waters, they shall not reach him.
7. You are a hiding place for me; you preserve me from trouble; you surround me with shouts of deliverance. Selah
8. I will instruct you and teach you in the way you should go; I will counsel you with my eye upon you.
9. Be not like a horse or a mule, without understanding, which must be curbed with bit and bridle, or it will not stay near you.
10. Many are the sorrows of the wicked, but steadfast love surrounds the one who trusts in the LORD.
11. Be glad in the LORD, and rejoice, O righteous, and shout for joy, all you upright in heart!

32.notes:
Psalms - Chapter 32
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
A penitential/wisdom psalm — 'Blessed is he whose transgression is forgiven'; David recounts the agony of unconfessed sin and the relief of confession; God is a hiding place and surrounds the forgiven with songs of deliverance.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Multiple authors — primarily David (73 psalms attributed),
plus Asaph (12), Sons of Korah (11), Solomon (2), Moses (1),
Heman (1), Ethan (1), and anonymous. The Psalter was compiled over
approximately 1000 years and served as Israel's hymnal and prayer
book. The book is divided into five 'books' (1-41, 42-72, 73-89,
90-106, 107-150), paralleling the five books of Moses. Key themes:
worship, lament, praise, trust, kingship, creation, wisdom,
Messianic prophecy, and the full range of human emotion brought
before God.

Classification: Penitential Psalm / Wisdom Psalm (2nd of 7
Penitential Psalms: 6, 32, 38, 51, 102, 130, 143)
Attributed Author: David ("A Psalm of David, Maschil")
Key Themes: Blessedness of forgiveness, confession versus silence,
God as hiding place, divine guidance, the physical toll of
unconfessed sin

Historical Context: Psalm 32 is widely understood as David's
companion to Psalm 51 — while Psalm 51 records the act of
confession after his sin with Bathsheba, Psalm 32 celebrates the
result of that confession: complete forgiveness. The superscription
"Maschil" indicates it is a teaching psalm, intended to instruct
others in the way of repentance and grace.

Augustine of Hippo reportedly had this psalm inscribed on the wall
beside his deathbed and wept as he read it. It was one of Martin
Luther's favorite psalms, calling it a "Pauline psalm" because of
its emphasis on grace apart from works. Paul himself quotes
verses 1-2 in Romans 4:6-8 to demonstrate that justification
comes through faith, not works — David pronouncing "blessedness"
on the one whose sin is covered apart from merit.

The psalm's structure moves from celebration (vv.1-2), to
testimony of what unconfessed sin produced (vv.3-4), to the
turning point of confession (v.5), to instruction for others
(vv.6-9), and finally to a wisdom summary and praise (vv.10-11).
The three Hebrew words for sin in verses 1-2 (pesha — rebellion,
chata'ah — missing the mark, avon — twisted iniquity) matched by
three words for forgiveness (nasa — lifted/carried away, kasah —
covered, lo chashab — not counted/imputed) reveal the
comprehensiveness of God's pardon.

Structure:
- The Blessedness of Forgiveness (vv. 1-2)
- The Misery of Unconfessed Sin (vv. 3-4)
- The Turning Point: Confession and Pardon (v. 5)
- Instruction: Seeking God While He May Be Found (vv. 6-7)
- Divine Guidance Promised (vv. 8-9)
- Wisdom Summary and Call to Praise (vv. 10-11)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.1-2            | Romans      | 4       | 6                  | 8: Paul quotes these verses directly to establish the doctrine of justification by faith apart from works, showing David understood imputed righteousness
v.1              | Psalm       | 51      | 1                  | 2: The companion psalm of confession. Psalm 51 is the prayer; Psalm 32 is the testimony after the prayer is answered
v.3-4            | Psalm       | 38      | 1                  | 8: Another penitential psalm describing the physical suffering that accompanies divine discipline for sin
v.5              | Proverbs    | 28      | 13                 | : "He that covereth his sins shall not prosper: but whoso confesseth and forsaketh them shall have mercy." The same principle stated in wisdom literature
v.5              | 1 John      | 1       | 9                  | : "If we confess our sins, he is faithful and just to forgive us our sins, and to cleanse us from all unrighteousness." The New Testament application of David's experience
v.6              | Isaiah      | 55      | 6                  | : "Seek ye the LORD while he may be found, call ye upon him while he is near." The urgency of seeking God in the available moment
v.8-9            | Psalm       | 25      | 8                  | 9,12: God guiding the humble and meek in His way — those teachable through gentle leading rather than requiring bit and bridle
v.10             | Psalm       | 34      | 19                 | 22: The contrast between the sorrows of the wicked and God's faithfulness to those who trust Him

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.1              | KJV         | "transgression" (both KJV/ESV): Hebrew pesha means rebellion, willful defiance — the strongest of the three sin words used. "Forgiven" is nasa — literally "lifted up and carried away," the same root used for the scapegoat carrying sins into the wilderness (Lev. 16:22).
v.2              | KJV         | "imputeth not" (KJV) / "counts no" (ESV): Hebrew lo chashab — "does not reckon, calculate, or charge to one's account." This is bookkeeping language — the debt is not entered in the ledger. Paul seizes on this accounting metaphor in Romans 4.
v.4              | KJV         | "my moisture is turned into the drought of summer" (KJV) / "my strength was dried up as by the heat of summer" (ESV): Hebrew leshad can mean "moisture," "sap," or "vitality." KJV preserves the more literal "moisture" while ESV interprets as "strength." The image is of a tree losing its life-giving sap under scorching heat.
v.6              | KJV         | "in a time when thou mayest be found" (KJV) / "at a time when you may be found" (ESV): Hebrew le'et metso — "in the time of finding." This suggests there are seasons of divine accessibility, moments of grace that should not be presumed upon or delayed.
v.9              | KJV         | "whose mouth must be held in with bit and bridle, lest they come near unto thee" (KJV) / "which must be curbed with bit and bridle, or it will not stay near you" (ESV): The KJV and ESV offer opposite interpretations — KJV suggests the bit keeps the animal from approaching, while ESV suggests the bit keeps it near. The Hebrew is genuinely ambiguous; the point is that God desires willing obedience rather than forced compliance.

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. David's three words for sin (rebellion, missing the mark, twisted
   iniquity) remind us that sin takes many forms — not only outright
   defiance but subtle drifting and distorted thinking. Which form of sin do
   you most struggle with? Do you tend to minimize certain sins because they
   seem less dramatic than others?

2. The physical toll of unconfessed sin (vv.3-4) is a reality many believers
   experience — sleeplessness, anxiety, exhaustion, even physical illness
   under the weight of hidden guilt. Is there any area where God's hand
   feels "heavy" upon you, pressing you toward confession?

3. Confession brought immediate forgiveness (v.5). God does not make us earn
   our way back or serve a probation period. How does this challenge a
   works-based mentality about restoring fellowship with God? Do you believe
   forgiveness is truly instantaneous upon genuine confession?

4. The contrast between verse 9's stubborn animal (requiring force) and
   verse 8's promise of gentle guidance ("I will guide thee with mine eye")
   presents a choice: will you be led by a glance from God's eye or dragged
   by bit and bridle? What does responsive obedience look like in your
   current season?

5. This psalm moves from isolation in guilt (vv.3-4) to community in praise
   (v.11). Sin isolates; forgiveness restores us to joyful fellowship.
   Consider: is unconfessed sin keeping you from full participation in the
   body of Christ?

RESOURCES
------------------------------------
The Bible Project — Psalms Overview:
  https://bibleproject.com/explore/video/psalms/

David Guzik — Psalms 32 Written Commentary:
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/psalms-32/

BibleProject Podcast:
  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

========================================
Study Notes generated for personal Bible study use.
Translations referenced: KJV, ESV, ASV, NET, WEB
Commentary sources: Enduring Word (enduringword.com), Charles
Spurgeon (Public Domain)
