40.ESV:
1. I waited patiently for the LORD; he inclined to me and heard my cry.
2. He drew me up from the pit of destruction, out of the miry bog, and set my feet upon a rock, making my steps secure.
3. He put a new song in my mouth, a song of praise to our God. Many will see and fear, and put their trust in the LORD.
4. Blessed is the man who makes the LORD his trust, who does not turn to the proud, to those who go astray after a lie!
5. You have multiplied, O LORD my God, your wondrous deeds and your thoughts toward us; none can compare with you! I will proclaim and tell of them, yet they are more than can be told.
6. In sacrifice and offering you have not delighted, but you have given me an open ear. Burnt offering and sin offering you have not required.
7. Then I said, “Behold, I have come; in the scroll of the book it is written of me:
8. I delight to do your will, O my God; your law is within my heart.”
9. I have told the glad news of deliverance in the great congregation; behold, I have not restrained my lips, as you know, O LORD.
10. I have not hidden your deliverance within my heart; I have spoken of your faithfulness and your salvation; I have not concealed your steadfast love and your faithfulness from the great congregation.
11. As for you, O LORD, you will not restrain your mercy from me; your steadfast love and your faithfulness will ever preserve me!
12. For evils have encompassed me beyond number; my iniquities have overtaken me, and I cannot see; they are more than the hairs of my head; my heart fails me.
13. Be pleased, O LORD, to deliver me! O LORD, make haste to help me!
14. Let those be put to shame and disappointed altogether who seek to snatch away my life; let those be turned back and brought to dishonor who delight in my hurt!
15. Let those be appalled because of their shame who say to me, “Aha, Aha!”
16. But may all who seek you rejoice and be glad in you; may those who love your salvation say continually, “Great is the LORD!”
17. As for me, I am poor and needy, but the Lord takes thought for me. You are my help and my deliverer; do not delay, O my God!

40.notes:
Psalms - Chapter 40
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
A thanksgiving and lament psalm — David recalls God lifting him from the pit and putting a new song in his mouth; sacrifice is not God's desire but obedience ('in the volume of the book it is written of me'); yet troubles surround him.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Multiple authors — primarily David (73 psalms attributed),
plus Asaph (12), Sons of Korah (11), Solomon (2), Moses (1),
Heman (1), Ethan (1), and anonymous. The Psalter was compiled over
approximately 1000 years and served as Israel's hymnal and prayer
book. The book is divided into five 'books' (1-41, 42-72, 73-89,
90-106, 107-150), paralleling the five books of Moses. Key themes:
worship, lament, praise, trust, kingship, creation, wisdom,
Messianic prophecy, and the full range of human emotion brought
before God.

Classification: Thanksgiving Psalm / Messianic Psalm
Attributed Author: David ("To the chief Musician, A Psalm of
David")
Key Themes: Patient waiting for God, deliverance from the pit,
the new song, sacrifice versus obedience, Messianic prophecy
(Christ's incarnation and will), delight in God's will

Historical Context: Psalm 40 is one of the most significant
Messianic psalms in the Psalter. While it begins as David's
personal testimony of deliverance, verses 6-8 transcend David's
experience and are directly applied to Christ in Hebrews 10:5-7,
where the author identifies these words as spoken by the Son of
God at His incarnation: "Lo, I come...to do thy will, O God."

The psalm has a two-part structure that puzzled ancient
interpreters. The first half (vv.1-10) is confident thanksgiving
and testimony, while the second half (vv.11-17) is urgent lament
and plea for help. This has led some scholars to suggest two
originally separate psalms were joined (note that vv.13-17 appear
independently as Psalm 70). However, the structure also reflects
authentic spiritual experience: past deliverance does not prevent
present crisis, and the memory of God's faithfulness becomes the
foundation for new prayers.

The Messianic content is remarkable. The phrase "mine ears hast
thou opened" (v.6, KJV) is rendered in the Septuagint (and
Hebrews 10:5) as "a body hast thou prepared me" — pointing to
the incarnation. "In the volume of the book it is written of me"
(v.7) suggests all Scripture testifies to Christ. "I delight to
do thy will" (v.8) describes perfect obedience that no mere human
has achieved — fulfilled only in Christ.

Structure:
- Testimony of Patient Waiting and Deliverance (vv. 1-3)
- The Blessedness of Trust (vv. 4-5)
- Sacrifice and Obedience: The Messianic Declaration (vv. 6-8)
- Proclamation in the Great Congregation (vv. 9-10)
- Plea for Continued Mercy (v. 11)
- Present Distress and Urgent Cry (vv. 12-17)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.1-2            | Psalm       | 27      | 14                 | ; Psalm 130:5-6: The theology of waiting — God's timing requires patient endurance, but His response is certain
v.2              | Psalm       | 69      | 2,14               | : Deliverance from miry clay/deep waters — Psalm 69 uses the same imagery from a position still in the pit, while Psalm 40 testifies from solid ground
v.3              | Psalm       | 33      | 3                  | ; Psalm 96:1; Revelation 5:9: The "new song" motif — fresh praise arising from fresh deliverance, culminating in the new song of the redeemed in heaven
v.6-8            | Hebrews     | 10      | 5                  | 10: The most important cross-reference. The author of Hebrews directly attributes these words to Christ at His incarnation, interpreting them as explaining why animal sacrifices are superseded by Christ's perfect obedience
v.6              | 1 Samuel    | 15      | 22                 | ; Hosea 6:6; Micah 6:6-8: The prophetic tradition of obedience over sacrifice — God desires heart compliance, not ritual performance
v.6              | Exodus      | 21      | 5                  | 6: The willing servant whose ear is pierced — a possible background to "mine ears hast thou opened," suggesting voluntary, permanent, joyful servitude
v.7              | Luke        | 24      | 27,44              | ; John 5:39: "In the volume of the book it is written of me" — Christ's own claim that all Scripture testifies about Him
v.13-17          | Psalm       | 70      | 1                  | 5: These verses appear as an independent psalm (Psalm 70), suggesting they circulated separately as an urgent prayer for help

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.2              | KJV         | "an horrible pit" (KJV) / "the pit of destruction" (ESV): Hebrew bor sha'on — bor means pit, cistern, or dungeon; sha'on means destruction, roaring, or tumult. KJV's "horrible" captures the terror; ESV's "destruction" identifies the pit's nature. The combination suggests a cistern of chaos — a place of drowning destruction.
v.6              | KJV         | "mine ears hast thou opened" (KJV) / "you have given me an open ear" (ESV): Hebrew oznayim karita li — literally "ears you have dug/pierced for me." This is the most theologically significant translation issue in the psalm. The Septuagint renders this as "a body you have prepared for me" (soma katartiso moi), which is the version quoted in Hebrews 10:5. The Hebrew likely refers to ears being "dug out" (created/opened) for hearing and obedience. The Septuagint's broader "body" may be an interpretive expansion — ears as representative of the whole body prepared for obedient service.
v.7              | KJV         | "in the volume of the book" (KJV) / "in the scroll of the book" (ESV): Hebrew bimgillat-sepher — a scroll (megillah) of a book/document. This refers to what is written about the speaker in God's authoritative record — whether the Torah, the broader Scripture, or the heavenly book of God's decree. Christ claimed all Scripture testified of Him.
v.12             | KJV         | "mine iniquities have taken hold upon me, so that I am not able to look up" (KJV) / "my iniquities have overtaken me, and I cannot see" (ESV): A puzzle in Messianic interpretation. How can Christ, who is sinless, speak of "mine iniquities"? The answer lies in substitutionary atonement — Christ bore our sins as His own (2 Corinthians 5:21, Isaiah 53:6). The shift from v.8's perfect obedience to v.12's sin-bearing mirrors the movement from incarnation to crucifixion.
v.17             | KJV         | "the Lord thinketh upon me" (KJV) / "the Lord takes thought for me" (ESV): Hebrew yachashav li — "He thinks/plans toward me." The same verb (chashab) used for "imputing" sin in Psalm 32:2 is here used positively — God "reckons" the poor and needy as worthy of attention. He actively plans on their behalf.

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. "I waited patiently for the LORD" (v.1) — the Hebrew doubles the verb for
   emphasis. Waiting is not passive idleness but active, expectant
   endurance. David's waiting was rewarded with complete transformation:
   from pit to rock, from sinking to standing. What are you currently
   waiting for? Can you wait with expectation rather than resignation?

2. Your testimony of deliverance becomes others' invitation to faith (v.3:
   "many shall see it, and fear, and shall trust"). Your story is not just
   for you. The pit God pulled you from and the rock He placed you on is
   meant to be proclaimed. Are you sharing your "new song" with others, or
   keeping your testimony private?

3. Verses 6-8 reveal that God's ultimate desire is not religious activity
   but heart obedience. You can perform every spiritual discipline and still
   miss what God wants most: willing, delighted submission to His will. Is
   your relationship with God characterized by duty or delight? Is His law
   "within your heart" (v.8) or merely on your schedule?

4. The psalm's two halves (thanksgiving in vv.1-10, lament in vv.11-17)
   reflect real spiritual life. Past deliverance does not immunize against
   future crisis. The memory of God's faithfulness does not prevent new
   problems, but it provides a foundation for new faith. Can you hold
   gratitude for past rescue and honesty about present struggle
   simultaneously?

5. "I am poor and needy; yet the Lord thinketh upon me" (v.17). You do not
   need to be impressive, self-sufficient, or spiritually advanced for God
   to attend to you. He thinks upon the poor and needy — those who know they
   have nothing to bring. Your insufficiency is not a barrier to God's
   attention; it is the very thing that draws it.

RESOURCES
------------------------------------
The Bible Project — Psalms Overview:
  https://bibleproject.com/explore/video/psalms/

David Guzik — Psalms 40 Written Commentary:
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/psalms-40/

BibleProject Podcast:
  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

========================================
Study Notes generated for personal Bible study use.
Translations referenced: KJV, ESV, ASV, NET, WEB
Commentary sources: Enduring Word (enduringword.com), Charles
Spurgeon (Public Domain)

41.ESV:
1. Blessed is the one who considers the poor! In the day of trouble the LORD delivers him;
2. the LORD protects him and keeps him alive; he is called blessed in the land; you do not give him up to the will of his enemies.
3. The LORD sustains him on his sickbed; in his illness you restore him to full health.
4. As for me, I said, “O LORD, be gracious to me; heal me, for I have sinned against you!”
5. My enemies say of me in malice, “When will he die, and his name perish?”
6. And when one comes to see me, he utters empty words, while his heart gathers iniquity; when he goes out, he tells it abroad.
7. All who hate me whisper together about me; they imagine the worst for me.
8. They say, “A deadly thing is poured out on him; he will not rise again from where he lies.”
9. Even my close friend in whom I trusted, who ate my bread, has lifted his heel against me.
10. But you, O LORD, be gracious to me, and raise me up, that I may repay them!
11. By this I know that you delight in me: my enemy will not shout in triumph over me.
12. But you have upheld me because of my integrity, and set me in your presence forever.
13. Blessed be the LORD, the God of Israel, from everlasting to everlasting! Amen and Amen.

4. I said, LORD, be merciful unto me: heal my soul; for I have sinned against thee.
5. Mine enemies speak evil of me, When shall he die, and his name perish?
6. And if he come to see me, he speaketh vanity: his heart gathereth iniquity to itself; when he goeth abroad, he telleth it.
7. All that hate me whisper together against me: against me do they devise my hurt.

9. Yea, mine own familiar friend, in whom I trusted, which did eat of my bread, hath lifted up his heel against me.
10. But thou, O LORD, be merciful unto me, and raise me up, that I may requite them.
11. By this I know that thou favourest me, because mine enemy doth not triumph over me.
12. And as for me, thou upholdest me in mine integrity, and settest me before thy face for ever.
13. Blessed be the LORD God of Israel from everlasting, and to everlasting. Amen, and Amen.

41.notes:
Psalms - Chapter 41
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
A lament and trust psalm closing Book I — blessed is he who considers the poor; David's enemies wish him dead, and even his close friend (prophetic of Judas) has lifted his heel against him, yet the LORD upholds him.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Multiple authors — primarily David (73 psalms attributed),
plus Asaph (12), Sons of Korah (11), Solomon (2), Moses (1),
Heman (1), Ethan (1), and anonymous. The Psalter was compiled over
approximately 1000 years and served as Israel's hymnal and prayer
book. The book is divided into five 'books' (1-41, 42-72, 73-89,
90-106, 107-150), paralleling the five books of Moses. Key themes:
worship, lament, praise, trust, kingship, creation, wisdom,
Messianic prophecy, and the full range of human emotion brought
before God.

Classification: Individual Lament/Thanksgiving Psalm
Attributed Author: David (To the chief Musician, A Psalm of David)
Key Themes: Compassion for the poor, betrayal by a friend, God's
faithfulness, healing, Messianic prophecy

Historical Context: Psalm 41 is the final psalm of Book I of the
Psalter (Psalms 1-41). David writes during a period of illness and
personal distress, when enemies surround him and even a trusted
friend has turned against him. Many commentators connect this to
the period of Absalom's rebellion, when Ahithophel — David's
trusted counselor who ate at his table — betrayed him (2 Samuel
15-17).

The psalm holds profound Messianic significance. Jesus directly
quoted verse 9 in John 13:18, applying it to Judas Iscariot's
betrayal: "He that eateth bread with me hath lifted up his heel
against me." This makes Psalm 41 one of the clearly identified
Messianic psalms in the New Testament, connecting David's
experience of betrayal to Christ's greater suffering.

As the closing psalm of Book I, verse 13 serves as a doxology
("Blessed be the LORD God of Israel from everlasting, and to
everlasting. Amen, and Amen.") Each of the five books of the
Psalter concludes with such a doxology (41:13; 72:18-19; 89:52;
106:48; 150:6). This literary structure reinforces the Psalter's
identity as a unified work of worship.

Structure:
- The Blessedness of Compassion (vv. 1-3)
- David's Confession and Plea (v. 4)
- The Malice of Enemies (vv. 5-8)
- The Betrayal of a Friend (v. 9)
- Prayer for Vindication (vv. 10-12)
- Doxology Closing Book I (v. 13)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.1              | Proverbs    | 19      | 17                 | "He that hath pity upon the poor lendeth unto the LORD; and that which he hath given will he pay him again." God rewards compassion for the needy
v.4              | Psalm       | 6       | 2                  | "Have mercy upon me, O LORD; for I am weak: O LORD, heal me; for my bones are vexed." David's repeated pattern of crying out for mercy in sickness
v.9              | John        | 13      | 18                 | "I speak not of you all: I know whom I have chosen: but that the scripture might be fulfilled, He that eateth bread with me hath lifted up his heel against me." Jesus identifies this verse as Messianic prophecy fulfilled in Judas
v.9              | 2 Samuel    | 15      | 12                 | Ahithophel's betrayal of David parallels Judas's betrayal of Christ, both trusted companions who turned traitor
v.11             | Psalm       | 35      | 19                 | "Let not them that are mine enemies wrongfully rejoice over me." The righteous trust that God will not allow final triumph to the wicked
v.12             | Psalm       | 25      | 21                 | "Let integrity and uprightness preserve me; for I wait on thee." Integrity as a hallmark of those God sustains
v.13             | Psalm       | 72      | 18-19              | The doxology closing Book II, showing the pattern of praise concluding each division of the Psalter
v.13             | 1 Chronicles | 16      | 36                 | "Blessed be the LORD God of Israel for ever and ever. And all the people said, Amen, and praised the LORD." The same doxological formula used in corporate worship

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.1              | KJV         | "considereth the poor" vs. ESV "considers the poor" — The Hebrew "maskil" (שָׂכַל) means to act wisely, to give thoughtful attention to. Both translations capture this, but the Hebrew emphasizes prudent, discerning compassion rather than mere pity. The word "poor" (dal) refers to the weak, thin, or helpless.
v.3              | KJV         | "thou wilt make all his bed in his sickness" vs. ESV "in his illness you restore him to full health" — The KJV preserves the vivid Hebrew metaphor of God literally turning or rearranging the sick person's bed (transforming a bed of illness into a bed of comfort). The ESV interprets the meaning more dynamically.
v.8              | KJV         | "An evil disease" vs. ESV "A deadly thing" — The Hebrew "debar beliyyaal" literally means "a thing of Belial" or "a worthless/wicked thing." KJV interprets this as a specific disease; ESV renders it more generally as something deadly or destructive poured upon him.
v.9              | KJV         | "mine own familiar friend" vs. ESV "my close friend" — The Hebrew phrase "ish shelomi" literally means "man of my peace" — one with whom David had a covenant of peace. Both translations capture the intimacy, but the Hebrew carries overtones of a formally established peaceful relationship.
v.12             | KJV         | "settest me before thy face for ever" vs. ESV "set me in your presence forever" — Nearly identical. The Hebrew "paneh" (face) conveys intimate, personal access to God — being in His direct sight and favor perpetually.

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. Compassion brings divine protection. The psalm opens with a promise:
   those who thoughtfully consider the poor and weak will find God
   considering them in their time of trouble. How am I actively, wisely
   caring for those in need around me?

2. Honest confession accompanies faithful prayer. David does not hide his
   sin from God (v. 4: "heal my soul; for I have sinned against thee").
   Authentic prayer requires acknowledging our own failures even while
   crying out against injustice done to us.

3. Betrayal by close friends is among the deepest wounds. David's pain — and
   ultimately Christ's — teaches us that intimate betrayal is not outside
   God's sovereign plan. If we experience such treachery, we are in the
   company of David and of Christ Himself.

4. God's final word is praise, not pain. Book I of the Psalms contains many
   laments, yet it concludes with a doxology. No matter how deep the valley,
   the trajectory of the faithful life bends toward eternal praise. Our
   present sufferings are not the final chapter.

5. Integrity does not prevent suffering but sustains us through it. David
   was upheld "in mine integrity" (v. 12) — not exempted from trial but
   preserved through it. Walking uprightly is not a guarantee against
   hardship but a foundation that holds firm when hardship comes.

------------------------------------

  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/psalms-41/

42.notes:
Psalms - Chapter 42
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
A lament of the Sons of Korah — 'As the hart panteth after the water brooks, so panteth my soul after thee, O God'; the psalmist, exiled from the temple, combats despair with the refrain: 'Why art thou cast down, O my soul? Hope thou in God.'

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Multiple authors — primarily David (73 psalms attributed),
plus Asaph (12), Sons of Korah (11), Solomon (2), Moses (1),
Heman (1), Ethan (1), and anonymous. The Psalter was compiled over
approximately 1000 years and served as Israel's hymnal and prayer
book. The book is divided into five 'books' (1-41, 42-72, 73-89,
90-106, 107-150), paralleling the five books of Moses. Key themes:
worship, lament, praise, trust, kingship, creation, wisdom,
Messianic prophecy, and the full range of human emotion brought
before God.

Classification: Individual Lament
Attributed Author: Sons of Korah (To the chief Musician, Maschil,
for the sons of Korah)
Key Themes: Spiritual thirst, longing for God's presence, despair
and hope, memory of worship, self-counsel

Historical Context: Psalm 42 opens Book II of the Psalter (Psalms
42-72) and is the first psalm attributed to the Sons of Korah — a
Levitical family of temple musicians descended from the rebel Korah
(Numbers 16), whose sons were spared by God's grace. This psalm is
universally recognized as paired with Psalm 43, likely originally
a single composition sharing the same refrain (42:5, 42:11, 43:5).

The psalmist is geographically distant from the temple, apparently
in the region of the Jordan headwaters, near Mount Hermon and
"the hill Mizar" (v. 6) — far north of Jerusalem. He remembers
leading worship processions to the house of God and now weeps in
exile, taunted by enemies who mock, "Where is thy God?" The
specific mention of geographic locations suggests a historical
exile — possibly during David's flight from Absalom (2 Samuel
15-17) or during the northern exile of Levitical worshipers.

This psalm is among the most emotionally transparent in Scripture.
It models how a believer wrestles with depression while refusing to
abandon faith. The psalmist speaks to his own soul, commanding it
to hope in God even when feelings fail. The imagery of the
thirsting deer has become one of the most beloved metaphors in
Christian worship and hymnody.

Structure:
- Desperate Longing for God (vv. 1-2)
- Tears and Taunting (v. 3)
- Memory of Past Worship (v. 4)
- First Refrain: Self-Counsel to Hope (v. 5)
- Cast Down but Remembering God (vv. 6-7)
- God's Lovingkindness and Prayer (v. 8)
- Complaint: Why Have You Forgotten Me? (vv. 9-10)
- Second Refrain: Self-Counsel to Hope (v. 11)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.1              | Psalm       | 63      | 1                  | "O God, thou art my God; early will I seek thee: my soul thirsteth for thee, my flesh longeth for thee in a dry and thirsty land." David's similar cry of spiritual thirst
v.1              | John        | 7       | 37                 | "If any man thirst, let him come unto me, and drink." Jesus offers Himself as the fulfillment of spiritual thirst
v.3              | Psalm       | 80      | 5                  | "Thou feedest them with the bread of tears; and givest them tears to drink in great measure." Tears as sustenance during sorrow
v.5              | Psalm       | 43      | 5                  | The identical refrain, confirming these psalms were composed as one unit
v.7              | Jonah       | 2       | 3                  | "All thy billows and thy waves passed over me." Jonah uses nearly identical language from the belly of the great fish — the experience of being overwhelmed by God's waves
v.7              | Psalm       | 69      | 1-2                | "Save me, O God; for the waters are come in unto my soul." The imagery of drowning in sorrow recurs throughout the Psalms
v.8              | Psalm       | 32      | 7                  | "Thou shalt compass me about with songs of deliverance." God surrounds the afflicted with His song even in trouble
v.10             | Psalm       | 22      | 1                  | "My God, my God, why hast thou forsaken me?" The pattern of crying out to God in perceived abandonment — most fully expressed by Christ on the cross

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.1              | KJV         | "hart" vs. ESV "deer" — The Hebrew "ayyal" (אַיָּל) refers specifically to a male deer or stag. KJV's archaic "hart" preserves this masculine specificity, while ESV uses the more general modern term. The intensity of panting (arag) suggests the animal is in extremity.
v.4              | KJV         | "I had gone with the multitude" vs. ESV "how I would go with the throng" — The Hebrew is difficult here. KJV renders it as a straightforward past tense memory. ESV captures the habitual aspect ("I would go") suggesting a repeated pattern of joyful worship now lost.
v.5              | KJV         | "the help of his countenance" vs. ESV "my salvation" — The Hebrew is debated. KJV follows the Masoretic text more literally: "the salvations of his face/countenance." ESV simplifies for readability. The Hebrew connects salvation directly to seeing God's face — being in His presence is itself the deliverance.
v.7              | KJV         | "waterspouts" vs. ESV "waterfalls" — The Hebrew "tsinnor" is rare and debated. It may refer to waterfalls, cataracts, water channels, or conduits. Given the northern geographic setting near Hermon's streams, "waterfalls" (ESV) fits the landscape, while "waterspouts" (KJV) evokes more violent water imagery.
v.11             | KJV         | "the health of my countenance" vs. ESV "my salvation and my God" — KJV's rendering captures the Hebrew idea that God's saving presence literally shows on one's face — He is the health (healing, wholeness) that restores the downcast countenance.

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. Spiritual thirst is not a sign of failure but of life. Only living souls
   thirst. The psalmist's desperate longing for God reveals a heart that
   knows what it needs. When we feel spiritual dryness, it may be evidence
   of genuine faith recognizing its need.

2. Preaching to yourself is a spiritual discipline. The refrain teaches us
   to speak truth to our own souls rather than passively listening to our
   emotions. When cast down, we can ask "Why?" and then command ourselves
   toward hope — not denying the pain but redirecting the soul toward God's
   faithfulness.

3. Remembering past faithfulness sustains present faith. The psalmist
   recalls leading worship processions (v. 4) and uses those memories as
   anchors. Keeping a record of God's past dealings with us provides fuel
   for hope in present darkness.

4. Tears and faith coexist. The psalmist weeps day and night (v. 3) while
   simultaneously affirming God's lovingkindness (v. 8). Biblical faith does
   not suppress grief — it grieves honestly while holding to God's
   character.

5. Community matters. Part of the psalmist's anguish is isolation from
   corporate worship (v. 4). We were not designed to walk through spiritual
   valleys alone. The absence of gathered worship deepens spiritual pain;
   its restoration brings healing.

------------------------------------

  https://bibleproject.com/explore/video/psalms/

David Guzik — Psalms 42 Written Commentary:
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/psalms-42/

BibleProject Podcast:
  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

5. Why art thou cast down, O my soul? and why art thou disquieted within me? hope in God: for I shall yet praise him, who is the health of my countenance, and my God.

2. For thou art the God of my strength; why hast thou cast me off? Why go I mourning because of the oppression of the enemy?

4. Then will I go unto the altar of God, Unto God my exceeding joy; And upon the harp will I praise thee, O God, my God.
5. Why art thou cast down, O my soul? And why art thou disquieted within me? Hope thou in God; for I shall yet praise him, [Who is] the help of my countenance, and my God. Psalm 44 For the Chief Musician. [A Psalm] of the sons of Korah. Maschil.
43.NET:
1. Vindicate me, O God! Fight for me against an ungodly nation. Deliver me from deceitful and evil men.
2. For you are the God who shelters me. Why do you reject me? Why must I walk around mourning because my enemies oppress me?

43.notes:
Psalms - Chapter 43
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
A lament continuation of Psalm 42 — the psalmist pleads for God to send His light and truth to lead him back to the holy hill and the altar of God, repeating the refrain: 'Hope in God, for I shall yet praise him.'

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Multiple authors — primarily David (73 psalms attributed),
plus Asaph (12), Sons of Korah (11), Solomon (2), Moses (1),
Heman (1), Ethan (1), and anonymous. The Psalter was compiled over
approximately 1000 years and served as Israel's hymnal and prayer
book. The book is divided into five 'books' (1-41, 42-72, 73-89,
90-106, 107-150), paralleling the five books of Moses. Key themes:
worship, lament, praise, trust, kingship, creation, wisdom,
Messianic prophecy, and the full range of human emotion brought
before God.

Classification: Individual Lament
Attributed Author: Anonymous (no superscription — likely a
continuation of Psalm 42, attributed to the Sons of Korah)
Key Themes: Prayer for vindication, light and truth as guides,
longing for worship, hope in God

Historical Context: Psalm 43 is one of the few psalms in Book II
without a superscription, strongly suggesting it was originally the
third stanza of Psalm 42. Both psalms share identical vocabulary,
theme, tone, and the same refrain ("Why art thou cast down, O my
soul?"). Ancient manuscripts including some Hebrew texts and the
Septuagint treat them as a single composition. The refrain appears
three times across the combined work (42:5, 42:11, 43:5), creating
a perfect tripartite structure.

While Psalm 42 expressed the despair of exile and spiritual
thirst, Psalm 43 moves toward resolution. The psalmist shifts from
lament to active petition — requesting that God send His light and
truth to guide him back to the holy hill and the altar. This is
the turning point of the combined psalm: from looking backward in
grief to looking forward in faith.

The prayer for vindication against an "ungodly nation" and
"deceitful and unjust man" suggests a specific adversary, possibly
connected to David's exile during Absalom's rebellion or to the
Levitical singers' displacement from their temple ministry. The
psalm demonstrates that honest complaint before God naturally leads
to renewed petition and restored hope.

Structure:
- Prayer for Vindication and Deliverance (vv. 1-2)
- Request for Light and Truth as Guides (v. 3)
- Anticipation of Restored Worship (v. 4)
- Third Refrain: Self-Counsel to Hope (v. 5)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.1              | Psalm       | 26      | 1                  | "Judge me, O LORD; for I have walked in mine integrity." The same bold appeal to God's righteous judgment
v.1              | Psalm       | 35      | 1                  | "Plead my cause, O LORD, with them that strive with me." God as advocate pleading the believer's legal case
v.2              | Psalm       | 42      | 9                  | "I will say unto God my rock, Why hast thou forgotten me?" The recurring question of perceived divine rejection bridges both psalms
v.3              | John        | 8       | 12                 | "I am the light of the world: he that followeth me shall not walk in darkness." Christ as the ultimate fulfillment of divine light leading God's people
v.3              | John        | 14      | 6                  | "I am the way, the truth, and the life." Jesus embodies the truth that guides believers to the Father's presence
v.3              | Psalm       | 27      | 4                  | "One thing have I desired of the LORD, that will I seek after; that I may dwell in the house of the LORD all the days of my life." The singular desire to be in God's presence
v.4              | Psalm       | 84      | 1-2                | "How amiable are thy tabernacles, O LORD of hosts! My soul longeth, yea, even fainteth for the courts of the LORD." Intense longing for temple worship
v.5              | Lamentations | 3       | 24                 | "The LORD is my portion, saith my soul; therefore will I hope in him." Self-counsel to hope based on God as one's inheritance

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.1              | KJV         | "Judge me" vs. ESV "Vindicate me" — The Hebrew "shaphat" (שָׁפַט) means to judge, govern, or render a verdict. KJV preserves the literal meaning; ESV interprets the expected outcome (vindication). The ESV choice clarifies that the psalmist is not asking for condemnation but for God to rule in his favor.
v.1              | KJV         | "ungodly nation" vs. ESV "ungodly people" — The Hebrew "goy lo chasid" literally means "a nation not faithful/loyal." KJV uses "nation" (closer to the Hebrew "goy"); ESV uses "people." The term may refer to a foreign nation or to faithless people within Israel.
v.2              | KJV         | "the God of my strength" vs. ESV "the God in whom I take refuge" — The Hebrew "elohey mauzzi" means "God of my fortress/stronghold." KJV emphasizes the strength aspect; ESV emphasizes the refuge/shelter aspect. Both are valid renderings of the fortress metaphor.
v.4              | KJV         | "God my exceeding joy" vs. ESV "God my exceeding joy" — Both translations agree. The Hebrew "el simchat gili" is an intensive construction: literally "God, the gladness of my exultation/rejoicing." God is not just a source of joy but joy at its most intense and overflowing.

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. When lost in darkness, pray for light and truth. The psalmist does not
   try to find his own way back — he asks God to send guides. In spiritual
   confusion, the answer is not self-help but divine revelation. God's Word
   (light) and God's character (truth) lead us home.

2. Honest prayer includes bold requests. "Judge me" is not timid. The
   psalmist asks God to take up his cause, to act as his advocate. We can
   bring our grievances to God with confidence when our cause is just,
   trusting His righteous judgment.

3. The goal of deliverance is worship. The psalmist doesn't merely want to
   escape his enemies — he wants to reach the altar (v. 4). True spiritual
   health is measured not by the absence of trouble but by the presence of
   worship. Our petitions should aim toward restored communion with God.

4. Depression has a trajectory toward hope. The three-fold refrain across
   Psalms 42-43 shows movement. The psalmist does not immediately feel
   better, but he persistently speaks truth to his soul. Healing from
   spiritual depression is often gradual, not instantaneous — and
   persistence in hope is itself an act of faith.

5. "My God" — the possessive pronoun matters. The psalm ends with the deeply
   personal claim: "my God" (v. 5). In the midst of feeling abandoned, the
   psalmist still claims God as his own. Maintaining personal trust even
   when God seems distant is the essence of persevering faith.

------------------------------------

David Guzik — Psalms 43 Written Commentary:
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/psalms-43/

44.notes:
Psalms - Chapter 44
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
A communal lament — the people recall God's past victories but now feel rejected, scattered, and slaughtered like sheep though they have not been unfaithful; they plead for God to awake and redeem them.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Multiple authors — primarily David (73 psalms attributed),
plus Asaph (12), Sons of Korah (11), Solomon (2), Moses (1),
Heman (1), Ethan (1), and anonymous. The Psalter was compiled over
approximately 1000 years and served as Israel's hymnal and prayer
book. The book is divided into five 'books' (1-41, 42-72, 73-89,
90-106, 107-150), paralleling the five books of Moses. Key themes:
worship, lament, praise, trust, kingship, creation, wisdom,
Messianic prophecy, and the full range of human emotion brought
before God.

Classification: Communal Lament
Attributed Author: Sons of Korah (To the chief Musician for the
sons of Korah, Maschil)
Key Themes: National suffering, innocent suffering, faithfulness
despite defeat, bold appeal to God, martyrdom

Historical Context: Psalm 44 is one of the most theologically
challenging psalms in the Psalter — a communal lament in which the
nation protests its suffering despite covenant faithfulness. Unlike
psalms where suffering is attributed to sin (e.g., Psalm 38), here
the people explicitly claim innocence: "All this is come upon us;
yet have we not forgotten thee, neither have we dealt falsely in
thy covenant" (v. 17). This creates a profound theological tension
— the righteous suffering without apparent cause.

The historical occasion is debated. Some connect it to military
defeats during the divided monarchy, the Babylonian exile, or the
Maccabean period. The psalm fits any situation where God's people
experience national defeat and persecution despite faithfulness.
Its universal quality explains why Paul quotes verse 22 in Romans
8:36: "For thy sake are we killed all the day long; we are counted
as sheep for the slaughter."

Paul's use in Romans 8 is transformative. He quotes this verse not
as a complaint but as a backdrop for triumph: "Nay, in all these
things we are more than conquerors through him that loved us"
(Romans 8:37). What the psalmist experienced as unanswered lament,
the New Testament transforms into the context for ultimate victory.
This psalm thus bridges the theology of innocent suffering in the
Old Testament with the theology of redemptive suffering in Christ.

Structure:
- Remembering God's Past Mighty Acts (vv. 1-3)
- Confession of Trust in God Alone (vv. 4-8)
- Lament: God Has Rejected Us (vv. 9-16)
- Protestation of Innocence (vv. 17-22)
- Bold Plea for God to Awaken and Help (vv. 23-26)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.1              | Deuteronomy | 6       | 20-23              | The command to tell children what God did in Egypt. The psalm reflects this generational transmission of faith
v.3              | Joshua      | 24      | 12-13              | "I gave you a land for which ye did not labour, and cities which ye built not." God's conquest of Canaan was His work, not Israel's
v.11             | Deuteronomy | 28      | 64                 | "The LORD shall scatter thee among all people." The covenant curses threatened scattering, yet the psalmist claims covenant faithfulness
v.22             | Romans      | 8       | 36-37              | Paul quotes this verse and responds: "In all these things we are more than conquerors through him that loved us." The New Testament transforms lament into triumph
v.22             | Isaiah      | 53      | 7                  | "He is brought as a lamb to the slaughter." The Suffering Servant imagery connects innocent suffering to Messianic redemption
v.23             | Mark        | 4       | 38                 | "Master, carest thou not that we perish?" The disciples' cry to the sleeping Jesus echoes the psalmist's plea to the apparently sleeping God
v.23             | 1 Kings     | 18      | 27                 | Elijah's mockery of Baal: "perhaps he sleepeth." The psalmist uses similar language but toward the true God in desperate faith, not mockery
v.26             | Psalm       | 6       | 4                  | "Return, O LORD, deliver my soul: oh save me for thy mercies' sake." The appeal to God's covenant love (hesed) as the basis for rescue

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.2              | KJV         | "plantedst them" vs. ESV "them you planted" — Both translate the Hebrew "nata" (planted) identically in meaning. The metaphor is agricultural: God uprooted nations and planted Israel in the land like a vine (cf. Psalm 80:8).
v.11             | KJV         | "sheep appointed for meat" vs. ESV "sheep for slaughter" — The Hebrew "tson maakhal" means literally "sheep of food/eating." KJV's "meat" (archaic for "food") is more literal; ESV's "slaughter" interprets the purpose. Both convey the same horrifying image of God's people as helpless victims.
v.14             | KJV         | "a shaking of the head" vs. ESV "a laughingstock" — The Hebrew "manos rosh" literally means "a shaking/wagging of the head" — a gesture of contempt and mockery. KJV preserves the physical gesture; ESV gives the interpretive meaning. The head- shaking recalls the mockers at Christ's cross (Matthew 27:39).
v.19             | KJV         | "the place of dragons" vs. ESV "the place of jackals" — The Hebrew "tannin" can mean sea monsters, serpents, or jackals depending on vocalization. ESV reads "tannim" (jackals) — desolate wilderness creatures. KJV's "dragons" reflects an older interpretation. The point is utter desolation and ruin.
v.26             | KJV         | "for thy mercies' sake" vs. ESV "for the sake of your steadfast love" — The Hebrew "hesed" (חֶסֶד) is one of the most theologically rich words in the Old Testament. KJV uses "mercies"; ESV uses "steadfast love." Hesed encompasses loyal love, covenant faithfulness, mercy, and lovingkindness — no single English word captures its fullness.

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. Faith does not guarantee freedom from suffering. This psalm shatters any
   prosperity gospel theology. The people were faithful, yet they suffered.
   Obedience to God sometimes makes us targets rather than protected. "For
   thy sake" (v. 22) — suffering because of God, not despite Him.

2. Honest lament is not unfaith. The psalmist asks God "Why?" and even tells
   God to "wake up." This raw honesty is preserved in inspired Scripture,
   validating our right to bring confusion and protest before God. The
   opposite of faith is not honest questioning but indifferent silence.

3. Remembering God's past acts anchors present hope. The psalm begins by
   recounting what God did for the fathers (vv. 1-3). Even when the present
   contradicts the past, rehearsing God's history builds a platform for
   renewed appeal.

4. Innocent suffering has redemptive purpose. Paul's use of verse 22 in
   Romans 8 reveals that what feels like meaningless slaughter is actually
   the context for being "more than conquerors." Our suffering for Christ's
   sake is never wasted, even when it feels senseless in the moment.

5. Corporate lament has a place in worship. This is not an individual's
   private complaint but a community's public cry. Churches need space for
   honest corporate grief — acknowledging together that things are not as
   they should be while together appealing to God's covenant love.

RESOURCES
------------------------------------
The Bible Project — Psalms Overview:
  https://bibleproject.com/explore/video/psalms/

David Guzik — Psalms 44 Written Commentary:
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/psalms-44/

BibleProject Podcast:
  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

Commentary sources: Enduring Word (enduringword.com), Charles
Spurgeon (Public Domain)

45.ESV:
1. My heart overflows with a pleasing theme; I address my verses to the king; my tongue is like the pen of a ready scribe.
2. You are the most handsome of the sons of men; grace is poured upon your lips; therefore God has blessed you forever.
3. Gird your sword on your thigh, O mighty one, in your splendor and majesty!
4. In your majesty ride out victoriously for the cause of truth and meekness and righteousness; let your right hand teach you awesome deeds!
5. Your arrows are sharp in the heart of the king’s enemies; the peoples fall under you.
6. Your throne, O God, is forever and ever. The scepter of your kingdom is a scepter of uprightness;
7. you have loved righteousness and hated wickedness. Therefore God, your God, has anointed you with the oil of gladness beyond your companions;
8. your robes are all fragrant with myrrh and aloes and cassia. From ivory palaces stringed instruments make you glad;
9. daughters of kings are among your ladies of honor; at your right hand stands the queen in gold of Ophir.
10. Hear, O daughter, and consider, and incline your ear: forget your people and your father’s house,
11. and the king will desire your beauty. Since he is your lord, bow to him.
12. The people of Tyre will seek your favor with gifts, the richest of the people.
13. All glorious is the princess in her chamber, with robes interwoven with gold.
14. In many-colored robes she is led to the king, with her virgin companions following behind her.
15. With joy and gladness they are led along as they enter the palace of the king.
16. In place of your fathers shall be your sons; you will make them princes in all the earth.
17. I will cause your name to be remembered in all generations; therefore nations will praise you forever and ever.

4. And in thy majesty ride prosperously because of truth and meekness and righteousness; and thy right hand shall teach thee terrible things.
5. Thine arrows are sharp in the heart of the king’s enemies; whereby the people fall under thee.
6. Thy throne, O God, is for ever and ever: the sceptre of thy kingdom is a right sceptre.
7. Thou lovest righteousness, and hatest wickedness: therefore God, thy God, hath anointed thee with the oil of gladness above thy fellows.
8. All thy garments smell of myrrh, and aloes, and cassia, out of the ivory palaces, whereby they have made thee glad.
9. Kings’ daughters were among thy honourable women: upon thy right hand did stand the queen in gold of Ophir.
10. Hearken, O daughter, and consider, and incline thine ear; forget also thine own people, and thy father’s house;

17. I will make thy name to be remembered in all generations: Therefore shall the peoples give thee thanks for ever and ever. Psalm 46 For the Chief Musician. [A Psalm] of the sons of Korah; set to Alamoth. A Song.

45.notes:
Psalms - Chapter 45
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
A royal wedding psalm (Messianic) — 'Thy throne, O God, is for ever and ever'; the king is praised for beauty and righteousness, the bride is told to forget her people and honor the king; applied to Christ in Hebrews 1.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Multiple authors — primarily David (73 psalms attributed),
plus Asaph (12), Sons of Korah (11), Solomon (2), Moses (1),
Heman (1), Ethan (1), and anonymous. The Psalter was compiled over
approximately 1000 years and served as Israel's hymnal and prayer
book. The book is divided into five 'books' (1-41, 42-72, 73-89,
90-106, 107-150), paralleling the five books of Moses. Key themes:
worship, lament, praise, trust, kingship, creation, wisdom,
Messianic prophecy, and the full range of human emotion brought
before God.

Classification: Royal/Wedding/Messianic Psalm
Attributed Author: Sons of Korah (To the chief Musician upon
Shoshannim, for the sons of Korah, Maschil, A Song of loves)
Key Themes: The King's beauty and glory, the royal bride, Messianic
kingship, divine throne, the marriage of Christ and His Church

Historical Context: Psalm 45 is unique in the Psalter — a royal
wedding song that transcends its immediate occasion to become one
of the most explicitly Messianic psalms in Scripture. The
superscription identifies it as "Shoshannim" (lilies — possibly a
tune name) and "A Song of loves" (a wedding song). The immediate
historical context may be Solomon's marriage to an Egyptian
princess or another royal wedding, but the language far exceeds
what can be said of any mere human king.

The key to the psalm's interpretation is verse 6: "Thy throne, O
God, is for ever and ever." The writer of Hebrews quotes this verse
(Hebrews 1:8-9) and applies it directly to Christ, using it as
proof of the Son's deity — God the Father addresses the Son as
"God." This makes Psalm 45 one of the clearest Old Testament
testimonies to the Messiah's divine nature. No earthly Israelite
king could legitimately be called "God" with an eternal throne.

The psalm divides into two portraits: the King (vv. 1-9) and the
Bride (vv. 10-15), followed by a promise of dynastic continuation
(vv. 16-17). In Christian interpretation, this pictures Christ and
His Church — the Bridegroom-King in His splendor and the Bride
called to leave her old life and give herself wholly to her Lord.
Ephesians 5:25-32 develops this marriage theology extensively.

Structure:
- The Poet's Introduction (v. 1)
- The King's Beauty and Grace (v. 2)
- The King as Warrior (vv. 3-5)
- The King's Eternal Throne (vv. 6-7)
- The King's Splendor and Court (vv. 8-9)
- The Bride's Call to Devotion (vv. 10-11)
- The Bride's Honor and Glory (vv. 12-15)
- Promise of Royal Posterity (vv. 16-17)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.2              | Song of Solomon | 5       | 10-16              | "My beloved is white and ruddy, the chiefest among ten thousand." The Bride describes her Bridegroom's beauty, echoing the psalmist's praise
v.6              | Hebrews     | 1       | 8-9                | "But unto the Son he saith, Thy throne, O God, is for ever and ever." The definitive New Testament application establishing Christ's deity
v.7              | Isaiah      | 61      | 1-3                | "The Spirit of the Lord GOD is upon me; because the LORD hath anointed me." The Messiah's anointing for His mission, fulfilled in Luke 4:18-21
v.7              | Acts        | 10      | 38                 | "God anointed Jesus of Nazareth with the Holy Ghost and with power." The fulfillment of the anointing above His fellows
v.8              | John        | 12      | 3                  | Mary anoints Jesus with costly spikenard, a fragrant act connecting to the King whose garments smell of myrrh and aloes
v.10-11          | Ephesians   | 5       | 25-27              | "Christ also loved the church, and gave himself for it... that he might present it to himself a glorious church." The marriage theology of King and Bride
v.10             | Ruth        | 1       | 16                 | "Thy people shall be my people, and thy God my God." Ruth's leaving her father's house and people to follow God models the bride's call in this psalm
v.17             | Isaiah      | 9       | 6-7                | "Of the increase of his government and peace there shall be no end." The eternal perpetuation of the Messianic King's name and reign

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.1              | KJV         | "My heart is inditing a good matter" vs. ESV "My heart overflows with a pleasing theme" — The Hebrew "rachash" (רָחַשׁ) means to bubble up, overflow, or stir. It occurs only here in the Old Testament. KJV's "inditing" (composing) is archaic; ESV's "overflows" captures the emotional intensity of inspired composition.
v.6              | KJV         | "Thy throne, O God" vs. ESV "Your throne, O God" — Both translate identically. The Hebrew "kise'aka elohim" has been debated: is it vocative ("O God") or descriptive ("your divine throne")? Hebrews 1:8 settles the matter for Christian interpretation: it is a direct address to the Son as God.
v.8              | KJV         | "whereby they have made thee glad" vs. ESV "stringed instruments make you glad" — The Hebrew "minni" is ambiguous. KJV connects it to the ivory palaces; ESV reads "minni" as an abbreviated form of "minnim" (stringed instruments). ESV's reading has strong lexical support and provides better parallelism.
v.9              | KJV         | "honourable women" vs. ESV "ladies of honor" — The Hebrew "yiqrot" means precious or honored ones. Both convey the same meaning — women of noble rank attending the King. The "queen" (shegal) at his right hand refers to the royal bride in her place of highest honor.
v.13             | KJV         | "The king's daughter is all glorious within" vs. ESV "All glorious is the princess in her chamber" — The Hebrew "penimah" can mean "within" (internally/inwardly) or "inside" (in the inner chambers). KJV emphasizes inner spiritual beauty; ESV reads it as a location (within the palace). Both interpretations have merit.

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. Christ's beauty surpasses all. In a world obsessed with appearance and
   celebrity, Psalm 45 directs our admiration toward the true King. His
   beauty is moral, spiritual, and eternal. Grace on His lips, righteousness
   in His rule, justice in His kingdom — this is what deserves our deepest
   admiration.

2. The King is God. Verse 6 is not metaphor or flattery — it is revelation.
   Jesus Christ is not merely a great teacher or moral example. He is God
   enthroned forever. Our worship, devotion, and obedience are His rightful
   due as the divine King.

3. Following Christ means leaving behind. The bride is told to "forget thy
   father's house" (v. 10). Discipleship requires forsaking old allegiances
   — not necessarily family relationships, but the old identity, old
   loyalties, and old ways that compete with Christ for first place. The
   cost is real, but the King's desire for us makes it worthwhile.

4. The Church's glory comes from the King. The bride is "all glorious" (v.
   13) — but her beauty derives from her relationship to the King, not from
   herself. Our righteousness, our beauty before God, comes solely from
   Christ. We are clothed in His splendor, not our own achievements.

5. Christ's kingdom is eternal and expanding. The psalm promises that the
   King's name will be "remembered in all generations" (v. 17) and praised
   "for ever and ever." We serve a King whose reign has no end and whose
   fame only grows. We are part of an eternal dynasty — not servants of a
   fading earthly power.

RESOURCES
------------------------------------
The Bible Project — Psalms Overview:
  https://bibleproject.com/explore/video/psalms/

David Guzik — Psalms 45 Written Commentary:
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/psalms-45/

BibleProject Podcast:
  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

46.notes:
Psalms - Chapter 46
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
A psalm of trust (Song of Zion) — 'God is our refuge and strength, a very present help in trouble'; though the earth be removed and mountains shake, the city of God stands; 'Be still, and know that I am God.'

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Multiple authors — primarily David (73 psalms attributed),
plus Asaph (12), Sons of Korah (11), Solomon (2), Moses (1),
Heman (1), Ethan (1), and anonymous. The Psalter was compiled over
approximately 1000 years and served as Israel's hymnal and prayer
book. The book is divided into five 'books' (1-41, 42-72, 73-89,
90-106, 107-150), paralleling the five books of Moses. Key themes:
worship, lament, praise, trust, kingship, creation, wisdom,
Messianic prophecy, and the full range of human emotion brought
before God.

Classification: Hymn of Trust/Confidence (Song of Zion)
Attributed Author: Sons of Korah (To the chief Musician for the
sons of Korah, A Song upon Alamoth)
Key Themes: God as refuge, fearlessness in chaos, God's sovereign
power, "Be still and know," divine presence in the city of God

Historical Context: Psalm 46 is one of the most beloved and
powerful hymns in Scripture — a triumphant declaration of God's
protection amidst cosmic chaos. The superscription "Alamoth"
(literally "maidens" or "sopranos") likely indicates a musical
direction for high-pitched voices or instruments. The historical
occasion is debated; many connect it to the miraculous deliverance
of Jerusalem from Sennacherib's Assyrian army in 701 BC (2 Kings
18-19), when 185,000 soldiers were destroyed overnight by the
angel of the LORD.

The psalm operates on three levels of chaos: cosmic (vv. 1-3: the
earth shaking and seas roaring), political (vv. 4-7: nations
raging and kingdoms tottering), and military (vv. 8-11: wars and
weapons). Against all three, God stands as unshakeable refuge. The
refrain "The LORD of hosts is with us; the God of Jacob is our
refuge" (vv. 7, 11) anchors each section in the twin truths of
God's sovereign power ("LORD of hosts") and covenant relationship
("God of Jacob").

This psalm famously inspired Martin Luther's hymn "Ein feste Burg
ist unser Gott" ("A Mighty Fortress Is Our God," 1529), written
during his confrontation with the Roman Catholic Church and the
threats against his life. Luther's hymn became the anthem of the
Reformation and has strengthened believers through every subsequent
generation of trial. The psalm's message remains timeless: when
everything shakes, God does not.

Structure:
- God Our Refuge in Cosmic Upheaval (vv. 1-3)
- God's Presence in the City (vv. 4-7)
- God's Sovereignty Over Nations and War (vv. 8-11)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.1              | Deuteronomy | 33      | 27                 | "The eternal God is thy refuge, and underneath are the everlasting arms." God as eternal refuge beneath His people
v.1              | Psalm       | 91      | 2                  | "I will say of the LORD, He is my refuge and my fortress: my God; in him will I trust." The same confidence expressed individually
v.4              | Revelation  | 22      | 1                  | "A pure river of water of life, clear as crystal, proceeding out of the throne of God." The ultimate fulfillment of the life-giving river in the city of God
v.4              | Ezekiel     | 47      | 1-9                | The river flowing from the temple that brings life wherever it goes, deepening as it flows
v.6              | Psalm       | 2       | 1                  | "Why do the heathen rage?" The nations' rage against God is futile — He melts the earth with His voice
v.9              | Isaiah      | 2       | 4                  | "They shall beat their swords into plowshares." God's ultimate cessation of war connects to the eschatological vision of peace
v.10             | Exodus      | 14      | 13-14              | "Stand still, and see the salvation of the LORD... The LORD shall fight for you, and ye shall hold your peace." The same command to cease striving and watch God work
v.11             | Isaiah      | 7       | 14                 | "Behold, a virgin shall conceive, and bear a son, and shall call his name Immanuel" (God with us). The refrain anticipates the Incarnation

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.1              | KJV         | "a very present help" vs. ESV "a very present help" — Both identical. The Hebrew "nimtsa me'od" is intensified: "found exceedingly" or "abundantly available." God is not merely available but overwhelmingly, superlatively accessible in trouble.
v.4              | KJV         | "the holy place of the tabernacles of the most High" vs. ESV "the holy habitation of the Most High" — The Hebrew "mishkeney" (plural of mishkan — tabernacle/dwelling) suggests multiple dwelling places or the fullness of God's dwelling. KJV's "tabernacles" preserves the plural; ESV smooths to singular "habitation."
v.5              | KJV         | "and that right early" vs. ESV "when morning dawns" — The Hebrew "lipnot boqer" literally means "at the turning of the morning" — at the break of dawn. KJV renders this idiomatically; ESV translates more literally. The point is God's help arrives precisely at the right moment, often at the darkest hour just before dawn.
v.6              | KJV         | "The heathen raged" (past tense) vs. ESV "The nations rage" (present tense) — The Hebrew verb forms here are debated. KJV reads them as completed action (God already conquered); ESV reads them as ongoing reality (nations currently raging). Both are grammatically possible; the theological point is the same — God's voice dissolves all opposition.
v.7              | KJV         | "our refuge" vs. ESV "our fortress" — The Hebrew "misgab" means a high place, stronghold, or secure height — an elevated fortress unreachable by enemies. ESV's "fortress" is more precise than KJV's "refuge" (which was already used for "machaseh" in v. 1). These are two different Hebrew words for shelter.

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. God is refuge, not merely resource. He is not simply someone we call upon
   in emergencies — He is the shelter we inhabit. There is a difference
   between running to God when trouble comes and already dwelling in God so
   that trouble finds us already secure. The psalm speaks of God as our
   permanent address, not our emergency contact.

2. "Be still" is a command, not a suggestion. In a culture of constant
   anxiety, productivity pressure, and frantic activity, verse 10 calls us
   to deliberate cessation. Stillness before God is not laziness — it is the
   active discipline of releasing our grip on outcomes and trusting His
   sovereignty. It requires more strength than striving.

3. When everything shakes, God remains unmoved. Mountains fall into the sea,
   nations rage, kingdoms totter — but "she shall not be moved" (v. 5).
   Whatever is shaking in your life right now is not the foundation. God is
   the foundation, and He cannot be shaken. We can release our fear
   precisely because His stability is absolute.

4. God's presence is better than perfect circumstances. Jerusalem had no
   river, but God was better than a river. We may lack what others have —
   resources, advantages, earthly security — but God's presence compensates
   for every absence. The gladness comes from Him, not from having
   everything figured out.

5. "The LORD of hosts is with us." This is the Immanuel promise that runs
   from Eden through Bethlehem to eternity. The God who commands angel
   armies is also the God of Jacob — the God who meets us in our weakness,
   wrestles with us, and gives us new names. His power and His proximity are
   equally ours.

RESOURCES
------------------------------------
The Bible Project — Psalms Overview:
  https://bibleproject.com/explore/video/psalms/

David Guzik — Psalms 46 Written Commentary:
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/psalms-46/

BibleProject Podcast:
  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

========================================
Study Notes generated for personal Bible study use.
Translations referenced: KJV, ESV, ASV, NET, WEB
Commentary sources: Enduring Word (enduringword.com), Charles
Spurgeon (Public Domain)

6. Sing to God! Sing! Sing to our king! Sing!
7. For God is king of the whole earth. Sing a well-written song.
8. God reigns over the nations. God sits on his holy throne.
9. The nobles of the nations assemble, along with the people of the God of Abraham, for God has authority over the rulers of the earth. He is highly exalted.
47.WEB:
1. Oh clap your hands, all you nations. Shout to God with the voice of triumph!
2. For Yahweh Most High is awesome. He is a great King over all the earth.
3. He subdues nations under us, and peoples under our feet.
4. He chooses our inheritance for us, the glory of Jacob whom he loved. Selah.
5. God has gone up with a shout, Yahweh with the sound of a trumpet.
6. Sing praises to God, sing praises. Sing praises to our King, sing praises.
7. For God is the King of all the earth. Sing praises with understanding.
8. God reigns over the nations. God sits on his holy throne.
9. The princes of the peoples are gathered together, the people of the God of Abraham. For the shields of the earth belong to God. He is greatly exalted!
47.notes:
Psalms - Chapter 47
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
An enthronement psalm — 'Clap your hands, all ye people'; God has gone up with a shout, the LORD with the sound of a trumpet; He reigns over the nations and sits upon His holy throne.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Multiple authors — primarily David (73 psalms attributed),
plus Asaph (12), Sons of Korah (11), Solomon (2), Moses (1),
Heman (1), Ethan (1), and anonymous. The Psalter was compiled over
approximately 1000 years and served as Israel's hymnal and prayer
book. The book is divided into five 'books' (1-41, 42-72, 73-89,
90-106, 107-150), paralleling the five books of Moses. Key themes:
worship, lament, praise, trust, kingship, creation, wisdom,
Messianic prophecy, and the full range of human emotion brought
before God.

Classification: Hymn of Praise / Enthronement Psalm
Attributed Author: Sons of Korah (To the chief Musician, A Psalm
for the sons of Korah)
Key Themes: Universal kingship of God, exuberant praise, God's
ascension, nations gathered to God, joyful worship

Historical Context: Psalm 47 is one of the "Enthronement Psalms"
(alongside Psalms 93, 95-99) celebrating God's kingship over all
the earth. It continues the triumphant tone of Psalm 46 and forms
part of a cluster of praise psalms by the Sons of Korah (46-48).
While some scholars connect it to the Ark's processional entry into
Jerusalem (2 Samuel 6) or to a New Year's celebration of God's
sovereignty, its language transcends any single historical occasion.

The psalm is remarkable for its universal scope — "all ye people"
(v. 1), "all the earth" (v. 2, 7), "the heathen/nations" (v. 8),
"the people of the God of Abraham" (v. 9). This is not merely
Israelite nationalism but a vision of God reigning over every
nation. The mention of "the God of Abraham" (v. 9) rather than "the
God of Moses" points to the Abrahamic promise: "in thee shall all
families of the earth be blessed" (Genesis 12:3).

Verse 5 — "God is gone up with a shout, the LORD with the sound of
a trumpet" — has been traditionally associated with Christ's
Ascension. The early church connected this psalm to Ascension Day
liturgy, understanding God's "going up" as fulfilled in Christ's
ascent to the Father's right hand (Acts 1:9-11). The psalm thus
looks forward to the day when every knee shall bow and the nations
gather under the rule of the ascended King.

Structure:
- Call to Exuberant Praise (vv. 1-4)
- God's Ascension with Shout and Trumpet (vv. 5-7)
- God's Universal Reign Over All Nations (vv. 8-9)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.1              | Isaiah      | 55      | 12                 | "Ye shall go out with joy... the mountains and the hills shall break forth before you into singing, and all the trees of the field shall clap their hands." Creation itself joins the applause
v.2              | Psalm       | 95      | 3                  | "For the LORD is a great God, and a great King above all gods." God's supremacy over all other claimed authorities
v.5              | Acts        | 1       | 9-11               | "While they beheld, he was taken up; and a cloud received him out of their sight." Christ's ascension fulfills the imagery of God going up with a shout
v.5              | Ephesians   | 4       | 8                  | "When he ascended up on high, he led captivity captive, and gave gifts unto men." Paul connects ascension to triumph and generosity
v.7              | 1 Corinthians | 14      | 15                 | "I will sing with the spirit, and I will sing with the understanding also." Worship that engages the mind, not just emotion
v.8              | Revelation  | 11      | 15                 | "The kingdoms of this world are become the kingdoms of our Lord, and of his Christ; and he shall reign for ever and ever." The ultimate fulfillment of God's universal reign
v.9              | Galatians   | 3       | 28-29              | "If ye be Christ's, then are ye Abraham's seed, and heirs according to the promise." All nations gathered as Abraham's children through faith
v.9              | Genesis     | 12      | 3                  | "In thee shall all families of the earth be blessed." The Abrahamic promise of universal blessing fulfilled in the gathering of all peoples to God

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.2              | KJV         | "terrible" vs. ESV "to be feared" — The Hebrew "nora" (נוֹרָא) means awe-inspiring, fearsome, or dreadful. KJV's "terrible" carried this meaning in 1611 (inspiring terror/awe) but now suggests "bad" in modern English. ESV's "to be feared" is clearer for contemporary readers. The word describes reverential dread before God's majesty.
v.4              | KJV         | "the excellency of Jacob" vs. ESV "the pride of Jacob" — The Hebrew "ga'on" can mean pride, majesty, excellency, or glory. KJV uses the positive sense (excellency — the land as Israel's glorious inheritance); ESV's "pride" can sound negative in English but here means "glory" or "honored possession."
v.6              | KJV         | "sing praises with understanding" vs. ESV "sing praises with a psalm" — The Hebrew "maskil" (v. 7) is debated. It can mean "with understanding/skill" (KJV) or refer to a specific type of psalm — a "maskil" (ESV). The KJV reading emphasizes intelligent, thoughtful worship; the ESV reading sees a musical term. Both are grammatically possible.
v.9              | KJV         | "the people of the God of Abraham" vs. ESV "the people of the God of Abraham" — Identical translation. The Hebrew construction is significant: the princes of the peoples are gathered AS the people of Abraham's God — indicating their conversion and inclusion, not mere political submission.

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. Worship should be exuberant. This psalm commands clapping, shouting, and
   singing — physical expressions of joy. While reverence has its place, so
   does jubilation. If God is truly King of all the earth, our response
   should sometimes overflow in visible, audible celebration. Subdued
   worship is not always more spiritual.

2. God's reign is universal, not tribal. "King of all the earth" (v. 7) —
   not merely King of our denomination, nation, or tradition. His
   sovereignty extends over every people, culture, and government. This
   expands our prayer life, our missions vision, and our understanding of
   God's purposes.

3. Christ ascended in triumph. Verse 5 reminds us that Jesus did not merely
   depart — He ascended as conquering King, enthroned at the Father's right
   hand with all authority. His current position is one of supreme
   authority, and He awaits the day when every knee will bow.

4. Worship should engage the mind. "Sing with understanding" (v. 7) calls us
   beyond emotional experience to theological comprehension. We should know
   what we're singing, why we're singing it, and whom we're singing to.
   Mindless worship dishonors the God who gave us minds.

5. All nations will be gathered. The vision of verse 9 — princes of every
   people assembled as God's own — fuels hope for global redemption. Every
   tongue, tribe, and nation has representatives destined for God's kingdom.
   This is not wishful thinking but prophetic certainty.

RESOURCES
------------------------------------
The Bible Project — Psalms Overview:
  https://bibleproject.com/explore/video/psalms/

David Guzik — Psalms 47 Written Commentary:
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/psalms-47/

BibleProject Podcast:
  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/
