58.notes:
Psalms - Chapter 58
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
An imprecatory psalm — David confronts unjust rulers who speak not righteousness, praying God will break their teeth and make them vanish; the righteous will rejoice when they see vengeance, for God judges the earth.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Multiple authors — primarily David (73 psalms attributed),
plus Asaph (12), Sons of Korah (11), Solomon (2), Moses (1),
Heman (1), Ethan (1), and anonymous. The Psalter was compiled over
approximately 1000 years and served as Israel's hymnal and prayer
book. The book is divided into five 'books' (1-41, 42-72, 73-89,
90-106, 107-150), paralleling the five books of Moses. Key themes:
worship, lament, praise, trust, kingship, creation, wisdom,
Messianic prophecy, and the full range of human emotion brought
before God.

Classification: Imprecatory Psalm
Attributed Author: David
Key Themes: Corrupt judges, injustice, innate human wickedness,
divine vengeance, God as righteous Judge, vindication of the
righteous

Historical Context: This Miktam of David is set to "Do Not Destroy"
(Al Tashheth) and addresses corrupt rulers or judges ("gods" /
"congregation" in v.1) who pervert justice. The Hebrew "elem" in
v.1 is debated — it could mean "silence" (are you silent about
justice?), "gods" (divine beings/rulers), or "congregation." The
psalm is one of the strongest imprecatory psalms, using vivid
imagery to call for God's judgment on the unjust. It may reflect
David's experience under Saul's corrupt administration or the
general oppression of the powerless by wicked authorities. The
psalm's harsh imagery (broken teeth, melting snails, bloody feet)
reflects the seriousness with which Scripture treats injustice.

Structure:
- Accusation Against Corrupt Judges (vv.1-2)
- The Nature of the Wicked from Birth (vv.3-5)
- Imprecations Against the Wicked (vv.6-9)
- The Righteous Vindicated (vv.10-11)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.1              | Psalm       | 82      | 1-2                | "God standeth in the congregation of the mighty; he judgeth among the gods." Same address to unjust rulers as "gods."
v.3              | Psalm       | 51      | 5                  | "In sin did my mother conceive me." Same doctrine of innate sinfulness
v.4              | Romans      | 3       | 13                 | "The poison of asps is under their lips." Paul uses this serpent imagery for universal sin
v.6              | Psalm       | 3       | 7                  | "Thou hast broken the teeth of the ungodly." Same imagery of disarming the wicked
v.10             | Revelation  | 19      | 2                  | "True and righteous are his judgments... he hath avenged the blood of his servants." Ultimate vindication
v.11             | Psalm       | 9       | 16                 | "The LORD is known by the judgment which he executeth." God revealed through justice

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.1              | KJV         | "O congregation" / ESV: "you gods" — The Hebrew "elem" is highly debated. It could be: (1) a scribal variant of "elim" (gods/mighty ones = rulers); (2) related to "elem" (silence); or (3) a form of "eylam" (congregation). ESV follows the interpretation of rulers as God's representatives.
v.8              | KJV         | "As a snail which melteth" / ESV: "like the snail that dissolves into slime" — The ancient understanding was that snails melted as they moved (their trail being their substance disappearing). The image is of gradual dissolution and disappearance.
v.9              | KJV         | "Before your pots can feel the thorns" / ESV: "Sooner than your pots can feel the heat of thorns" — An image of swiftness: before a cooking fire of thorns can even heat the pot, God's judgment comes. Thorns burn quickly but even that speed is too slow for God's justice.

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. Those in authority will answer for how they use their power (vv.1-2).
   Position carries responsibility. Every judge, ruler, and leader will give
   account for whether they used their authority for justice or for personal
   gain.

2. Human wickedness is innate, not merely environmental (v.3). This is
   uncomfortable but biblical. We are not born neutral and corrupted by
   society — we are born with a bent toward sin. This makes grace necessary,
   not optional.

3. It is right to pray for justice against oppressors (v.6). The imprecatory
   psalms are not about personal revenge but about God's protection of the
   vulnerable. When the powerful crush the weak, it is righteous to cry out
   for God to intervene.

4. God's judgment vindicates His character (v.11). When justice finally
   comes, it proves that God is real, that He sees, and that righteousness
   matters. Delayed justice is not denied justice — God WILL judge the
   earth.

59.notes:
Psalms - Chapter 59
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
A lament — David prays for deliverance when Saul sends men to watch his house; enemies prowl like howling dogs, but God is David's fortress and strength; he will sing of God's power and steadfast love in the morning.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Multiple authors — primarily David (73 psalms attributed),
plus Asaph (12), Sons of Korah (11), Solomon (2), Moses (1),
Heman (1), Ethan (1), and anonymous. The Psalter was compiled over
approximately 1000 years and served as Israel's hymnal and prayer
book. The book is divided into five 'books' (1-41, 42-72, 73-89,
90-106, 107-150), paralleling the five books of Moses. Key themes:
worship, lament, praise, trust, kingship, creation, wisdom,
Messianic prophecy, and the full range of human emotion brought
before God.

Classification: Individual Lament Psalm
Attributed Author: David
Key Themes: Deliverance from enemies, innocence under persecution,
God as fortress and defense, morning praise, God's sovereign
laughter over the wicked

Historical Context: The superscription connects this psalm to when
Saul sent men to watch David's house in order to kill him (1 Samuel
19:11-17). Michal, David's wife, warned him and helped him escape
through a window. The "dogs" prowling the city (vv.6,14) likely
represent Saul's agents circling David's house at night. This
Miktam is set to "Do Not Destroy" (Al Tashheth). David protests
his innocence — he has committed no transgression against Saul —
yet is hunted like a criminal. The psalm uses a refrain structure
with the dog imagery (vv.6,14) creating a frame around the
central section.

Structure:
- Cry for Deliverance (vv.1-5)
- The Prowling Enemies (vv.6-7)
- God's Sovereign Derision (vv.8-10)
- Prayer for Just Judgment (vv.11-13)
- The Prowling Enemies Repeated (vv.14-15)
- Morning Song of Praise (vv.16-17)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.1              | 1 Samuel    | 19      | 11-17              | Michal helping David escape Saul's assassins, the historical setting
v.6              | Philippians | 3       | 2                  | "Beware of dogs." Dogs as a biblical image for the unclean and hostile
v.8              | Psalm       | 2       | 4                  | "He that sitteth in the heavens shall laugh." Same divine laughter at human opposition
v.9              | Psalm       | 18      | 2                  | "The LORD is my rock, and my fortress, and my deliverer." David's consistent fortress imagery
v.11             | Romans      | 9       | 17                 | God preserving Pharaoh to display His power — similar to David's "slay them not, lest my people forget."
v.16             | Psalm       | 30      | 5                  | "Weeping may endure for a night, but joy cometh in the morning." Same evening/morning contrast
v.22             | 1 Peter     | 5       | 7                  | "Cast your burden on the LORD."

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.9              | KJV         | "Because of his strength will I wait upon thee" / ESV: "O my Strength, I will watch for you" — The Hebrew is ambiguous: "his strength" (the enemy's) or "my Strength" (addressed to God). ESV reads it as a divine title; KJV reads it as the enemy's strength prompting David to wait on God. ESV is more likely correct given the parallel in v.17.
v.10             | KJV         | "The God of my mercy shall prevent me" / ESV: "My God in his steadfast love will meet me" — "Prevent" in 1611 English meant "go before" (from Latin praevenire). ESV clarifies: God goes ahead of David to meet him. God's mercy anticipates our need.
v.15             | KJV         | "grudge if they be not satisfied" / ESV: "growl if they do not get their fill" — The image of dogs scavenging for food and growling in frustration. The enemies are reduced from threatening hunters to pathetic scavengers.

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. Innocence does not prevent persecution (vv.3-4). David is clear: "not for
   my transgression, nor for my sin." Sometimes you suffer not because
   you've done wrong but because others are doing wrong. Don't assume
   persecution means you deserve it.

2. God laughs at what terrifies you (v.8). The enemies that keep you awake
   at night are a joke to the God who rules the universe. His perspective
   should inform yours — what seems overwhelming from earth is laughable
   from heaven.

3. Morning is for singing (v.16). After the long night of danger, choose to
   greet the morning with praise. Don't let last night's fears set the tone
   for today. Morning is for mercy and music.

4. God is both strength and fortress (v.17). He is offensive power
   (strength) and defensive protection (fortress). Whatever you need —
   energy to advance or shelter to rest — He provides both.

RESOURCES
------------------------------------
The Bible Project — Psalms Overview:
  https://bibleproject.com/explore/video/psalms/

David Guzik — Psalms 59 Written Commentary:
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/psalms-59/

BibleProject Podcast:
  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

Study Notes generated for personal Bible study use.
Translations referenced: KJV, ESV, ASV, NET, WEB
Commentary sources: Enduring Word (enduringword.com), Charles
Spurgeon (Public Domain)

60.ESV:
1. O God, you have rejected us, broken our defenses; you have been angry; oh, restore us.
2. You have made the land to quake; you have torn it open; repair its breaches, for it totters.

5. That your beloved ones may be delivered, give salvation by your right hand and answer us!
6. God has spoken in his holiness: “With exultation I will divide up Shechem and portion out the Vale of Succoth.
7. Gilead is mine; Manasseh is mine; Ephraim is my helmet; Judah is my scepter.

60.notes:
Psalms - Chapter 60
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
A communal lament after military defeat — God has rejected Israel and broken them, yet David cries for help: 'Give us aid against the enemy, for the help of man is vain.' God declares victory over surrounding nations.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Multiple authors — primarily David (73 psalms attributed),
plus Asaph (12), Sons of Korah (11), Solomon (2), Moses (1),
Heman (1), Ethan (1), and anonymous. The Psalter was compiled over
approximately 1000 years and served as Israel's hymnal and prayer
book. The book is divided into five 'books' (1-41, 42-72, 73-89,
90-106, 107-150), paralleling the five books of Moses. Key themes:
worship, lament, praise, trust, kingship, creation, wisdom,
Messianic prophecy, and the full range of human emotion brought
before God.

Classification: Community Lament / Royal Psalm
Attributed Author: David
Key Themes: National defeat and divine discipline, God's
sovereignty over all nations, hope after setback, the futility of
human help, confidence in God's ultimate victory

Historical Context: The superscription places this psalm during
David's wars in the northeast (Aram-naharaim and Aram-zobah) when
Joab returned and struck 12,000 of Edom in the Valley of Salt
(2 Samuel 8:13; 1 Chronicles 18:12). While David fought in the
north, Edom attacked from the south, causing an initial defeat
that felt like divine rejection. The psalm is a Miktam set to
"Shushan Eduth" (Lily of the Testimony) and is marked "for
teaching." It moves from lament over defeat to a divine oracle of
sovereignty to renewed confidence. Verses 6-12 reappear nearly
verbatim in Psalm 108:7-13.

Structure:
- Lament Over National Defeat (vv.1-3)
- A Banner of Hope (vv.4-5)
- God's Oracle of Sovereignty (vv.6-8)
- Question of Faith (vv.9-10)
- Confidence in God Alone (vv.11-12)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.1              | 2 Samuel    | 8       | 13                 | Joab's victory over Edom in the Valley of Salt, the historical context
v.4              | Isaiah      | 11      | 10                 | "There shall be a root of Jesse, which shall stand for an ensign of the people." Christ as the ultimate banner
v.6-8            | Psalm       | 108     | 7-13               | Nearly identical repetition of these verses in a later psalm
v.8              | Genesis     | 25      | 23                 | "The elder shall serve the younger." God's sovereignty over Edom (Esau) fulfilled here
v.11             | Psalm       | 146     | 3                  | "Put not your trust in princes." Same warning against human reliance
v.12             | Philippians | 4       | 13                 | "I can do all things through Christ which strengtheneth me." Same principle of divine empowerment

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.4              | KJV         | "that it may be displayed because of the truth" / ESV: "that they may flee to it from the bow" — This is a significant difference. Hebrew "qoshet" could mean "truth" or be related to "qesheth" (bow). ESV follows an emendation suggesting a military banner to rally to for safety. KJV keeps "truth" as the reason for the banner.
v.8              | KJV         | "over Edom will I cast out my shoe" / ESV: "upon Edom I cast my shoe" — Casting a sandal over property indicated ownership (Ruth 4:7). God claims Edom as His possession to dispose of as He wills.
v.8              | KJV         | "Philistia, triumph thou because of me" / ESV: "over Philistia I shout in triumph" — The Hebrew is ambiguous. KJV reads it as ironic/sarcastic; ESV reads it as God's shout of victory over Philistia. ESV is more consistent with the context of God's dominion over enemy nations.

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. God can use defeat to teach dependence (vv.1-3). Israel's initial loss
   drove them back to God. Sometimes God allows setbacks to break our
   self-sufficiency and remind us that victory comes from Him alone.

2. God is sovereign over nations we consider enemies (vv.6-8). No nation, no
   opposition, no human power is outside God's control. Moab, Edom,
   Philistia — all are His to command. Your "enemies" are already under His
   authority.

3. Human help is vain without God (v.11). Alliances, strategies, resources —
   all empty without divine backing. This doesn't mean don't plan; it means
   don't trust in planning AS God.

4. Through God, we do valiantly (v.12). The partnership: God empowers, we
   act. Neither passive waiting nor self-reliant striving — but
   God-dependent, courageous action.

RESOURCES
------------------------------------
The Bible Project — Psalms Overview:
  https://bibleproject.com/explore/video/psalms/

David Guzik — Psalms 60 Written Commentary:
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/psalms-60/

BibleProject Podcast:
  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

========================================
Study Notes generated for personal Bible study use.
Translations referenced: KJV, ESV, ASV, NET, WEB
Commentary sources: Enduring Word (enduringword.com), Charles
Spurgeon (Public Domain)

3. for you have been my refuge, a strong tower against the enemy.
4. Let me dwell in your tent forever! Let me take refuge under the shelter of your wings! Selah
5. For you, O God, have heard my vows; you have given me the heritage of those who fear your name.
6. Prolong the life of the king; may his years endure to all generations!
7. May he be enthroned forever before God; appoint steadfast love and faithfulness to watch over him!
8. So will I ever sing praises to your name, as I perform my vows day after day.

1. Hear my cry, God. Listen to my prayer.
2. From the end of the earth, I will call to you, when my heart is overwhelmed. Lead me to the rock that is higher than I.
3. For you have been a refuge for me, a strong tower from the enemy.
4. I will dwell in your tent forever. I will take refuge in the shelter of your wings. Selah.
5. For you, God, have heard my vows. You have given me the heritage of those who fear your name.
6. You will prolong the king’s life; his years shall be for generations.
7. He shall be enthroned in God’s presence forever. Appoint your loving kindness and truth, that they may preserve him.

61.notes:
Psalms - Chapter 61
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
A psalm of trust — from the ends of the earth David cries to God: 'Lead me to the rock that is higher than I'; he asks to dwell in God's tent forever, sheltered under His wings.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Multiple authors — primarily David (73 psalms attributed),
plus Asaph (12), Sons of Korah (11), Solomon (2), Moses (1),
Heman (1), Ethan (1), and anonymous. The Psalter was compiled over
approximately 1000 years and served as Israel's hymnal and prayer
book. The book is divided into five 'books' (1-41, 42-72, 73-89,
90-106, 107-150), paralleling the five books of Moses. Key themes:
worship, lament, praise, trust, kingship, creation, wisdom,
Messianic prophecy, and the full range of human emotion brought
before God.

Classification: Royal Psalm / Individual Lament
Attributed Author: David
Key Themes: Crying out from a distance, the rock higher than I,
God as shelter and strong tower, longing for God's presence,
prayer for the king, eternal praise

Historical Context: This psalm likely comes from a time when David
was far from Jerusalem — possibly during Absalom's rebellion when
he fled east across the Jordan (2 Samuel 15-17). The phrase "from
the end of the earth" suggests geographical distance from the
sanctuary. David's heart is "overwhelmed" (faint), yet he reaches
toward God from afar. The prayer for the king (vv.6-7) may be
David praying for himself in the third person (a royal convention)
or may have been adapted for later liturgical use. The Messianic
overtones in "He shall abide before God for ever" point beyond any
earthly king to Christ.

Structure:
- Cry From a Distance (vv.1-2)
- God as Shelter and Tower (vv.3-4)
- Heritage of the God-Fearers (v.5)
- Prayer for the King (vv.6-7)
- Vow of Perpetual Praise (v.8)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.2              | Psalm       | 18      | 2                  | "The LORD is my rock, and my fortress." Same rock imagery for God's stability
v.2              | Psalm       | 42      | 6                  | "O my God, my soul is cast down within me: therefore will I remember thee from the land of Jordan." Same crying from geographical distance
v.3              | Proverbs    | 18      | 10                 | "The name of the LORD is a strong tower: the righteous runneth into it, and is safe."
v.4              | Psalm       | 27      | 4                  | "One thing have I desired of the LORD... to dwell in the house of the LORD all the days of my life."
v.6-7            | 2 Samuel    | 7       | 13,16              | God's promise to David: "I will establish the throne of his kingdom for ever." The Davidic covenant background
v.7              | Psalm       | 89      | 1-4                | The Davidic covenant psalm elaborating on eternal kingship

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.2              | KJV         | "when my heart is overwhelmed" / ESV: "when my heart is faint" — Hebrew "ataf" = to be faint, feeble, covered over (like a garment covering). Both translations work; "overwhelmed" captures the sense of being covered/smothered by distress.
v.4              | KJV         | "I will abide in thy tabernacle for ever" / ESV: "Let me dwell in your tent forever!" — KJV reads as confident declaration; ESV as a prayer/wish. The Hebrew supports both readings. ESV's exclamatory wish captures the longing quality.
v.7              | KJV         | "O prepare mercy and truth, which may preserve him" / ESV: "appoint steadfast love and faithfulness to watch over him" — ESV personifies hesed and emeth as guardians appointed to protect the king. A beautiful image of God's covenant attributes as personal bodyguards.

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. You can cry to God from anywhere (vv.1-2). "From the end of the earth" —
   no distance is too far for prayer. No situation is too remote for God to
   hear. Even when you feel at the farthest point from God, you can still
   call.

2. When overwhelmed, seek the rock higher than yourself (v.2). The solution
   to being overwhelmed is not self-sufficiency but elevation to something
   greater. God is the rock you cannot reach on your own — He must "lead"
   you there.

3. Past faithfulness is the foundation of present trust (v.3). Remember what
   God HAS done. His track record is your evidence. The God who was your
   shelter before will be your shelter again.

4. The deepest human longing is for permanent presence with God (v.4). Under
   everything — success, failure, danger, peace — what the soul truly wants
   is to dwell with God forever. Every other desire is a shadow of this one.

RESOURCES
------------------------------------
The Bible Project — Psalms Overview:
  https://bibleproject.com/explore/video/psalms/

David Guzik — Psalms 61 Written Commentary:
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/psalms-61/

BibleProject Podcast:
  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

Translations referenced: KJV, ESV, ASV, NET, WEB
Commentary sources: Enduring Word (enduringword.com), Charles
Spurgeon (Public Domain)

6. He only is my rock and my salvation, my fortress; I shall not be shaken.

9. Those of low estate are but a breath; those of high estate are a delusion; in the balances they go up; they are together lighter than a breath.

10. Trust not in oppression, And become not vain in robbery: If riches increase, set not your heart [thereon].

62.NET:
1. For the music director, Jeduthun; a psalm of David. For God alone I patiently wait; he is the one who delivers me.
2. He alone is my protector and deliverer. He is my refuge; I will not be upended.
3. How long will you threaten a man like me? All of you are murderers, as dangerous as a leaning wall or an unstable fence.

62.notes:
Psalms - Chapter 62
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
A psalm of quiet trust — 'My soul waiteth only upon God; from him cometh my salvation'; men are but a breath, trust not in extortion or riches; power and steadfast love belong to God alone.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Multiple authors — primarily David (73 psalms attributed),
plus Asaph (12), Sons of Korah (11), Solomon (2), Moses (1),
Heman (1), Ethan (1), and anonymous. The Psalter was compiled over
approximately 1000 years and served as Israel's hymnal and prayer
book. The book is divided into five 'books' (1-41, 42-72, 73-89,
90-106, 107-150), paralleling the five books of Moses. Key themes:
worship, lament, praise, trust, kingship, creation, wisdom,
Messianic prophecy, and the full range of human emotion brought
before God.

Classification: Trust / Confidence Psalm
Attributed Author: David (directed to Jeduthun, a worship leader)
Key Themes: Waiting on God alone, soul silence before God, God as
rock and fortress, the vanity of human trust, power and mercy
belong to God

Historical Context: This psalm is directed "to Jeduthun" (one of
David's three chief musicians — 1 Chronicles 16:41-42). It may
date from Absalom's rebellion when David faced both political
conspiracy (v.4) and personal betrayal (v.4b — "they bless with
their mouth, but they curse inwardly"). The repeated word "only"
(Hebrew "ak") appears six times, making it the psalm's key word.
David insists that God ALONE is sufficient — not human alliances,
not wealth, not power. The psalm moves from personal testimony
(vv.1-7) to public exhortation (vv.8-12) and concludes with one
of Scripture's clearest statements about God's nature: power and
mercy belong to Him.

Structure:
- David's Soul Waits in Silence (vv.1-2)
- Rebuke of Enemies (vv.3-4)
- Renewed Declaration of Trust (vv.5-7)
- Exhortation to Trust God (v.8)
- The Vanity of Human Trust (vv.9-10)
- God's Final Word: Power and Mercy (vv.11-12)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.1              | Psalm       | 37      | 7                  | "Rest in the LORD, and wait patiently for him." Same concept of quiet waiting
v.2              | Psalm       | 18      | 2                  | "The LORD is my rock, and my fortress." Same rock/fortress language
v.8              | 1 Samuel    | 1       | 15                 | Hannah: "I have poured out my soul before the LORD." The practice David commands
v.9              | Isaiah      | 40      | 15,17              | Nations are "as a drop of a bucket... they are counted to him less than nothing." Same theme of human insubstantiality
v.10             | 1 Timothy   | 6       | 17                 | "Charge them that are rich in this world, that they be not highminded, nor trust in uncertain riches."
v.12             | Romans      | 2       | 6                  | "Who will render to every man according to his deeds." God's just recompense
v.12             | Revelation  | 22      | 12                 | "My reward is with me, to give every man according as his work shall be."

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.1              | KJV         | "Truly my soul waiteth upon God" / ESV: "For God alone my soul waits in silence" — Hebrew "ak" = only, surely, indeed. ESV adds "in silence" from "dumiyyah" which KJV renders more loosely. The Hebrew emphasizes both exclusivity (God alone) and manner (in silence/stillness).
v.9              | KJV         | "lighter than vanity" / ESV: "lighter than a breath" — Hebrew "hebel" (same word as Ecclesiastes' "vanity") = breath, vapor, mist. All humanity together, weighed against God's purposes, is lighter than a puff of air.
v.11             | KJV         | "God hath spoken once; twice have I heard this" / ESV: "Once God has spoken; twice have I heard this" — A Hebrew numerical saying (like Proverbs 30) emphasizing certainty. "Once... twice" = definitively, with absolute clarity. What follows is beyond doubt.

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. Silence before God is strength, not weakness (v.1). In a world that
   rewards noise and hustle, choosing stillness before God is radical trust.
   It says "I don't need to manipulate outcomes — God is enough."

2. You may be shaken but you won't be destroyed (v.2). "Not GREATLY moved" —
   faith doesn't promise zero turbulence. It promises you won't be
   overthrown. The rock sways in the storm but doesn't crumble.

3. Pour out your heart without editing (v.8). God doesn't need polished
   prayers. He wants raw honesty. Pour it out — the anger, the fear, the
   confusion, the grief. He is big enough to receive it all.

4. No human being can bear the weight of being your God (v.9). Don't put
   people on God's throne — they are lighter than breath. People will
   disappoint. Only God can bear the weight of your ultimate trust.

5. God is both powerful AND merciful (vv.11-12). This is the gospel in two
   words. If God were only powerful, we would be terrified. If only
   merciful, we would doubt His ability. He is both — and that changes
   everything.

63.notes:
Psalms - Chapter 63
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
A psalm of longing — David in the wilderness of Judah thirsts for God: 'My soul thirsteth for thee; my flesh longeth for thee in a dry and thirsty land.' God's lovingkindness is better than life.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Multiple authors — primarily David (73 psalms attributed),
plus Asaph (12), Sons of Korah (11), Solomon (2), Moses (1),
Heman (1), Ethan (1), and anonymous. The Psalter was compiled over
approximately 1000 years and served as Israel's hymnal and prayer
book. The book is divided into five 'books' (1-41, 42-72, 73-89,
90-106, 107-150), paralleling the five books of Moses. Key themes:
worship, lament, praise, trust, kingship, creation, wisdom,
Messianic prophecy, and the full range of human emotion brought
before God.

Classification: Individual Praise / Trust Psalm
Attributed Author: David
Key Themes: Thirsting for God, God's love better than life, soul
satisfaction in God, nighttime meditation, clinging to God, joy
under God's wings

Historical Context: The superscription places this psalm "when he
was in the wilderness of Judah" — likely during his flight from
Saul (1 Samuel 23-24) or from Absalom (2 Samuel 15:23-28). The
physical thirst of the wilderness becomes a metaphor for spiritual
longing. David is separated from the sanctuary ("so as I have seen
thee in the sanctuary" — v.2) yet experiences God's presence even
in the desert. This is one of the most beloved psalms of personal
devotion in Scripture. Early church tradition made it a morning
psalm ("early will I seek thee"), and it has been recited at
morning services throughout Christian history. The intimacy and
passion for God expressed here is unmatched in its intensity.

Structure:
- Thirsting for God in the Desert (vv.1-2)
- God's Love Better Than Life (vv.3-5)
- Nighttime Meditation and Joy (vv.6-8)
- Judgment on Enemies (vv.9-10)
- The King Rejoices in God (v.11)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.1              | Psalm       | 42      | 1-2                | "As the hart panteth after the water brooks, so panteth my soul after thee, O God." Same thirst imagery
v.1              | John        | 7       | 37                 | Jesus: "If any man thirst, let him come unto me, and drink." Christ as the answer to spiritual thirst
v.3              | Philippians | 1       | 21                 | "For to me to live is Christ, and to die is gain." Paul's echo of David's valuation
v.5              | Isaiah      | 55      | 1-2                | "Come ye to the waters... eat ye that which is good, and let your soul delight itself in fatness." Same satisfaction imagery
v.7              | Psalm       | 57      | 1                  | "In the shadow of thy wings will I make my refuge." Same protective wing image
v.8              | Deuteronomy | 10      | 20                 | "Thou shalt fear the LORD thy God... and shalt cleave unto him." The command David obeys
v.8              | Song of Solomon | 3       | 4                  | "I held him, and would not let him go." Same clinging love

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.1              | KJV         | "early will I seek thee" / ESV: "earnestly I seek you" — Hebrew "shachar" can mean "dawn/early" or "seek earnestly/ diligently." KJV takes the temporal sense (morning seeking); ESV takes the intensity sense (earnest seeking). Both are valid — David seeks early AND earnestly.
v.5              | KJV         | "marrow and fatness" / ESV: "fat and rich food" — Hebrew "cheleb" = fat, the best portion. In ancient culture, fat was the richest, most prized part of a meal. It represents abundance and satisfaction, not mere sustenance.
v.8              | KJV         | "My soul followeth hard after thee" / ESV: "My soul clings to you" — Hebrew "dabaq" = to cling, stick, adhere (same word used of marriage in Genesis 2:24). ESV's "clings" captures the adhesive, inseparable quality better than KJV's "followeth hard after," though KJV captures the pursuit dimension.

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. Let physical needs remind you of spiritual needs (v.1). David's physical
   thirst in the desert became a lens for understanding his deeper thirst
   for God. Don't just meet physical needs — let them point you to the
   deeper need only God satisfies.

2. God's love is better than life (v.3). This is the ultimate test: if you
   lost everything but God's love, would you still praise? David says yes.
   God's steadfast love outweighs all that life offers — because it outlasts
   life itself.

3. Satisfaction in God is feast-level, not merely sufficient (v.5). God
   doesn't just keep your soul alive — He makes it fat with abundance. The
   life of devotion is not spiritual starvation but spiritual feasting.

4. Cling to God AND be held by God (v.8). This is the beautiful paradox of
   the Christian life: we pursue God with all our might AND discover that
   His right hand has been holding us all along. Your clinging and His
   upholding are simultaneous.

5. Seek God in the night watches (v.6). When you can't sleep, don't reach
   for your phone — reach for God. Sleepless hours can become the richest
   hours of meditation and intimacy.

RESOURCES
------------------------------------
The Bible Project — Psalms Overview:
  https://bibleproject.com/explore/video/psalms/

David Guzik — Psalms 63 Written Commentary:
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/psalms-63/

BibleProject Podcast:
  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

4. shooting from ambush at the blameless, shooting at him suddenly and without fear.
5. They hold fast to their evil purpose; they talk of laying snares secretly, thinking, “Who can see them?”
6. They search out injustice, saying, “We have accomplished a diligent search.” For the inward mind and heart of a man are deep.
7. But God shoots his arrow at them; they are wounded suddenly.
8. They are brought to ruin, with their own tongues turned against them; all who see them will wag their heads.
9. Then all mankind fears; they tell what God has brought about and ponder what he has done.
10. Let the righteous one rejoice in the LORD and take refuge in him! Let all the upright in heart exult!

64.NET:
1. For the music director, a psalm of David. Listen to me, O God, as I offer my lament! Protect my life from the enemy’s terrifying attacks.
2. Hide me from the plots of evil men, from the crowd of evildoers.
3. They sharpen their tongues like swords; they aim their arrows, a slanderous charge,
4. in order to shoot down the innocent in secluded places. They shoot at him suddenly and are unafraid of retaliation.
5. They encourage one another to carry out their evil deed. They plan how to hide snares and boast, “Who will see them?”
6. They devise unjust schemes; they disguise a well-conceived plot. Man’s inner thoughts cannot be discovered.
7. But God will shoot at them; suddenly they will be wounded by an arrow.
8. Their slander will bring about their demise. All who see them will shudder,

1. Hear my voice, God, in my complaint. Preserve my life from fear of the enemy.
2. Hide me from the conspiracy of the wicked, from the noisy crowd of the ones doing evil;

64.notes:
Psalms - Chapter 64
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
A lament — David prays for protection from the secret plots of the wicked whose tongues are sharp swords; God will suddenly shoot His arrow at them, and all mankind will fear and declare His work.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Multiple authors — primarily David (73 psalms attributed),
plus Asaph (12), Sons of Korah (11), Solomon (2), Moses (1),
Heman (1), Ethan (1), and anonymous. The Psalter was compiled over
approximately 1000 years and served as Israel's hymnal and prayer
book. The book is divided into five 'books' (1-41, 42-72, 73-89,
90-106, 107-150), paralleling the five books of Moses. Key themes:
worship, lament, praise, trust, kingship, creation, wisdom,
Messianic prophecy, and the full range of human emotion brought
before God.

Classification: Individual Lament Psalm
Attributed Author: David
Key Themes: Secret plots of enemies, the tongue as weapon, God's
sudden counter-attack, divine poetic justice, the righteous
rejoicing in God's judgment

Historical Context: No specific historical event is named in the
superscription, but the psalm describes a situation where David
faces secret conspiracies and verbal attacks from those who plot in
hidden. This could fit many periods in David's life — Saul's
courtiers, Absalom's conspiracy, or any number of political
intrigues. The psalm is notable for its "boomerang" structure: the
arrows the wicked aim at the righteous (vv.3-4) become God's arrow
aimed at them (v.7). Their own tongue brings them down (v.8). The
theme of hidden plots met by sudden divine intervention runs
throughout.

Structure:
- Prayer for Protection (vv.1-2)
- The Enemy's Secret Attacks (vv.3-6)
- God's Sudden Counter-Strike (vv.7-8)
- Universal Recognition of God's Justice (vv.9-10)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.3              | Psalm       | 57      | 4                  | "Whose teeth are spears and arrows, and their tongue a sharp sword." Same weaponized tongue imagery
v.5              | Psalm       | 10      | 11                 | "He hath said in his heart, God hath forgotten: he hideth his face." Same assumed divine blindness
v.7              | Psalm       | 7       | 12-13              | "He hath bent his bow, and made it ready. He hath also prepared for him the instruments of death." God's weapons against the wicked
v.8              | Psalm       | 9       | 15                 | "The heathen are sunk down in the pit that they made: in the net which they hid is their own foot taken." Same boomerang justice
v.9              | Psalm       | 58      | 11                 | "Verily there is a God that judgeth in the earth." Same universal recognition of divine justice
v.10             | Psalm       | 32      | 11                 | "Be glad in the LORD, and rejoice, ye righteous: and shout for joy, all ye that are upright in heart."

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.1              | KJV         | "preserve my life from fear of the enemy" / ESV: "preserve my life from dread of the enemy" — Both translate "pachad" (fear/dread). The prayer is not just for physical preservation but for freedom from the paralyzing fear that enemies create.
v.6              | KJV         | "They search out iniquities; they accomplish a diligent search" / ESV: "They search out injustice, saying, 'We have accomplished a diligent search'" — The wicked are thorough in their evil planning. They "research" their wickedness with the same diligence a scholar would apply to study. But v.6b adds: "the inward thought of every one of them, and the heart, is deep" — human wickedness is deeper than even their own investigations reveal.
v.8              | KJV         | "So they shall make their own tongue to fall upon themselves" / ESV: "with their own tongues turned against them" — The tongue that was their weapon becomes their downfall. Their own words condemn them.

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. Words aimed in secret are still seen by God (v.5). "Who shall see them?"
   — God does. Every whispered slander, every secret plot, every hidden
   cruelty is fully visible to the One who sees all. Privacy from God is an
   illusion.

2. God's justice often mirrors the crime (v.7). Arrows shot at the righteous
   become God's arrow at the wicked. What you launch against others often
   returns to you. This is not karma — it is divine justice with poetic
   precision.

3. God's timing is sudden (v.7). The wicked assume they have endless time.
   Then suddenly — God acts. Don't mistake God's patience for God's absence.
   His intervention comes swiftly when it comes.

4. The purpose of judgment is declaration (v.9). God judges not merely to
   punish but to teach. His acts of justice declare His character to all who
   observe. When God acts, wisdom responds by "wisely considering" what He
   has done.

RESOURCES
------------------------------------
The Bible Project — Psalms Overview:
  https://bibleproject.com/explore/video/psalms/

David Guzik — Psalms 64 Written Commentary:
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/psalms-64/

BibleProject Podcast:
  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

Translations referenced: KJV, ESV, ASV, NET, WEB
Commentary sources: Enduring Word (enduringword.com), Charles
Spurgeon (Public Domain)

65.ESV:
1. Praise is due to you, O God, in Zion, and to you shall vows be performed.
2. O you who hear prayer, to you shall all flesh come.
3. When iniquities prevail against me, you atone for our transgressions.
4. Blessed is the one you choose and bring near, to dwell in your courts! We shall be satisfied with the goodness of your house, the holiness of your temple!
5. By awesome deeds you answer us with righteousness, O God of our salvation, the hope of all the ends of the earth and of the farthest seas;
6. the one who by his strength established the mountains, being girded with might;
7. who stills the roaring of the seas, the roaring of their waves, the tumult of the peoples,
8. so that those who dwell at the ends of the earth are in awe at your signs. You make the going out of the morning and the evening to shout for joy.
9. You visit the earth and water it; you greatly enrich it; the river of God is full of water; you provide their grain, for so you have prepared it.
10. You water its furrows abundantly, settling its ridges, softening it with showers, and blessing its growth.
11. You crown the year with your bounty; your wagon tracks overflow with abundance.
12. The pastures of the wilderness overflow, the hills gird themselves with joy,
13. the meadows clothe themselves with flocks, the valleys deck themselves with grain, they shout and sing together for joy.

7. Which stilleth the noise of the seas, the noise of their waves, and the tumult of the people.

10. Thou waterest the ridges thereof abundantly: thou settlest the furrows thereof: thou makest it soft with showers: thou blessest the springing thereof.
11. Thou crownest the year with thy goodness; and thy paths drop fatness.

65.ASV:

5. By terrible things thou wilt answer us in righteousness, Oh God of our salvation, Thou that art the confidence of all the ends of the earth, And of them that are afar off upon the sea:
6. Who by his strength setteth fast the mountains, Being girded about with might;
7. Who stilleth the roaring of the seas, The roaring of their waves, And the tumult of the peoples.
8. They also that dwell in the uttermost parts are afraid at thy tokens: Thou makest the outgoings of the morning and evening to rejoice.
9. Thou visitest the earth, and waterest it, Thou greatly enrichest it; The river of God is full of water: Thou providest them grain, when thou hast so prepared the earth.
10. Thou waterest its furrows abundantly; Thou settlest the ridges thereof: Thou makest it soft with showers; Thou blessest the springing thereof.
11. Thou crownest the year with thy goodness; And thy paths drop fatness.
12. They drop upon the pastures of the wilderness; And the hills are girded with joy.
13. The pastures are clothed with flocks; The valleys also are covered over with grain; They shout for joy, they also sing. Psalm 66 For the Chief Musician. A song, a Psalm.

65.notes:
Psalms - Chapter 65
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
A harvest thanksgiving psalm — praise awaits God in Zion; He forgives iniquity, answers prayer with awesome deeds, stills the roaring seas, and crowns the year with goodness as the pastures overflow.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Multiple authors — primarily David (73 psalms attributed),
plus Asaph (12), Sons of Korah (11), Solomon (2), Moses (1),
Heman (1), Ethan (1), and anonymous. The Psalter was compiled over
approximately 1000 years and served as Israel's hymnal and prayer
book. The book is divided into five 'books' (1-41, 42-72, 73-89,
90-106, 107-150), paralleling the five books of Moses. Key themes:
worship, lament, praise, trust, kingship, creation, wisdom,
Messianic prophecy, and the full range of human emotion brought
before God.

Classification: Hymn of Praise / Thanksgiving / Creation Psalm
Attributed Author: David
Key Themes: Praise for answered prayer, atonement for sin, the
blessedness of God's chosen ones, God's sovereignty over nature,
abundant provision, the joy of creation

Historical Context: This is likely a harvest thanksgiving psalm,
celebrating God's provision of rain and agricultural abundance
after a period of need. It may have been composed for a specific
harvest festival or after a drought was broken. The psalm is
remarkable for its scope — it moves from the temple (vv.1-4) to
cosmic creation (vv.5-8) to the specific fields and valleys of
Israel (vv.9-13). It is one of the most joyful psalms in the
Psalter, with creation itself personified as shouting and singing
for joy. The progression from sin forgiven to creation rejoicing
shows the connection between spiritual and physical blessing in
Israel's theology.

Structure:
- Praise in Zion and Answered Prayer (vv.1-2)
- Atonement for Sin (v.3)
- Blessedness of the Chosen (v.4)
- God's Awesome Deeds in Creation (vv.5-8)
- God's Provision for the Earth (vv.9-13)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.2              | Isaiah      | 56      | 7                  | "My house shall be called an house of prayer for all people." God's universal accessibility
v.3              | 1 John      | 2       | 2                  | "He is the propitiation for our sins: and not for ours only, but also for the sins of the whole world."
v.4              | Ephesians   | 1       | 4                  | "He hath chosen us in him before the foundation of the world." Divine election
v.4              | John        | 6       | 44                 | "No man can come to me, except the Father which hath sent me draw him." God causing approach
v.7              | Psalm       | 89      | 9                  | "Thou rulest the raging of the sea: when the waves thereof arise, thou stillest them."
v.9              | Deuteronomy | 11      | 11-12              | "A land which the LORD thy God careth for: the eyes of the LORD thy God are always upon it." God's agricultural care for Israel
v.13             | Romans      | 8       | 19-22              | Creation groaning and waiting for redemption — the counterpart to creation singing

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.1              | KJV         | "Praise waiteth for thee" / ESV: "Praise is due to you" — Hebrew "dumiyyah" (silence/stillness) is difficult here. Some read "praise silently waits" (KJV sense); others emend to "tehillah" = "praise is fitting/due" (ESV sense). The idea may be that praise awaits God in quiet readiness.
v.5              | KJV         | "By terrible things in righteousness wilt thou answer us" / ESV: "By awesome deeds you answer us with righteousness" — "Terrible" (KJV) = awe-inspiring, not frightening. ESV's "awesome" is clearer for modern readers. God's answers are dramatically powerful.
v.11             | KJV         | "thy paths drop fatness" / ESV: "your wagon tracks overflow with abundance" — Hebrew "ma'gal" = track, rut, wagon path. ESV helpfully specifies: wherever God's chariot travels through the land, abundance flows in its tracks like spilled grain or oil.

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. God hears prayer — that's His character (v.2). Prayer is not shouting
   into a void. The God of the universe is defined by hearing. And ALL flesh
   can come — no one is excluded from His audience.

2. Access to God is a gift, not an achievement (v.4). "Thou choosest and
   causest to approach." If you are drawn to God, it is because He drew you
   first. Proximity to God is grace, not merit.

3. God crowns the year with goodness (v.11). Look back over the past year —
   God's goodness is the crown. Even in hard years, His provision is
   present. His wagon tracks leave abundance in their wake.

4. All creation sings (v.13). The hills, valleys, and meadows worship. If
   nature sings to its Creator, how much more should those made in His
   image? Our silence is the anomaly, not our praise.

RESOURCES
------------------------------------
The Bible Project — Psalms Overview:
  https://bibleproject.com/explore/video/psalms/

David Guzik — Psalms 65 Written Commentary:
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/psalms-65/

BibleProject Podcast:
  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

========================================
Study Notes generated for personal Bible study use.
Translations referenced: KJV, ESV, ASV, NET, WEB
Commentary sources: Enduring Word (enduringword.com), Charles
Spurgeon (Public Domain)

66.notes:
Psalms - Chapter 66
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
A communal thanksgiving psalm — 'Make a joyful noise unto God, all ye lands'; the psalmist recounts God's mighty works (the Red Sea crossing), acknowledges testing like silver refined, and offers praise for answered prayer.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Multiple authors — primarily David (73 psalms attributed),
plus Asaph (12), Sons of Korah (11), Solomon (2), Moses (1),
Heman (1), Ethan (1), and anonymous. The Psalter was compiled over
approximately 1000 years and served as Israel's hymnal and prayer
book. The book is divided into five 'books' (1-41, 42-72, 73-89,
90-106, 107-150), paralleling the five books of Moses. Key themes:
worship, lament, praise, trust, kingship, creation, wisdom,
Messianic prophecy, and the full range of human emotion brought
before God.

Classification: Hymn of Praise / Thanksgiving Psalm
Attributed Author: Anonymous (no named author in superscription)
Key Themes: Universal praise, God's awesome deeds, testing and
refining through trials, personal testimony of answered prayer,
the condition for being heard by God

Historical Context: This psalm has no named author and may
commemorate a specific national deliverance (possibly from the
Assyrian threat — 2 Kings 19). It uniquely combines community
praise (vv.1-12) with individual thanksgiving (vv.13-20). The
transition from "we" to "I" at v.13 is distinctive — the community
celebration of God's deliverance leads to one voice offering
personal testimony. The psalm may have been used liturgically when
an individual brought thank offerings to the temple, incorporating
the congregation's praise with personal vows. The "fire and water"
of v.12 likely refers to severe national trial, while the "wealthy
place" (or "place of abundance") marks God's deliverance.

Structure:
- Call to Universal Praise (vv.1-4)
- God's Awesome Deeds Recalled (vv.5-7)
- Praise for Preservation Through Testing (vv.8-12)
- Personal Vow and Offering (vv.13-15)
- Personal Testimony (vv.16-20)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.6              | Exodus      | 14      | 21-22              | The Red Sea crossing. "He turned the sea into dry land."
v.10             | 1 Peter     | 1       | 7                  | "That the trial of your faith, being much more precious than of gold that perisheth, though it be tried with fire."
v.12             | Isaiah      | 43      | 2                  | "When thou passest through the waters, I will be with thee; and through the rivers, they shall not overflow thee."
v.18             | Psalm       | 24      | 3-4                | "Who shall ascend into the hill of the LORD?... He that hath clean hands, and a pure heart."
v.18             | James       | 4       | 3                  | "Ye ask, and receive not, because ye ask amiss." Conditions for prayer
v.18             | John        | 9       | 31                 | "God heareth not sinners: but if any man be a worshipper of God, and doeth his will, him he heareth."

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.1              | KJV         | "Make a joyful noise" / ESV: "Shout for joy" — Hebrew "hari'u" = shout, blast (like a trumpet). Both capture the exuberance; ESV's "shout for joy" is more natural in modern English.
v.3              | KJV         | "How terrible art thou in thy works" / ESV: "How awesome are your deeds" — "Terrible" (nora) in 1611 meant "inspiring awe/terror." Modern "terrible" means "bad." ESV's "awesome" (in its original sense) is more accurate for modern readers.
v.12             | KJV         | "a wealthy place" / ESV: "a place of abundance" — Hebrew "revayah" = abundance, overflow, saturation. ESV is clearer; the point is overflow and abundance, not merely material wealth.

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. God's testing is refining, not punishing (v.10). Silver is tried by fire
   not to destroy it but to purify it. When God allows trials, He is working
   toward a purer you, not a destroyed you.

2. The path to abundance goes THROUGH fire and water (v.12). There is no
   shortcut to the "wealthy place." The through-ness of trial is the pathway
   to blessing. Don't demand exemption from the process.

3. Cherished sin blocks prayer (v.18). The issue is not perfection but
   direction. If you are clinging to known sin — defending it, hiding it,
   refusing to release it — don't expect God to respond to your prayers. Let
   it go, and the line is clear again.

4. Testimony is powerful (v.16). "Come and hear... what he hath done for my
   soul." Personal testimony of God's faithfulness is one of the most
   convincing forms of witness. Tell your story.

RESOURCES
------------------------------------
The Bible Project — Psalms Overview:
  https://bibleproject.com/explore/video/psalms/

David Guzik — Psalms 66 Written Commentary:
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/psalms-66/

BibleProject Podcast:
  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

========================================
Study Notes generated for personal Bible study use.
Translations referenced: KJV, ESV, ASV, NET, WEB
Commentary sources: Enduring Word (enduringword.com), Charles
Spurgeon (Public Domain)

3. let the peoples praise you, God. Let all the peoples praise you.

67.notes:
Psalms - Chapter 67
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
A missionary psalm — a prayer for God's blessing upon Israel so that His way may be known among all nations and all peoples may praise Him; the earth shall yield her increase.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Multiple authors — primarily David (73 psalms attributed),
plus Asaph (12), Sons of Korah (11), Solomon (2), Moses (1),
Heman (1), Ethan (1), and anonymous. The Psalter was compiled over
approximately 1000 years and served as Israel's hymnal and prayer
book. The book is divided into five 'books' (1-41, 42-72, 73-89,
90-106, 107-150), paralleling the five books of Moses. Key themes:
worship, lament, praise, trust, kingship, creation, wisdom,
Messianic prophecy, and the full range of human emotion brought
before God.

Classification: Hymn of Praise / Missionary Psalm
Attributed Author: Anonymous
Key Themes: God's blessing for global mission, all nations
praising God, God's righteous governance, harvest blessing, the
Aaronic benediction extended to the world

Historical Context: This brief, beautiful psalm (7 verses) is
often called the "Old Testament Lord's Prayer" or the "Missionary
Psalm." It opens by echoing the Aaronic Benediction (Numbers
6:24-26) but with a PURPOSE: "that thy way may be known upon
earth" (v.2). Israel's blessing was never meant to be hoarded —
it was intended to be a conduit of God's self-revelation to ALL
nations. The psalm has a concentric structure with the refrain
"Let the peoples praise thee" (vv.3,5) framing the center (v.4).
It was likely used at harvest festivals (v.6 — "the earth has
yielded her increase") to remind Israel that their agricultural
blessing was connected to their calling to be a light to the
nations.

Structure:
- Prayer for Blessing with Purpose (vv.1-2)
- Refrain: Let the Peoples Praise God (v.3)
- Nations Rejoicing in God's Justice (v.4)
- Refrain: Let the Peoples Praise God (v.5)
- Harvest Blessing and Universal Fear (vv.6-7)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.1              | Numbers     | 6       | 24-26              | The Aaronic Benediction: "The LORD bless thee, and keep thee: The LORD make his face shine upon thee." Psalm 67 applies this blessing missionally
v.2              | Genesis     | 12      | 2-3                | "I will bless thee... and thou shalt be a blessing... in thee shall all families of the earth be blessed." The Abrahamic mission statement
v.4              | Revelation  | 15      | 4                  | "Who shall not fear thee, O Lord, and glorify thy name? for thou only art holy: for all nations shall come and worship before thee."
v.6              | Leviticus   | 26      | 4                  | "Then I will give you rain in due season, and the land shall yield her increase." Harvest as sign of covenant faithfulness
v.7              | Psalm       | 22      | 27                 | "All the ends of the world shall remember and turn unto the LORD: and all the kindreds of the nations shall worship before thee."

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.1              | KJV         | "God be merciful unto us" / ESV: "May God be gracious to us" — Hebrew "chanan" = to be gracious, show favor. ESV's "gracious" is closer to the Hebrew; KJV's "merciful" (racham) is a related but different concept.
v.2              | KJV         | "thy saving health" / ESV: "your saving power" — Hebrew "yeshuah" = salvation, deliverance. KJV's "saving health" is archaic; "salvation" or "saving power" captures the comprehensive deliverance God offers all nations.
v.4              | KJV         | "govern the nations" / ESV: "guide the nations" — Hebrew "nachah" = lead, guide. ESV's "guide" is more precise; the image is of a shepherd leading his flock, not a king issuing decrees from a throne.

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. Blessing has a missionary purpose (vv.1-2). God blesses you not so you
   can accumulate but so you can transmit. Every blessing received is a
   blessing meant to flow through you to others. The question is always:
   "That WHAT?"

2. God's just rule brings joy, not fear (v.4). Nations should SING FOR JOY
   because God judges righteously. Under human rulers, judgment is often
   corrupt. Under God's governance, everyone gets equity. That is cause for
   celebration.

3. The harvest is evidence of God's faithfulness (v.6). Physical provision
   points to spiritual reality. When God blesses materially, receive it as a
   sign of His covenant care — and share it as a witness.

4. The trajectory of faith is always outward (v.7). From "us" to "all the
   ends of the earth." A faith that stays turned inward has missed its
   purpose. Biblical blessing always pushes outward toward those who have
   not yet known God.

------------------------------------

David Guzik — Psalms 67 Written Commentary:
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/psalms-67/

BibleProject Podcast:
  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

68.notes:
Psalms - Chapter 68
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
A triumphal processional psalm — God arises and His enemies scatter; He is father to the fatherless, leads captives to freedom, marches through the wilderness, ascends on high receiving gifts; 'blessed be the Lord who daily loadeth us with benefits.'

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Multiple authors — primarily David (73 psalms attributed),
plus Asaph (12), Sons of Korah (11), Solomon (2), Moses (1),
Heman (1), Ethan (1), and anonymous. The Psalter was compiled over
approximately 1000 years and served as Israel's hymnal and prayer
book. The book is divided into five 'books' (1-41, 42-72, 73-89,
90-106, 107-150), paralleling the five books of Moses. Key themes:
worship, lament, praise, trust, kingship, creation, wisdom,
Messianic prophecy, and the full range of human emotion brought
before God.

Classification: Hymn of Praise / Processional Psalm / Royal Psalm
Attributed Author: David
Key Themes: God as warrior-king, the march of God through history,
care for the vulnerable, the ascension of God, gifts for men,
God's victorious procession, universal praise

Historical Context: One of the most complex and magnificent psalms
in the Psalter. Likely composed for the procession of the Ark of
the Covenant to Jerusalem (2 Samuel 6) or for use in subsequent
festival processions. The opening words echo Numbers 10:35: "Rise
up, LORD, and let thine enemies be scattered" — the ancient cry
when the Ark moved forward. The psalm traces God's march through
history: from Sinai (vv.7-8) through the wilderness to the
Promised Land, to the establishment of Zion as His dwelling place.
Paul quotes v.18 in Ephesians 4:8 and applies it to Christ's
ascension, making this psalm deeply Messianic.

Structure:
- God Arises: Enemies Scatter, Righteous Rejoice (vv.1-6)
- God's March Through History (vv.7-14)
- Zion Chosen Over Bashan (vv.15-18)
- Praise for Daily Benefits and Salvation (vv.19-23)
- The Processional Described (vv.24-27)
- Prayer for God's Power and Universal Praise (vv.28-35)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.1              | Numbers     | 10      | 35                 | "Rise up, LORD, and let thine enemies be scattered." The Ark-procession cry
v.5              | James       | 1       | 27                 | "Pure religion... is this, To visit the fatherless and widows." God's heart reflected
v.8              | Exodus      | 19      | 18                 | Sinai trembling at God's presence. Same theophany recalled
v.18             | Ephesians   | 4       | 8-10               | Paul's quotation and Christological interpretation of this verse
v.18             | Acts        | 2       | 33                 | "Having received of the Father the promise of the Holy Ghost, he hath shed forth this." Christ's ascension gifts
v.19             | Lamentations | 3       | 22-23              | "His compassions fail not. They are new every morning." Daily benefits/mercies
v.31             | Acts        | 8       | 27                 | The Ethiopian eunuch — early fulfillment of "Ethiopia shall stretch out her hands unto God."

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.4              | KJV         | "extol him that rideth upon the heavens" / ESV: "lift up a song to him who rides through the deserts" — Hebrew "araboth" can mean "clouds/heavens" or "deserts/plains." KJV follows one tradition (heavenly rider); ESV another (desert march). Both are defensible; the parallelism with v.33 ("rideth upon the heavens") supports KJV here.
v.13             | KJV         | "Though ye have lien among the pots" / ESV: "though you men lie among the sheepfolds" — Hebrew "shephattayim" is debated: ash-heaps/cooking pots (KJV) or sheepfolds (ESV). The point is the same: from lowly, dirty conditions to dove-like glory. Transformation from humiliation to beauty.
v.18             | KJV         | "received gifts for men" / ESV: "receiving gifts among men" — Paul in Eph 4:8 changes this to "gave gifts unto men." The Hebrew "laqach" = received/took. Paul interprets this as Christ receiving gifts from the Father in order to give them to His people.

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. The God who scatters enemies fathers the fatherless (vv.1,5). God's power
   and tenderness are not contradictions — they are companions. He uses His
   might to protect the vulnerable. His strength serves His compassion.

2. God daily loads us with benefits (v.19). Not weekly, not annually —
   DAILY. The challenge is awareness. Are you noticing what God loads onto
   you each day? Or are you too focused on what you lack to see what you
   have?

3. God holds the exits from death (v.20). The ultimate power. No disease, no
   enemy, no circumstance has the final word — God does. Death's door has a
   key, and God holds it.

4. The ascended Christ gives gifts to His people (v.18). Christ's ascension
   was not departure — it was enthronement for the purpose of gift-giving.
   The Spirit, spiritual gifts, grace — all flow from the ascended King to
   His people.

RESOURCES
------------------------------------
The Bible Project — Psalms Overview:
  https://bibleproject.com/explore/video/psalms/

David Guzik — Psalms 68 Written Commentary:
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/psalms-68/

BibleProject Podcast:
  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

========================================
Study Notes generated for personal Bible study use.
Translations referenced: KJV, ESV, ASV, NET, WEB
Commentary sources: Enduring Word (enduringword.com), Charles
Spurgeon (Public Domain)

69.ESV:
1. Save me, O God! For the waters have come up to my neck.
2. I sink in deep mire, where there is no foothold; I have come into deep waters, and the flood sweeps over me.
3. I am weary with my crying out; my throat is parched. My eyes grow dim with waiting for my God.
4. More in number than the hairs of my head are those who hate me without cause; mighty are those who would destroy me, those who attack me with lies. What I did not steal must I now restore?
5. O God, you know my folly; the wrongs I have done are not hidden from you.
6. Let not those who hope in you be put to shame through me, O Lord GOD of hosts; let not those who seek you be brought to dishonor through me, O God of Israel.
7. For it is for your sake that I have borne reproach, that dishonor has covered my face.
8. I have become a stranger to my brothers, an alien to my mother’s sons.
9. For zeal for your house has consumed me, and the reproaches of those who reproach you have fallen on me.
10. When I wept and humbled my soul with fasting, it became my reproach.
11. When I made sackcloth my clothing, I became a byword to them.
12. I am the talk of those who sit in the gate, and the drunkards make songs about me.
13. But as for me, my prayer is to you, O LORD. At an acceptable time, O God, in the abundance of your steadfast love answer me in your saving faithfulness.
14. Deliver me from sinking in the mire; let me be delivered from my enemies and from the deep waters.
15. Let not the flood sweep over me, or the deep swallow me up, or the pit close its mouth over me.
16. Answer me, O LORD, for your steadfast love is good; according to your abundant mercy, turn to me.
17. Hide not your face from your servant, for I am in distress; make haste to answer me.
18. Draw near to my soul, redeem me; ransom me because of my enemies!
19. You know my reproach, and my shame and my dishonor; my foes are all known to you.
20. Reproaches have broken my heart, so that I am in despair. I looked for pity, but there was none, and for comforters, but I found none.
21. They gave me poison for food, and for my thirst they gave me sour wine to drink.
22. Let their own table before them become a snare; and when they are at peace, let it become a trap.
23. Let their eyes be darkened, so that they cannot see, and make their loins tremble continually.
24. Pour out your indignation upon them, and let your burning anger overtake them.
25. May their camp be a desolation; let no one dwell in their tents.
26. For they persecute him whom you have struck down, and they recount the pain of those you have wounded.
27. Add to them punishment upon punishment; may they have no acquittal from you.
28. Let them be blotted out of the book of the living; let them not be enrolled among the righteous.
29. But I am afflicted and in pain; let your salvation, O God, set me on high!
30. I will praise the name of God with a song; I will magnify him with thanksgiving.
31. This will please the LORD more than an ox or a bull with horns and hoofs.
32. When the humble see it they will be glad; you who seek God, let your hearts revive.
33. For the LORD hears the needy and does not despise his own people who are prisoners.
34. Let heaven and earth praise him, the seas and everything that moves in them.
35. For God will save Zion and build up the cities of Judah, and people shall dwell there and possess it;
36. the offspring of his servants shall inherit it, and those who love his name shall dwell in it.

69.notes:
Psalms - Chapter 69
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
A Messianic lament — David sinks in deep mire, hated without cause, zealous for God's house; he prays for rescue and imprecates his enemies; quoted repeatedly in the NT as prophetic of Christ's suffering.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Multiple authors — primarily David (73 psalms attributed),
plus Asaph (12), Sons of Korah (11), Solomon (2), Moses (1),
Heman (1), Ethan (1), and anonymous. The Psalter was compiled over
approximately 1000 years and served as Israel's hymnal and prayer
book. The book is divided into five 'books' (1-41, 42-72, 73-89,
90-106, 107-150), paralleling the five books of Moses. Key themes:
worship, lament, praise, trust, kingship, creation, wisdom,
Messianic prophecy, and the full range of human emotion brought
before God.

Classification: Messianic Psalm / Individual Lament / Imprecatory
Attributed Author: David
Key Themes: Suffering for God's sake, rejection and reproach,
Messianic suffering, imprecation against enemies, praise over
sacrifice, hope for Zion's salvation

Historical Context: Psalm 69 is the second most-quoted psalm in
the New Testament (after Psalm 22). It is deeply Messianic — the
NT applies it to Christ's cleansing of the temple (v.9 — John
2:17), His suffering on the cross (v.21 — Matt 27:34,48), and
the judgment of Judas (v.25 — Acts 1:20). Set to "Shoshannim"
(Lilies), the psalm describes one who suffers for God's sake, is
rejected by family, finds no comfort, and is given gall and
vinegar. While David wrote from his own experience of persecution,
the Holy Spirit superintended language that would find ultimate
fulfillment in Christ. The psalm is one of the longest laments,
combining deep personal anguish, fierce imprecation, and exalted
praise.

Structure:
- Desperate Cry: Drowning in Suffering (vv.1-4)
- Confession and Concern for Others (vv.5-6)
- Reproach for God's Sake (vv.7-12)
- Prayer for Deliverance (vv.13-18)
- The Broken Heart (vv.19-21)
- Imprecation Against Enemies (vv.22-28)
- Praise and Hope (vv.29-36)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.4              | John        | 15      | 25                 | Jesus quotes: "They hated me without a cause."
v.9              | John        | 2       | 17                 | "The zeal of thine house hath eaten me up." Applied to Jesus cleansing the temple
v.9              | Romans      | 15      | 3                  | "The reproaches of them that reproached thee fell on me." Paul applies to Christ
v.21             | Matthew     | 27      | 34,48              | Vinegar and gall offered to Jesus at the cross
v.22             | Romans      | 11      | 9-10               | Paul quotes this imprecation regarding unbelieving Israel
v.25             | Acts        | 1       | 20                 | Peter quotes this regarding Judas: "Let his habitation be desolate."
v.28             | Exodus      | 32      | 32                 | 33; Revelation 3:5 — The "book of the living" — God's register of the righteous

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.1              | KJV         | "the waters are come in unto my soul" / ESV: "the waters have come up to my neck" — Hebrew "nephesh" can mean "soul" or "throat/neck." ESV reads it physically (water at neck-level); KJV reads it spiritually (waters invading the soul). Both are valid; the drowning imagery works either way.
v.4              | KJV         | "then I restored that which I took not away" / ESV: "What I did not steal must I now restore?" — David is forced to make restitution for crimes he didn't commit. Applied to Christ: He paid for sins He never committed.
v.21             | KJV         | "gall" / ESV: "poison" — Hebrew "rosh" = a poisonous plant. KJV retains the specific term; ESV generalizes. Matthew 27:34 uses "gall" (Greek "chole") connecting directly to this psalm.

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. Suffering for God's sake is real suffering (v.7). "For thy sake I have
   borne reproach." Following God does not immunize you from pain —
   sometimes it causes pain. But suffering for God's sake has meaning that
   random suffering does not.

2. The deepest pain is uncomforted pain (v.20). Looking for pity and finding
   none. Looking for comforters and finding none. If you know someone
   suffering, be present. Your presence may be the difference between
   survivable and unbearable grief.

3. Christ fulfilled this psalm in His passion. Every element of David's
   suffering finds its ultimate expression in Jesus: the hatred without
   cause, the zeal for God's house, the vinegar and gall, the broken heart.
   Our Savior knows the depth of human suffering from the inside.

4. Praise is better than sacrifice (vv.30-31). God doesn't want your
   performance — He wants your heart. A genuine song of praise from a broken
   person pleases God more than a thousand ritually perfect offerings from a
   cold heart.

RESOURCES
------------------------------------
The Bible Project — Psalms Overview:
  https://bibleproject.com/explore/video/psalms/

David Guzik — Psalms 69 Written Commentary:
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/psalms-69/

BibleProject Podcast:
  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

========================================
Study Notes generated for personal Bible study use.
Translations referenced: KJV, ESV, ASV, NET, WEB
Commentary sources: Enduring Word (enduringword.com), Charles
Spurgeon (Public Domain)

4. May all who seek you rejoice and be glad in you! May those who love your salvation say evermore, “God is great!”
