88.notes:
Psalms - Chapter 88
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
The darkest lament in the Psalter — Heman the Ezrahite cries day and night, feeling abandoned among the dead, overwhelmed by God's wrath, cut off from friends; uniquely, this psalm ends without resolution or hope.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Multiple authors — primarily David (73 psalms attributed),
plus Asaph (12), Sons of Korah (11), Solomon (2), Moses (1),
Heman (1), Ethan (1), and anonymous. The Psalter was compiled over
approximately 1000 years and served as Israel's hymnal and prayer
book. The book is divided into five 'books' (1-41, 42-72, 73-89,
90-106, 107-150), paralleling the five books of Moses. Key themes:
worship, lament, praise, trust, kingship, creation, wisdom,
Messianic prophecy, and the full range of human emotion brought
before God.

Classification: Individual Lament Psalm (the darkest psalm)
Attributed Author: Heman the Ezrahite (Sons of Korah)
Key Themes: Unrelieved suffering, divine wrath experienced,
isolation and abandonment, proximity to death, unanswered prayer,
faith persisting without resolution

Historical Context: Psalm 88 is the DARKEST psalm in the Psalter
— the only lament that ends without any note of hope, resolution,
or praise. It has dual attribution: "A Song or Psalm for the sons
of Korah" and "Maschil of Heman the Ezrahite." Heman was one of
David's chief musicians (1 Chronicles 6:33), known for wisdom
(1 Kings 4:31). The psalm describes someone who has been afflicted
"from my youth" (v.15) — this is not a temporary crisis but
lifelong suffering. Yet the very existence of the psalm IN
Scripture is its message: even the prayer that receives no visible
answer is STILL prayer. Faith can persist in total darkness. The
last word is "darkness" — and God honored this cry by preserving
it in His Word.

Structure:
- Cry to God Day and Night (vv.1-2)
- At Death's Door (vv.3-5)
- God Has Done This (vv.6-8)
- Daily Weeping and Reaching (v.9)
- Theological Protest: Can the Dead Praise? (vv.10-12)
- Morning Prayer and God's Rejection (vv.13-14)
- Lifelong Suffering (vv.15-17)
- Final Isolation: Darkness (v.18)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.1              | 1 Kings     | 4       | 31                 | Heman listed among the wisest men. Even wisdom doesn't prevent suffering
v.5              | Isaiah      | 38      | 18                 | "The grave cannot praise thee, death can not celebrate thee." Same theological argument
v.6              | Jonah       | 2       | 3                  | "Thou hadst cast me into the deep." Same experience of divine casting
v.8              | Job         | 19      | 13-19              | "He hath put my brethren far from me, and mine acquaintance are verily estranged from me." Nearly identical isolation
v.14             | Psalm       | 22      | 1                  | "My God, my God, why hast thou forsaken me?" Christ's cry from the cross — the ultimate fulfillment of this darkness
v.18             | Mark        | 15      | 33                 | "There was darkness over the whole land." The literal darkness at Calvary echoing this psalm

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.5              | KJV         | "Free among the dead" / ESV: "like one set loose among the dead" — Hebrew "chophshi" = free, released. Paradoxically, freedom here means being released from God's attention — set loose in death, unremembered. A terrifying "freedom."
v.15             | KJV         | "I suffer thy terrors I am distracted" / ESV: "I suffer your terrors; I am helpless" — Hebrew "afunah" is rare and uncertain. Meanings proposed: distracted, helpless, numb, in despair. All capture the disorientation of prolonged, divine-sourced suffering.
v.18             | KJV         | "mine acquaintance into darkness" / ESV: "my companions have become darkness" — The final word. ESV's rendering is even more stark: darkness is not just where friends have gone but what they have BECOME. Everything is darkness.

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. Faith can persist in total darkness (v.1). The psalmist calls God "God of
   MY salvation" while experiencing no salvation. This is the deepest faith
   — trust without evidence, hope without feeling, persistence without
   reward.

2. It is acceptable to end a prayer in darkness (v.18). Not every prayer
   resolves happily. Not every lament ends in praise. Scripture validates
   the experience of unresolved suffering. You don't need to manufacture
   false hope. You can end in darkness and still be heard.

3. Isolation in suffering is real (v.8). If friends have withdrawn, if you
   feel like a horror to others, this psalm says: you are not alone in
   feeling alone. Heman felt it. Christ experienced it on the cross.

4. The psalm's existence IS the answer. God did not answer Heman's prayer
   within the psalm — but He preserved it in Scripture for 3000 years. The
   prayer is the testimony. Praying in darkness is its own act of faith,
   even without resolution.

RESOURCES
------------------------------------
The Bible Project — Psalms Overview:
  https://bibleproject.com/explore/video/psalms/

David Guzik — Psalms 88 Written Commentary:
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/psalms-88/

BibleProject Podcast:
  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

Spurgeon (Public Domain)

89.ESV:
1. I will sing of the steadfast love of the LORD forever; with my mouth I will make known your faithfulness to all generations.

4. ‘I will establish your offspring forever, and build your throne for all generations.’” Selah
5. Let the heavens praise your wonders, O LORD, your faithfulness in the assembly of the holy ones!
6. For who in the skies can be compared to the LORD? Who among the heavenly beings is like the LORD,
7. a God greatly to be feared in the council of the holy ones, and awesome above all who are around him?

17. For you are the glory of their strength; by your favor our horn is exalted.
18. For our shield belongs to the LORD, our king to the Holy One of Israel.

22. The enemy shall not outwit him; the wicked shall not humble him.
23. I will crush his foes before him and strike down those who hate him.
24. My faithfulness and my steadfast love shall be with him, and in my name shall his horn be exalted.
25. I will set his hand on the sea and his right hand on the rivers.
26. He shall cry to me, ‘You are my Father, my God, and the Rock of my salvation.’
27. And I will make him the firstborn, the highest of the kings of the earth.
28. My steadfast love I will keep for him forever, and my covenant will stand firm for him.
29. I will establish his offspring forever and his throne as the days of the heavens.
30. If his children forsake my law and do not walk according to my rules,
31. if they violate my statutes and do not keep my commandments,
32. then I will punish their transgression with the rod and their iniquity with stripes,
33. but I will not remove from him my steadfast love or be false to my faithfulness.
34. I will not violate my covenant or alter the word that went forth from my lips.
35. Once for all I have sworn by my holiness; I will not lie to David.
36. His offspring shall endure forever, his throne as long as the sun before me.
37. Like the moon it shall be established forever, a faithful witness in the skies.” Selah
38. But now you have cast off and rejected; you are full of wrath against your anointed.
39. You have renounced the covenant with your servant; you have defiled his crown in the dust.
40. You have breached all his walls; you have laid his strongholds in ruins.
41. All who pass by plunder him; he has become the scorn of his neighbors.
42. You have exalted the right hand of his foes; you have made all his enemies rejoice.
43. You have also turned back the edge of his sword, and you have not made him stand in battle.
44. You have made his splendor to cease and cast his throne to the ground.
45. You have cut short the days of his youth; you have covered him with shame. Selah

47. Remember how short my time is! For what vanity you have created all the children of man!
48. What man can live and never see death? Who can deliver his soul from the power of Sheol? Selah

50. Remember, O Lord, how your servants are mocked, and how I bear in my heart the insults of all the many nations,
51. with which your enemies mock, O LORD, with which they mock the footsteps of your anointed.

41. All who pass by have robbed him; he has become an object of disdain to his neighbors.
42. You have allowed his adversaries to be victorious and all his enemies to rejoice.
43. You turn back his sword from the adversary and have not sustained him in battle.
44. You have brought to an end his splendor and have knocked his throne to the ground.
45. You have cut short his youth and have covered him with shame. (Selah)
46. How long, O Lord, will this last? Will you remain hidden forever? Will your anger continue to burn like fire?
47. Take note of my brief lifespan. Why do you make all people so mortal?
48. No man can live on without experiencing death or deliver his life from the power of Sheol. (Selah)
49. Where are your earlier faithful deeds, O Lord, the ones performed in accordance with your reliable oath to David?
50. Take note, O Lord, of the way your servants are taunted and of how I must bear so many insults from people.
51. Your enemies, O Lord, hurl insults; they insult your chosen king as they dog his footsteps.
52. The Lord deserves praise forevermore! We agree! We agree!

89.notes:
Psalms - Chapter 89
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
A royal lament closing Book III — Ethan celebrates God's covenant with David ('his seed shall endure forever'), praises God's faithfulness and power, then agonizes because the anointed king appears rejected and humiliated.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Multiple authors — primarily David (73 psalms attributed),
plus Asaph (12), Sons of Korah (11), Solomon (2), Moses (1),
Heman (1), Ethan (1), and anonymous. The Psalter was compiled over
approximately 1000 years and served as Israel's hymnal and prayer
book. The book is divided into five 'books' (1-41, 42-72, 73-89,
90-106, 107-150), paralleling the five books of Moses. Key themes:
worship, lament, praise, trust, kingship, creation, wisdom,
Messianic prophecy, and the full range of human emotion brought
before God.

Classification: Royal / Covenant / Lament Psalm
Attributed Author: Ethan the Ezrahite
Key Themes: God's covenant faithfulness (hesed), the Davidic
covenant, God's incomparability, the tension between divine
promises and present suffering, the apparent failure of God's oath

Historical Context: This massive psalm by Ethan the Ezrahite
(1 Kings 4:31 — one of Israel's wisest men) closes Book III of
the Psalter. It begins with exuberant praise of God's hesed and
faithfulness, then recounts the Davidic covenant in detail
(2 Samuel 7), and then — shockingly — laments that the covenant
seems BROKEN (vv.38-51). The psalm likely dates to the Babylonian
exile or shortly before, when the Davidic dynasty was destroyed
or severely threatened. The agonizing tension: God SWORE to David
an eternal throne, yet the throne lies in the dust. How can God's
oath fail? The psalm ends not with resolution but with a
desperate question (v.49) and a brief doxology (v.52) closing
Book III.

Structure:
- Praise for God's Hesed and Faithfulness (vv.1-4)
- Hymn to God's Incomparability (vv.5-18)
- The Davidic Covenant Rehearsed (vv.19-37)
- The Apparent Failure of the Covenant (vv.38-45)
- Lament: How Long? (vv.46-51)
- Doxology Closing Book III (v.52)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.3-4            | 2 Samuel    | 7       | 12-16              | The Davidic covenant. The basis of everything in this psalm
v.9              | Mark        | 4       | 39                 | Jesus stilling the storm: "Peace, be still." Fulfilling what only God can do (Ps 89:9)
v.20             | 1 Samuel    | 16      | 13                 | David's anointing with oil
v.26             | Romans      | 8       | 15                 | "Abba, Father." Jesus as the Son who calls God "Father" (fulfilling v.26)
v.27             | Colossians  | 1       | 15,18              | Christ as "firstborn" — the highest of kings, fulfilling v.27
v.34             | Romans      | 11      | 29                 | "The gifts and calling of God are without repentance." God's covenant stands
v.52             | Psalm       | 41      | 13                 | ; 72:18-19; 106:48 — The doxologies closing each of the five books of the Psalter

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.10             | KJV         | "Thou hast broken Rahab in pieces" / ESV: "You crushed Rahab like a carcass" — "Rahab" = Egypt (poetic name), or the mythological chaos-sea-monster. God crushed Egypt/chaos at the Exodus.
v.19             | KJV         | "thy holy one" / ESV: "your godly one" — Hebrew "chasid" = the one to whom the vision was given. Could refer to Nathan the prophet or to David himself receiving God's covenant promise.
v.52             | KJV         | This verse is the doxology closing Book III of the Psalter. It is not part of Ethan's psalm proper but an editorial addition marking the book division: "Blessed be the LORD for evermore. Amen, and Amen."

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. You can celebrate God's faithfulness AND question His apparent failure
   (vv.1-4, 38-51). This psalm holds both together without resolving the
   tension. Faith does not require understanding. Praise and lament can
   coexist.

2. God's covenant is unconditional even when discipline is real (vv.33-34).
   He will punish disobedience but NEVER break His word. Discipline is not
   abandonment. Consequences are not covenant annulment.

3. The answer to this psalm is Christ. The Davidic throne DID seem to fail.
   But Jesus — born of David's line — sits on an eternal throne. What seemed
   like covenant failure was covenant delay. God's timing exceeds human
   timelines.

4. "Where are your former lovingkindnesses?" (v.49) is a prayer for all who
   feel God has forgotten His promises. Hold Him to His word. Remind Him of
   what He swore. This is not irreverence — it is covenant partnership.

  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/psalms-89/

90.notes:
Psalms - Chapter 90
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
A psalm of Moses opening Book IV — 'LORD, thou hast been our dwelling place in all generations'; God is eternal, man is dust; 'teach us to number our days, that we may apply our hearts unto wisdom.'

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Multiple authors — primarily David (73 psalms attributed),
plus Asaph (12), Sons of Korah (11), Solomon (2), Moses (1),
Heman (1), Ethan (1), and anonymous. The Psalter was compiled over
approximately 1000 years and served as Israel's hymnal and prayer
book. The book is divided into five 'books' (1-41, 42-72, 73-89,
90-106, 107-150), paralleling the five books of Moses. Key themes:
worship, lament, praise, trust, kingship, creation, wisdom,
Messianic prophecy, and the full range of human emotion brought
before God.

Classification: Community Lament / Wisdom Psalm
Attributed Author: Moses (the only psalm attributed to Moses)
Key Themes: God's eternity vs. human brevity, the brevity of life,
sin and divine wrath, numbering our days, prayer for God's mercy
and the establishment of human work

Historical Context: Psalm 90 opens Book IV of the Psalter (90-106)
and is the oldest psalm in the collection — attributed to Moses,
making it potentially 3400+ years old. As the man who spent 40
years watching a generation die in the wilderness (Numbers
14:29-35), Moses had unparalleled experience with human mortality.
The psalm's meditation on the brevity of life (70-80 years, v.10)
and God's wrath consuming sinful humanity may directly reflect the
wilderness generation's slow death. Yet it is not despairing —
it moves from sober recognition of mortality to prayer for wisdom,
mercy, gladness, and the establishment of human work. The phrase
"from everlasting to everlasting, thou art God" (v.2) is one of
Scripture's grandest theological declarations.

Structure:
- God as Eternal Dwelling Place (vv.1-2)
- Human Mortality Under God's Authority (vv.3-6)
- Sin and Divine Wrath (vv.7-11)
- Prayer for Wisdom (v.12)
- Prayer for Mercy, Gladness, and Purpose (vv.13-17)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.1              | Deuteronomy | 33      | 27                 | "The eternal God is thy refuge, and underneath are the everlasting arms." Moses' final words
v.2              | 2 Peter     | 3       | 8                  | "One day is with the Lord as a thousand years, and a thousand years as one day." Echoing v.4
v.3              | Genesis     | 3       | 19                 | "Dust thou art, and unto dust shalt thou return." Moses connects to his own writing
v.4              | 2 Peter     | 3       | 8                  | Peter directly cites this verse regarding God's perspective on time
v.10             | James       | 4       | 14                 | "For what is your life? It is even a vapour, that appeareth for a little time, and then vanisheth away."
v.12             | Ephesians   | 5       | 15-16              | "Redeeming the time, because the days are evil." The NT application of numbering days
v.17             | 1 Corinthians | 15      | 58                 | "Your labour is not in vain in the Lord." The NT promise that work established by God lasts

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.3              | KJV         | "Thou turnest man to destruction" / ESV: "You return man to dust" — Hebrew "dakka" = dust, crushed substance. ESV is more literal and connects directly to Genesis 3:19. God returns humanity to its origin — dust.
v.9              | KJV         | "we spend our years as a tale that is told" / ESV: "we bring our years to an end like a sigh" — Hebrew "hegeh" = a moan, a murmur, a sigh. ESV's "sigh" captures the brevity and sadness better. Life ends with a whisper, not a bang.
v.10             | KJV         | "threescore years and ten" / ESV: "seventy" — Same number, different expression. KJV preserves the archaic beauty; ESV is plain but clear. The observation: even in Moses' time, human lifespan was recognizably brief.

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. God is your dwelling place across generations (v.1). Before you were
   born, God was home. After you die, God remains. Your true address is not
   geographical but theological — God Himself is where you live.

2. Life is breathtakingly brief (v.10). 70-80 years. Even the strong ones
   are toil. This is not morbid but urgent — if life is short, what will you
   do with it? Brevity produces focus.

3. Number your days (v.12). The one who knows life's limit lives with
   wisdom. Count backward from the end. What matters? What doesn't? Let
   mortality teach you priority.

4. Ask God to establish your work (v.17). Human effort without God is
   temporary. But work committed to God, established by God, can outlast the
   worker. Pray this daily: "God, make my work last beyond my life."

5. Even Moses needed morning mercy (v.14). "Satisfy us EARLY with thy
   mercy." Every day begins with a need for fresh grace. No one outgrows the
   need for daily mercy — not even the man who spoke with God face to face.

RESOURCES
------------------------------------
The Bible Project — Psalms Overview:
  https://bibleproject.com/explore/video/psalms/

David Guzik — Psalms 90 Written Commentary:
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/psalms-90/

BibleProject Podcast:
  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

4. He will cover you with his pinions, and under his wings you will find refuge; his faithfulness is a shield and buckler.
5. You will not fear the terror of the night, nor the arrow that flies by day,
6. nor the pestilence that stalks in darkness, nor the destruction that wastes at noonday.
7. A thousand may fall at your side, ten thousand at your right hand, but it will not come near you.
8. You will only look with your eyes and see the recompense of the wicked.

91.notes:
Psalms - Chapter 91
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
A psalm of trust and divine protection — 'He that dwelleth in the secret place of the most High shall abide under the shadow of the Almighty'; no plague, no evil shall befall; angels guard; God promises rescue and long life.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Multiple authors — primarily David (73 psalms attributed),
plus Asaph (12), Sons of Korah (11), Solomon (2), Moses (1),
Heman (1), Ethan (1), and anonymous. The Psalter was compiled over
approximately 1000 years and served as Israel's hymnal and prayer
book. The book is divided into five 'books' (1-41, 42-72, 73-89,
90-106, 107-150), paralleling the five books of Moses. Key themes:
worship, lament, praise, trust, kingship, creation, wisdom,
Messianic prophecy, and the full range of human emotion brought
before God.

Classification: Trust / Confidence Psalm
Attributed Author: Anonymous (Jewish tradition: Moses)
Key Themes: Divine protection, dwelling in God's presence, freedom
from fear, angelic guardianship, treading on enemies, God's
personal promises to the faithful

Historical Context: Psalm 91 has no superscription. Jewish tradition
attributes it to Moses (pairing it with Psalm 90). It is the great
"protection psalm" — the most comprehensive statement of divine
shelter in all of Scripture. Satan quoted vv.11-12 during Christ's
temptation (Matthew 4:6), attempting to twist the promise into
presumption. The psalm uses four divine names: Most High (Elyon),
Almighty (Shaddai), LORD (Yahweh), and God (Elohim) — indicating
comprehensive, covenant protection from every angle. The psalm
moves between voices: the speaker (vv.1-2,9), addressing the
faithful (vv.3-8,10-13), and God speaking directly (vv.14-16).
It was likely used liturgically as a blessing pronounced over
worshippers or warriors.

Structure:
- The Secret Place of the Most High (vv.1-2)
- Deliverance from Dangers (vv.3-8)
- The Condition: Making God Your Dwelling (v.9)
- Angelic Protection (vv.10-13)
- God Speaks Directly (vv.14-16)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.1              | Psalm       | 27      | 5                  | "In the secret of his tabernacle shall he hide me." Same secret place concept
v.4              | Ruth        | 2       | 12                 | "Under whose wings thou art come to trust." Same wing-protection imagery
v.11             | Matthew     | 4       | 6                  | Satan quotes this verse to tempt Jesus
v.11             | Hebrews     | 1       | 14                 | "Are they not all ministering spirits, sent forth to minister for them who shall be heirs of salvation?" Angels serving believers
v.13             | Luke        | 10      | 19                 | Jesus: "I give unto you power to tread on serpents and scorpions." Authority over evil
v.13             | Romans      | 16      | 20                 | "The God of peace shall bruise Satan under your feet shortly."

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.3              | KJV         | "noisome pestilence" / ESV: "deadly pestilence" — "Noisome" (KJV) = harmful, noxious (archaic English). ESV's "deadly" is clearer. The Hebrew "deber havvot" = plague/ pestilence of destruction.
v.5-6            | KJV         | Four dangers listed: terror by night (sudden attack), arrow by day (deliberate attack), pestilence in darkness (hidden disease), destruction at noonday (open calamity). Together they cover all 24 hours and all types of threat.
v.13             | KJV         | "the dragon" / ESV: "the serpent" — Hebrew "tannin" = serpent, dragon, sea monster. Applied spiritually: the believer has authority to tread on every form of evil power.

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. Protection is for those who DWELL, not visit (v.1). The secret place is
   not for tourists but for residents. Consistent, intimate communion with
   God is the condition for the promises that follow. Do you live there or
   just visit?

2. God's protection covers all angles (vv.5-6). Night and day, secret and
   open, disease and violence — every type of threat is addressed. There is
   no angle from which danger can approach that God has not anticipated.

3. God's promises are not a license for presumption (vv.11-12). Satan used
   these words to tempt Jesus into recklessness. Trust is not testing. Faith
   walks in God's ways (v.11 — "in all thy WAYS"); it does not manufacture
   crises to prove God.

4. "I will be with him IN trouble" (v.15). Not "I will keep him FROM
   trouble" but "I will be with him IN it." The promise is presence, not
   exemption. God doesn't promise a trouble-free life but a never-alone
   life.

RESOURCES
------------------------------------
The Bible Project — Psalms Overview:
  https://bibleproject.com/explore/video/psalms/

David Guzik — Psalms 91 Written Commentary:
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/psalms-91/

BibleProject Podcast:
  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

2. to declare your steadfast love in the morning, and your faithfulness by night,
3. to the music of the lute and the harp, to the melody of the lyre.
4. For you, O LORD, have made me glad by your work; at the works of your hands I sing for joy.
5. How great are your works, O LORD! Your thoughts are very deep!
6. The stupid man cannot know; the fool cannot understand this:
7. that though the wicked sprout like grass and all evildoers flourish, they are doomed to destruction forever;
8. but you, O LORD, are on high forever.
9. For behold, your enemies, O LORD, for behold, your enemies shall perish; all evildoers shall be scattered.
10. But you have exalted my horn like that of the wild ox; you have poured over me fresh oil.
11. My eyes have seen the downfall of my enemies; my ears have heard the doom of my evil assailants.
12. The righteous flourish like the palm tree and grow like a cedar in Lebanon.
13. They are planted in the house of the LORD; they flourish in the courts of our God.
14. They still bear fruit in old age; they are ever full of sap and green,
15. to declare that the LORD is upright; he is my rock, and there is no unrighteousness in him.

92.notes:
Psalms - Chapter 92
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
A Sabbath psalm of praise — it is good to declare God's lovingkindness every morning; the wicked spring up like grass but perish; the righteous flourish like the palm tree, still bearing fruit in old age.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Multiple authors — primarily David (73 psalms attributed),
plus Asaph (12), Sons of Korah (11), Solomon (2), Moses (1),
Heman (1), Ethan (1), and anonymous. The Psalter was compiled over
approximately 1000 years and served as Israel's hymnal and prayer
book. The book is divided into five 'books' (1-41, 42-72, 73-89,
90-106, 107-150), paralleling the five books of Moses. Key themes:
worship, lament, praise, trust, kingship, creation, wisdom,
Messianic prophecy, and the full range of human emotion brought
before God.

Classification: Thanksgiving / Wisdom Psalm
Attributed Author: Anonymous (superscription: "A Psalm or Song for
the sabbath day")
Key Themes: The goodness of thanksgiving, morning lovingkindness
and nightly faithfulness, the fate of the wicked vs. flourishing of
the righteous, fruitfulness in old age, God as upright rock

Historical Context: The only psalm explicitly designated for the
Sabbath day. The Mishnah confirms it was sung by the Levites in
the temple every Sabbath. Its themes fit the Sabbath perfectly:
resting in God's faithfulness, reflecting on His works, and
trusting His justice. The contrast between the wicked (who
"sprout like grass" — fast growth, quick death) and the righteous
(who "flourish like the palm tree" — slow growth, long life) is
central wisdom theology. The psalm's final promise — fruitfulness
in old age — connects beautifully to the Sabbath theme of
sustained vitality through divine rest.

Structure:
- The Goodness of Praise (vv.1-3)
- Joy in God's Works (vv.4-5)
- The Fool Cannot Understand (vv.6-7)
- God Eternal, Enemies Perish (vv.8-9)
- Personal Exaltation (vv.10-11)
- The Righteous Flourish (vv.12-15)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.1              | Hebrews     | 13      | 15                 | "Let us offer the sacrifice of praise to God continually." Praise as ongoing sacrifice
v.5              | Romans      | 11      | 33                 | "O the depth of the riches both of the wisdom and knowledge of God!" God's deep thoughts
v.7              | Psalm       | 73      | 17-19              | Same wisdom: wicked flourish temporarily but perish permanently
v.12             | Psalm       | 1       | 3                  | "He shall be like a tree planted by the rivers of water." Same tree-flourishing image
v.14             | Isaiah      | 46      | 4                  | "Even to your old age I am he; and even to hoar hairs will I carry you." God's care in old age
v.15             | Deuteronomy | 32      | 4                  | "He is the Rock, his work is perfect." Same "Rock" declaration

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.10             | KJV         | "like the horn of an unicorn" / ESV: "like that of the wild ox" — Hebrew "re'em" = a wild ox or aurochs (now extinct). KJV's "unicorn" reflects older translation tradition. ESV is more accurate: the horn of the wild ox represented formidable strength.
v.14             | KJV         | "fat and flourishing" / ESV: "full of sap and green" — Hebrew "deshenim" = fat, rich, fertile. Both capture the image of continued vitality — no drying out, no withering in old age.

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. Declare God's character morning and evening (v.2). Begin the day
   announcing His love; end the day acknowledging His faithfulness. This
   frames every 24 hours in God's character.

2. The wicked sprout fast; the righteous grow slow (vv.7,12). Don't be
   deceived by quick growth. Grass rises overnight and dies tomorrow. Cedars
   take decades but last centuries. Be a cedar, not grass.

3. Fruitfulness has no age limit (v.14). If you are planted in God's house,
   your most fruitful years may be ahead regardless of your age. Spiritual
   vitality does not depend on physical youth.

4. The Sabbath is for thanksgiving and reflection (v.1). Rest is not
   emptiness — it is filled with praise, gratitude, and wonder at God's
   works. True Sabbath is active worship, not mere inactivity.

RESOURCES
------------------------------------
The Bible Project — Psalms Overview:
  https://bibleproject.com/explore/video/psalms/

David Guzik — Psalms 92 Written Commentary:
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/psalms-92/

BibleProject Podcast:
  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

========================================
Study Notes generated for personal Bible study use.
Translations referenced: KJV, ESV, ASV, NET, WEB
Commentary sources: Enduring Word (enduringword.com), Charles
Spurgeon (Public Domain)

93.notes:
Psalms - Chapter 93
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
An enthronement psalm — 'The LORD reigneth, he is clothed with majesty'; His throne is established from of old; though floods lift their voice, the LORD on high is mightier than the mighty waters.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Multiple authors — primarily David (73 psalms attributed),
plus Asaph (12), Sons of Korah (11), Solomon (2), Moses (1),
Heman (1), Ethan (1), and anonymous. The Psalter was compiled over
approximately 1000 years and served as Israel's hymnal and prayer
book. The book is divided into five 'books' (1-41, 42-72, 73-89,
90-106, 107-150), paralleling the five books of Moses. Key themes:
worship, lament, praise, trust, kingship, creation, wisdom,
Messianic prophecy, and the full range of human emotion brought
before God.

Classification: Enthronement / Kingship Psalm
Attributed Author: Anonymous
Key Themes: The LORD reigns, divine majesty, God's eternal throne,
God mightier than chaos waters, the sureness of God's testimonies,
holiness befitting His house

Historical Context: Psalm 93 begins a series of "enthronement
psalms" (93-99) celebrating God's kingship over creation. These
psalms were likely used liturgically to celebrate God's sovereignty
— possibly during the Feast of Tabernacles or New Year festivals.
"The LORD reigneth" (Yahweh malak) is the theme declaration. The
psalm is brief (5 verses) but cosmic in scope: God's kingship is
displayed in His majesty (v.1), eternity (v.2), power over chaos
(vv.3-4), and the reliability of His word (v.5). The "floods"
(vv.3-4) represent both literal ocean power and the metaphorical
forces of chaos and opposition — all subordinate to God's might.

Structure:
- The LORD Reigns in Majesty (v.1)
- His Throne is Eternal (v.2)
- The Floods Rise but God is Mightier (vv.3-4)
- His Testimonies are Sure (v.5)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.1              | Psalm       | 97      | 1                  | ; 99:1 — "The LORD reigneth." Same enthronement declaration opening multiple psalms
v.2              | Psalm       | 90      | 2                  | "From everlasting to everlasting, thou art God." God's eternal nature
v.4              | Psalm       | 89      | 9                  | "Thou rulest the raging of the sea: when the waves thereof arise, thou stillest them."
v.4              | Mark        | 4       | 39                 | Jesus calming the storm: "Peace, be still." Demonstrating divine authority over waters
v.5              | Psalm       | 19      | 7                  | "The testimony of the LORD is sure, making wise the simple." Same "sure" testimonies

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.1              | KJV         | "clothed with strength, wherewith he hath girded himself" / ESV: "he has put on strength as his belt" — Military imagery: a king-warrior girding for battle. God is not merely decorated with strength — He is equipped for action.
v.3              | KJV         | "The floods have lifted up" — repeated three times for emphatic effect. The threefold repetition mimics the relentless crashing of waves — only to be overwhelmed by God's superior might in v.4.

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. The LORD reigns NOW (v.1). Not "will reign someday" but reigns THIS
   MOMENT. Whatever chaos you face, it exists within the reign of God. He is
   on His throne — robed, girded, ruling.

2. God is mightier than whatever overwhelms you (v.4). The floods lift their
   voice — loudly, repeatedly, relentlessly. But God on high is MIGHTIER.
   Your overwhelming circumstance is not God's match. He exceeds every
   flood.

3. God's Word is as sure as His power (v.5). The God who overpowers oceans
   also speaks reliable words. His testimonies are as unshakeable as His
   throne. What He says, He means. What He promises, He does.

RESOURCES
------------------------------------
The Bible Project — Psalms Overview:
  https://bibleproject.com/explore/video/psalms/

David Guzik — Psalms 93 Written Commentary:
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/psalms-93/

BibleProject Podcast:
  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

Study Notes generated for personal Bible study use.
Translations referenced: KJV, ESV, ASV, NET, WEB
Commentary sources: Enduring Word (enduringword.com), Charles
Spurgeon (Public Domain)

94.ESV:
1. O LORD, God of vengeance, O God of vengeance, shine forth!
2. Rise up, O judge of the earth; repay to the proud what they deserve!
3. O LORD, how long shall the wicked, how long shall the wicked exult?
4. They pour out their arrogant words; all the evildoers boast.
5. They crush your people, O LORD, and afflict your heritage.
6. They kill the widow and the sojourner, and murder the fatherless;
7. and they say, “The LORD does not see; the God of Jacob does not perceive.”
8. Understand, O dullest of the people! Fools, when will you be wise?
9. He who planted the ear, does he not hear? He who formed the eye, does he not see?
10. He who disciplines the nations, does he not rebuke? He who teaches man knowledge—
11. the LORD—knows the thoughts of man, that they are but a breath.
12. Blessed is the man whom you discipline, O LORD, and whom you teach out of your law,
13. to give him rest from days of trouble, until a pit is dug for the wicked.
14. For the LORD will not forsake his people; he will not abandon his heritage;
15. for justice will return to the righteous, and all the upright in heart will follow it.
16. Who rises up for me against the wicked? Who stands up for me against evildoers?
17. If the LORD had not been my help, my soul would soon have lived in the land of silence.
18. When I thought, “My foot slips,” your steadfast love, O LORD, held me up.
19. When the cares of my heart are many, your consolations cheer my soul.
20. Can wicked rulers be allied with you, those who frame injustice by statute?
21. They band together against the life of the righteous and condemn the innocent to death.
22. But the LORD has become my stronghold, and my God the rock of my refuge.
23. He will bring back on them their iniquity and wipe them out for their wickedness; the LORD our God will wipe them out.

4. How long shall they utter and speak hard things? and all the workers of iniquity boast themselves?
5. They break in pieces thy people, O LORD, and afflict thine heritage.
6. They slay the widow and the stranger, and murder the fatherless.
7. Yet they say, The LORD shall not see, neither shall the God of Jacob regard it.
8. Understand, ye brutish among the people: and ye fools, when will ye be wise?
9. He that planted the ear, shall he not hear? he that formed the eye, shall he not see?
10. He that chastiseth the heathen, shall not he correct? he that teacheth man knowledge, shall not he know?
11. The LORD knoweth the thoughts of man, that they are vanity.
12. Blessed is the man whom thou chastenest, O LORD, and teachest him out of thy law;
13. That thou mayest give him rest from the days of adversity, until the pit be digged for the wicked.
14. For the LORD will not cast off his people, neither will he forsake his inheritance.
15. But judgment shall return unto righteousness: and all the upright in heart shall follow it.
16. Who will rise up for me against the evildoers? or who will stand up for me against the workers of iniquity?
17. Unless the LORD had been my help, my soul had almost dwelt in silence.
18. When I said, My foot slippeth; thy mercy, O LORD, held me up.
19. In the multitude of my thoughts within me thy comforts delight my soul.
20. Shall the throne of iniquity have fellowship with thee, which frameth mischief by a law?
21. They gather themselves together against the soul of the righteous, and condemn the innocent blood.
22. But the LORD is my defence; and my God is the rock of my refuge.
23. And he shall bring upon them their own iniquity, and shall cut them off in their own wickedness; yea, the LORD our God shall cut them off.

20. Shall the throne of wickedness have fellowship with you, which brings about mischief by statute?
21. They gather themselves together against the soul of the righteous, and condemn the innocent blood.
22. But Yahweh has been my high tower, my God, the rock of my refuge.
23. He has brought on them their own iniquity, and will cut them off in their own wickedness. Yahweh, our God, will cut them off.
94.notes:
Psalms - Chapter 94
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
A lament calling for divine justice — 'O LORD God, to whom vengeance belongeth, show thyself'; how long will the wicked triumph and crush God's people? The LORD will not forsake His inheritance or let the unjust prevail.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Multiple authors — primarily David (73 psalms attributed),
plus Asaph (12), Sons of Korah (11), Solomon (2), Moses (1),
Heman (1), Ethan (1), and anonymous. The Psalter was compiled over
approximately 1000 years and served as Israel's hymnal and prayer
book. The book is divided into five 'books' (1-41, 42-72, 73-89,
90-106, 107-150), paralleling the five books of Moses. Key themes:
worship, lament, praise, trust, kingship, creation, wisdom,
Messianic prophecy, and the full range of human emotion brought
before God.

Classification: Community Lament / Wisdom Psalm
Attributed Author: Anonymous
Key Themes: God of vengeance, cry against oppressors, the folly
of thinking God cannot see, blessedness of divine discipline,
God's comfort amid anxious thoughts, God as defense and refuge

Historical Context: This psalm follows the enthronement psalms
(93, 95-99) that declare "The LORD reigns," yet addresses the
painful reality that wicked oppressors still crush God's people.
If God reigns, why do the wicked triumph? The psalm answers: God
DOES see (vv.9-10), He WILL judge (vv.23), and meanwhile He
disciplines and comforts His own (vv.12-13,19). The oppressors
described (killing widows, strangers, fatherless — v.6) and their
legislative injustice ("frameth mischief by a law" — v.20) suggest
a period of institutional corruption — possibly the late monarchy
or exile. Paul quotes v.11 in 1 Corinthians 3:20.

Structure:
- Cry for God to Avenge (vv.1-3)
- Description of Oppressors (vv.4-7)
- Warning to the Foolish (vv.8-11)
- Blessedness of Discipline (vv.12-15)
- Personal Testimony of God's Help (vv.16-19)
- Corrupt Thrones Cannot Partner with God (vv.20-21)
- Confidence in God's Justice (vv.22-23)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.9              | Proverbs    | 20      | 12                 | "The hearing ear, and the seeing eye, the LORD hath made even both of them."
v.11             | 1 Corinthians | 3       | 20                 | Paul quotes: "The Lord knoweth the thoughts of the wise, that they are vain."
v.12             | Hebrews     | 12      | 5-6                | "Whom the Lord loveth he chasteneth." Discipline as evidence of love
v.14             | Romans      | 11      | 1-2                | "God hath not cast away his people." Same assurance
v.17             | Psalm       | 115     | 17                 | "The dead praise not the LORD, neither any that go down into silence." The "silence" David nearly entered
v.19             | Philippians | 4       | 6-7                | "The peace of God... shall keep your hearts and minds." NT version of God's comforts amid anxious thoughts

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.1              | KJV         | "O LORD God, to whom vengeance belongeth" / ESV: "O LORD, God of vengeance" — "Vengeance" (naqam) is not vindictiveness but righteous retribution — setting wrongs right. It belongs EXCLUSIVELY to God (Rom 12:19). He alone has the right and the impartiality to avenge.
v.20             | KJV         | "which frameth mischief by a law" / ESV: "those who frame injustice by statute" — Legalized injustice: institutionalized oppression. The most dangerous evil is evil given legal standing. The psalmist asks: can such legislators have fellowship with God? No.

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. God created perception — of course He perceives (v.9). The atheist's
   assumption that God doesn't see is absurd. He made ears; He hears. He
   made eyes; He sees. Your oppressor is observed. Every act is witnessed.

2. Divine discipline is blessing in disguise (v.12). If God is correcting
   you, He values you. Uncontested sin means neglect. Correction means
   investment. Receive discipline as education.

3. God's comfort meets anxious thoughts (v.19). You will have many thoughts
   — worried, spinning, overwhelming. God does not promise to eliminate them
   but to insert His comforts INTO them. His consolations delight the soul
   even amid the storm.

4. Legalized injustice will not stand (v.20). When governments "frame
   mischief by law," they do not thereby gain God's endorsement. No statute
   can make injustice just. God opposes systemic evil just as He opposes
   personal evil.

95.notes:
Psalms - Chapter 95
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
A worship call and warning — 'O come, let us sing unto the LORD; let us kneel before our Maker'; but 'harden not your heart, as in the provocation' at Meribah — quoted extensively in Hebrews 3-4.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Multiple authors — primarily David (73 psalms attributed),
plus Asaph (12), Sons of Korah (11), Solomon (2), Moses (1),
Heman (1), Ethan (1), and anonymous. The Psalter was compiled over
approximately 1000 years and served as Israel's hymnal and prayer
book. The book is divided into five 'books' (1-41, 42-72, 73-89,
90-106, 107-150), paralleling the five books of Moses. Key themes:
worship, lament, praise, trust, kingship, creation, wisdom,
Messianic prophecy, and the full range of human emotion brought
before God.

Classification: Enthronement / Worship / Warning Psalm
Attributed Author: Anonymous (Hebrews 4:7 says "in David")
Key Themes: Joyful worship, God as Creator-King, we are His
sheep, TODAY hearing God's voice, warning against hardened hearts,
Meribah/Massah as negative example, God's rest forfeited

Historical Context: Psalm 95 is among the most important psalms
in Christian theology because of its use in Hebrews 3-4. The
writer of Hebrews quotes vv.7-11 extensively to warn Christians
against unbelief. The psalm divides dramatically: vv.1-7a are
joyful worship; vv.7b-11 are a stern warning. The shift from
"let us sing" to "harden not your heart" is jarring and
intentional — worship without obedience is the very danger. The
Venite (vv.1-7) has been used in Christian morning prayer services
for centuries. "Today if ye will hear his voice" emphasizes
immediacy — the moment of decision is NOW, not later.

Structure:
- Call to Joyful Worship (vv.1-2)
- Reasons for Worship: God is Great (vv.3-5)
- Call to Reverent Worship (vv.6-7a)
- Warning: Today Hear His Voice (vv.7b-11)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.1              | Psalm       | 100     | 1                  | "Make a joyful noise unto the LORD." Same exuberant worship call
v.7              | Hebrews     | 3       | 7                  | 4:11 — The extended commentary on this psalm, warning Christians against the same unbelief
v.7              | 2 Corinthians | 6       | 2                  | "Now is the accepted time; behold, now is the day of salvation." Same "today" urgency
v.8              | Exodus      | 17      | 1-7                | Meribah/Massah: Israel testing God at the waters of strife
v.11             | Hebrews     | 4       | 1-3                | "Let us therefore fear, lest, a promise being left us of entering into his rest, any of you should seem to come short of it."
v.11             | Numbers     | 14      | 22-23              | "Those men which have seen my glory... and have tempted me now these ten times... they shall not see the land."

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.7              | KJV         | "To day if ye will hear his voice" / ESV: "Today, if you hear his voice" — The Hebrew could be conditional ("if you hear") or temporal ("when you hear"). Either way: the moment of hearing demands immediate response. Don't delay.
v.8              | KJV         | "as in the provocation" / ESV: "as at Meribah" — KJV translates the place name (Meribah = provocation/strife; Massah = temptation/testing). ESV retains the Hebrew names. Both refer to Exodus 17 where Israel quarreled with God at the water.
v.11             | KJV         | "my rest" / ESV: "my rest" — In the original context, "rest" meant the Promised Land (Canaan). Hebrews reinterprets it as God's eternal Sabbath-rest still available to believers. Multiple layers of meaning.

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. Worship should be joyful AND reverent (vv.1-2, 6). Singing AND kneeling.
   Noise AND prostration. Biblical worship is both exuberant and humble —
   never merely one without the other.

2. TODAY is the day of decision (v.7). Not tomorrow. Not when conditions
   improve. Not when you "feel ready." Today. Every delay is a step toward
   hardness. Respond now.

3. Hardness happens through hearing without obeying (v.8). The Israelites
   HEARD God — they were not ignorant. They hardened because they heard and
   REFUSED. Repeated hearing without response produces spiritual
   calcification.

4. Unbelief forfeits God's rest (v.11). The wilderness generation lost
   Canaan not because of inability but unbelief. God's rest is still
   available — but it can be forfeited. Don't assume you'll get another
   chance tomorrow.

------------------------------------

  https://bibleproject.com/explore/video/psalms/

  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/psalms-95/

BibleProject Podcast:
  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

96.notes:
Psalms - Chapter 96
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
An enthronement hymn — 'O sing unto the LORD a new song; sing unto the LORD, all the earth'; declare His glory among the nations; let creation rejoice, for the LORD comes to judge the earth with righteousness.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Multiple authors — primarily David (73 psalms attributed),
plus Asaph (12), Sons of Korah (11), Solomon (2), Moses (1),
Heman (1), Ethan (1), and anonymous. The Psalter was compiled over
approximately 1000 years and served as Israel's hymnal and prayer
book. The book is divided into five 'books' (1-41, 42-72, 73-89,
90-106, 107-150), paralleling the five books of Moses. Key themes:
worship, lament, praise, trust, kingship, creation, wisdom,
Messianic prophecy, and the full range of human emotion brought
before God.

Classification: Enthronement / Hymn of Praise
Attributed Author: Anonymous (cf. 1 Chronicles 16:23-33 — Davidic)
Key Themes: A new song, declaring God's glory among the nations,
the LORD above all gods, worship in holiness, creation rejoicing,
God coming to judge the earth

Historical Context: This psalm appears (with variations) in
1 Chronicles 16:23-33 as part of the song David commissioned when
the Ark was brought to Jerusalem. It is one of the "enthronement
psalms" proclaiming God's kingship. The call to sing a "new song"
indicates a fresh experience of God's saving power requiring
fresh worship. The missionary scope is remarkable: "Declare his
glory among the nations" (v.3), "all the earth" (v.1), "all
peoples" (v.3). The psalm ends with the most comprehensive call
to creation worship in Scripture — heavens, earth, sea, fields,
and trees all rejoicing because God comes to judge.

Structure:
- Call to Sing a New Song (vv.1-3)
- The LORD Above All Gods (vv.4-6)
- Ascribe Glory to the LORD (vv.7-9)
- The LORD Reigns (v.10)
- All Creation Rejoices at His Coming (vv.11-13)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.1              | 1 Chronicles | 16      | 23-33              | The parallel passage from David's Ark-of-the-Covenant celebration
v.1              | Revelation  | 5       | 9                  | "Thou art worthy... for thou wast slain, and hast redeemed us... out of every... nation." The new song of the Lamb
v.5              | Isaiah      | 44      | 9-10               | "They that make a graven image are all of them vanity." Idol polemic
v.10             | Matthew     | 28      | 19                 | "Go ye therefore, and teach all nations." The Great Commission echoing this missionary psalm
v.13             | Acts        | 17      | 31                 | "He hath appointed a day, in the which he will judge the world in righteousness."
v.13             | Revelation  | 22      | 20                 | "Even so, come, Lord Jesus." The anticipation of His coming

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.5              | KJV         | "idols" / ESV: "worthless idols" — Hebrew "elilim" = literally "nothings" or "worthless things." A wordplay on "elohim" (God/gods). The gods of the nations are not "elohim" but "elilim" — not gods but nothings.
v.9              | KJV         | "worship the LORD in the beauty of holiness" / ESV: "Worship the LORD in the splendor of holiness" — "Beauty of holiness" could mean holy attire (priestly garments) or the inherent beauty/splendor of holiness itself. Either way: worship requires holiness, and holiness is beautiful.

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. God deserves new songs (v.1). Don't let worship become routine. Fresh
   experiences of God call for fresh expressions of praise. Sing something
   new — write something new — let your worship match the freshness of His
   mercies.

2. The gods of the nations are worthless nothings (v.5). Whatever competes
   with God for your worship — career, comfort, approval, money — is
   "elilim." Literally nothing. The LORD made the heavens. Nothing else
   compares. Stop worshipping nothings.

3. God's coming judgment is good news for creation (v.13). Trees sing,
   fields exult, seas roar with JOY — because when God judges, He sets
   things RIGHT. Judgment is not destruction but restoration. The earth
   longs for it.

RESOURCES
------------------------------------
The Bible Project — Psalms Overview:
  https://bibleproject.com/explore/video/psalms/

David Guzik — Psalms 96 Written Commentary:
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/psalms-96/

BibleProject Podcast:
  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

Commentary sources: Enduring Word (enduringword.com), Charles
Spurgeon (Public Domain)

97.notes:
Psalms - Chapter 97
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
An enthronement psalm — 'The LORD reigneth; let the earth rejoice'; clouds and darkness surround Him, fire goes before Him, mountains melt like wax; Zion is glad because of His judgments. Worship Him, all ye gods.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Multiple authors — primarily David (73 psalms attributed),
plus Asaph (12), Sons of Korah (11), Solomon (2), Moses (1),
Heman (1), Ethan (1), and anonymous. The Psalter was compiled over
approximately 1000 years and served as Israel's hymnal and prayer
book. The book is divided into five 'books' (1-41, 42-72, 73-89,
90-106, 107-150), paralleling the five books of Moses. Key themes:
worship, lament, praise, trust, kingship, creation, wisdom,
Messianic prophecy, and the full range of human emotion brought
before God.

Classification: Enthronement / Theophany Psalm
Attributed Author: Anonymous
Key Themes: The LORD reigns, theophany in fire and lightning,
creation trembles before God, shame of idol-worshippers, Zion's
gladness, loving the LORD means hating evil, light sown for the
righteous

Historical Context: Another enthronement psalm celebrating God's
kingship with theophanic imagery (fire, lightning, melting
mountains — reminiscent of Sinai in Exodus 19). Hebrews 1:6 may
quote v.7 ("worship him, all ye gods") in reference to angels
worshipping Christ, giving this psalm Christological significance.
The psalm moves from cosmic terror before God's glory to Zion's
gladness at His judgments to the practical command: "Ye that love
the LORD, hate evil" (v.10). The final image — "light is sown for
the righteous" (v.11) — is beautiful: light as a seed planted now,
to be harvested as joy later.

Structure:
- The LORD Reigns: Earth Rejoice (v.1)
- Theophanic Glory (vv.2-6)
- Idol-Worshippers Shamed (v.7)
- Zion Glad at God's Judgments (vv.8-9)
- The Righteous: Love God, Hate Evil (vv.10-12)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.1              | Psalm       | 93      | 1                  | ; 99:1 — "The LORD reigneth." The enthronement series declaration
v.2              | Exodus      | 19      | 16-18              | Sinai in clouds, darkness, fire, earthquake. Same theophany
v.5              | Micah       | 1       | 4                  | "The mountains shall be molten under him." Same melting-mountains imagery
v.7              | Hebrews     | 1       | 6                  | "Let all the angels of God worship him." Possibly quoting this verse regarding Christ
v.10             | Romans      | 12      | 9                  | "Abhor that which is evil; cleave to that which is good." Same paired command
v.10             | Amos        | 5       | 15                 | "Hate the evil, and love the good, and establish judgment in the gate."

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.7              | KJV         | "worship him, all ye gods" / ESV: "worship him, all you gods!" — The "gods" (elohim) may be angelic beings, human judges, or pagan deities. All powers — whatever their nature — must bow before Yahweh. Hebrews 1:6 reads it as angels worshipping Christ.
v.11             | KJV         | "Light is sown" / ESV: "Light is sown" — Some manuscripts read "zarach" (dawns/shines) instead of "zara" (sown). Both create beautiful images: light either dawns on the righteous or is planted like a seed for future harvest. The Masoretic text (sown) is followed by both translations.

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. God is simultaneously hidden and just (v.2). Clouds surround Him — you
   can't always SEE His ways. But His throne is righteous — you can always
   TRUST His character, even when His methods are obscured.

2. Loving God and hating evil are inseparable (v.10). Tolerance of evil is
   not love — it is betrayal of love. If you love the LORD, you must
   actively oppose what He opposes. Love without hatred of evil is
   sentimentality, not devotion.

3. Light is already planted for your future (v.11). Even if today is dark,
   light has been SOWN. It is in the ground, growing. Gladness is coming for
   the upright. Trust the seed underground. The harvest is certain.

RESOURCES
------------------------------------
The Bible Project — Psalms Overview:
  https://bibleproject.com/explore/video/psalms/

David Guzik — Psalms 97 Written Commentary:
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/psalms-97/

BibleProject Podcast:
  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

========================================
Study Notes generated for personal Bible study use.
Translations referenced: KJV, ESV, ASV, NET, WEB
Commentary sources: Enduring Word (enduringword.com), Charles
Spurgeon (Public Domain)

98.notes:
Psalms - Chapter 98
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
An enthronement psalm — 'O sing unto the LORD a new song, for he hath done marvellous things'; His right hand and holy arm have won the victory; let the sea roar and rivers clap, for He comes to judge the earth.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Multiple authors — primarily David (73 psalms attributed),
plus Asaph (12), Sons of Korah (11), Solomon (2), Moses (1),
Heman (1), Ethan (1), and anonymous. The Psalter was compiled over
approximately 1000 years and served as Israel's hymnal and prayer
book. The book is divided into five 'books' (1-41, 42-72, 73-89,
90-106, 107-150), paralleling the five books of Moses. Key themes:
worship, lament, praise, trust, kingship, creation, wisdom,
Messianic prophecy, and the full range of human emotion brought
before God.

Classification: Enthronement / Praise Psalm
Attributed Author: Anonymous
Key Themes: New song for new salvation, God's victory by His own
power, salvation revealed to all nations, joyful noise with
instruments, nature worshipping, God coming to judge with equity

Historical Context: Psalm 98 closely parallels Psalm 96 (both call
for a "new song" and end with creation rejoicing at God's coming
judgment). It also echoes Isaiah 52:10: "The LORD hath made bare
his holy arm in the eyes of all the nations; and all the ends of
the earth shall see the salvation of our God." The psalm celebrates
a specific salvation event (v.1 — "he hath done marvellous
things") and calls for escalating praise: vocal singing (vv.1-3),
instrumental music (vv.4-6), and cosmic celebration (vv.7-9). The
hymn "Joy to the World" by Isaac Watts is a direct paraphrase of
this psalm — bringing its Christmas/Messianic application to the
forefront.

Structure:
- New Song for God's Victory (vv.1-3)
- Joyful Noise with Instruments (vv.4-6)
- All Creation Celebrates (vv.7-9)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.1              | Isaiah      | 52      | 10                 | "The LORD hath made bare his holy arm in the eyes of all the nations." Nearly identical language
v.1              | Isaiah      | 59      | 16                 | "His arm brought salvation unto him." God's unilateral victory
v.3              | Luke        | 2       | 30-31              | Simeon: "Mine eyes have seen thy salvation, which thou hast prepared before the face of all people."
v.4              | Psalm       | 100     | 1                  | "Make a joyful noise unto the LORD."
v.8              | Isaiah      | 55      | 12                 | "The mountains and the hills shall break forth before you into singing, and all the trees of the field shall clap their hands."
v.9              | Psalm       | 96      | 13                 | Nearly identical ending: God coming to judge with righteousness and equity

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.1              | KJV         | "gotten him the victory" / ESV: "worked salvation for him" — Hebrew "hoshia lo" = saved/delivered for him(self). God's salvation is self-achieved — He needed no assistance. The "victory" (KJV) / "salvation" (ESV) is by God's power alone.
v.4              | KJV         | "Make a joyful noise unto the LORD, all the earth: make a loud noise, and rejoice, and sing praise" / ESV: "Make a joyful noise to the LORD, all the earth; break forth into joyous song and sing praises!" — Four commands: noise, loud noise, rejoice, sing. The escalation from general shout to specific song.

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. God won the victory by Himself (v.1). No human contribution required. His
   right hand and holy arm — HIS power alone. This is the gospel: salvation
   is God's work, not yours. Receive it; don't try to earn it.

2. God remembers His promises (v.3). He doesn't forget. If He promised mercy
   and truth, He will deliver. "He remembered" — and what He remembers, He
   does.

3. Let everything worship (vv.7-8). If rivers can clap and hills can sing,
   surely you can praise. When creation itself worships, human silence is
   the anomaly. Join the universal chorus.

4. "Joy to the World" — this is its source. The Lord HAS come. He HAS done
   marvelous things. Let nature and nations sing. Isaac Watts saw Christ in
   this psalm, and rightly so.

RESOURCES
------------------------------------
The Bible Project — Psalms Overview:
  https://bibleproject.com/explore/video/psalms/

David Guzik — Psalms 98 Written Commentary:
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/psalms-98/

BibleProject Podcast:
  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

========================================
Study Notes generated for personal Bible study use.
Translations referenced: KJV, ESV, ASV, NET, WEB
Commentary sources: Enduring Word (enduringword.com), Charles
Spurgeon (Public Domain)

99.notes:
Psalms - Chapter 99
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
An enthronement psalm — 'The LORD reigneth; let the people tremble'; He is great in Zion, a lover of justice; Moses, Aaron, and Samuel called upon Him and He answered; He is holy (repeated three times).

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Multiple authors — primarily David (73 psalms attributed),
plus Asaph (12), Sons of Korah (11), Solomon (2), Moses (1),
Heman (1), Ethan (1), and anonymous. The Psalter was compiled over
approximately 1000 years and served as Israel's hymnal and prayer
book. The book is divided into five 'books' (1-41, 42-72, 73-89,
90-106, 107-150), paralleling the five books of Moses. Key themes:
worship, lament, praise, trust, kingship, creation, wisdom,
Messianic prophecy, and the full range of human emotion brought
before God.

Classification: Enthronement / Holiness Psalm
Attributed Author: Anonymous
Key Themes: The LORD reigns, God's holiness (triple refrain),
trembling before God, justice and equity, God answering His
servants, forgiveness AND consequences

Historical Context: The last of the enthronement psalms (93-99).
Its distinguishing feature is the triple refrain "He is holy"
(vv.3,5,9) — echoing the Seraphim's "Holy, holy, holy" in Isaiah
6:3. The psalm moves from God's cosmic reign (vv.1-3) to His just
rule over Jacob (vv.4-5) to His historical relationship with Moses,
Aaron, and Samuel (vv.6-9). These three intercessors exemplify the
psalm's dual theme: God forgives (answered their prayers) AND
avenges (took vengeance on their inventions/wrongdoings). Grace
and accountability coexist in God's holiness. He pardons sin but
does not ignore consequences.

Structure:
- The LORD Reigns: Tremble! (vv.1-3)
- The King Loves Justice (vv.4-5)
- God Answered Moses, Aaron, Samuel (vv.6-7)
- Forgiveness AND Consequences (vv.8-9)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.1              | Psalm       | 80      | 1                  | "Thou that dwellest between the cherubims, shine forth." Same enthroned-on-cherubim imagery
v.1              | Isaiah      | 6       | 1-3                | "Holy, holy, holy, is the LORD of hosts." The triple holiness declaration
v.6              | 1 Samuel    | 12      | 18                 | Samuel called on the LORD and the LORD answered with thunder. God responding to intercessors
v.6              | Exodus      | 32      | 11-14              | Moses interceding and God answering
v.8              | Numbers     | 20      | 12                 | Moses forgiven but denied entry to Canaan. Grace with consequences
v.8              | Numbers     | 12      | 1-15               | Miriam forgiven but leprous seven days. Same principle

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.3              | KJV         | "thy great and terrible name; for it is holy" / ESV: "your great and awesome name! Holy is he!" — ESV restructures to make "Holy is he" an exclamation/refrain. This helps the reader see the triple "holy" structure (vv.3,5,9).
v.8              | KJV         | "thou tookest vengeance of their inventions" / ESV: "an avenger of their wrongdoings" — Hebrew "alilotheihem" = their deeds/practices/inventions. God forgave Moses and Aaron personally but still enforced consequences for their specific actions (Moses couldn't enter Canaan; Aaron's sons died).

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. Holiness should produce trembling (v.1). Not casual familiarity but holy
   awe. God is accessible through grace — but He is still HOLY. Approach
   with reverence, not flippancy.

2. God forgives AND allows consequences (v.8). These are not contradictions.
   A parent can forgive a child's misdeed while still enforcing natural
   consequences. God pardons sin while allowing its earthly effects to play
   out. Grace does not erase all consequences.

3. God answers those who call on Him (v.6). Moses called — God answered.
   Aaron called — God answered. Samuel called — God answered. The pattern:
   those who call receive answers. Do you call? Do you expect answers?

4. "He is holy" is the foundation of everything (vv.3,5,9). Not just one
   attribute among many — holiness IS God's essential character. Everything
   else flows from this. Worship, justice, forgiveness, consequences — all
   are expressions of His holiness.

RESOURCES
------------------------------------
The Bible Project — Psalms Overview:
  https://bibleproject.com/explore/video/psalms/

David Guzik — Psalms 99 Written Commentary:
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/psalms-99/

BibleProject Podcast:
  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

========================================
Study Notes generated for personal Bible study use.
Translations referenced: KJV, ESV, ASV, NET, WEB
Commentary sources: Enduring Word (enduringword.com), Charles
Spurgeon (Public Domain)

1. Make a joyful noise to the LORD, all the earth!
2. Serve the LORD with gladness! Come into his presence with singing!

4. Enter his gates with thanksgiving, and his courts with praise! Give thanks to him; bless his name!
5. For the LORD is good; his steadfast love endures forever, and his faithfulness to all generations.

100.notes:
Psalms - Chapter 100
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
A psalm of thanksgiving — 'Make a joyful noise unto the LORD, all ye lands'; know that the LORD is God who made us; enter His gates with thanksgiving; 'his mercy is everlasting and his truth endureth to all generations.'

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Multiple authors — primarily David (73 psalms attributed),
plus Asaph (12), Sons of Korah (11), Solomon (2), Moses (1),
Heman (1), Ethan (1), and anonymous. The Psalter was compiled over
approximately 1000 years and served as Israel's hymnal and prayer
book. The book is divided into five 'books' (1-41, 42-72, 73-89,
90-106, 107-150), paralleling the five books of Moses. Key themes:
worship, lament, praise, trust, kingship, creation, wisdom,
Messianic prophecy, and the full range of human emotion brought
before God.

Classification: Hymn of Praise / Thanksgiving Psalm
Attributed Author: Anonymous (superscription: "A Psalm of praise/
thanksgiving")
Key Themes: Joyful worship, serving God with gladness, knowing
God as Creator and Shepherd, entering His presence with
thanksgiving, God's eternal goodness, mercy, and faithfulness

Historical Context: Psalm 100 is one of the most beloved and
frequently recited psalms in all of Scripture. Only 5 verses, yet
it contains the fullness of worship theology. The superscription
identifies it as "A Psalm for thanksgiving" (todah) — likely used
when bringing a todah (thank offering) to the temple. It serves as
the climactic conclusion to the enthronement psalm series (93-100),
summarizing the appropriate response to God's kingship: joyful
service, knowing Him rightly, entering His presence, and
acknowledging His character. The Jubilate Deo (Latin translation of
v.1) has been set to music thousands of times and used in
Christian liturgy for two millennia. This is the psalm that
captures the heartbeat of worship in five perfect verses.

Structure:
- Command: Joyful Noise and Service (vv.1-2)
- Foundation: Know Who God Is (v.3)
- Command: Enter with Thanksgiving and Praise (v.4)
- Foundation: Know What God Is Like (v.5)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.1              | Psalm       | 98      | 4                  | "Make a joyful noise unto the LORD, all the earth." Same universal call
v.2              | Philippians | 4       | 4                  | "Rejoice in the Lord alway: and again I say, Rejoice." Joy as command
v.3              | Psalm       | 95      | 6-7                | "Let us kneel before the LORD our maker. For he is our God; and we are the people of his pasture." Nearly identical
v.3              | Ephesians   | 2       | 10                 | "We are his workmanship, created in Christ Jesus." He made us
v.4              | Hebrews     | 13      | 15                 | "Let us offer the sacrifice of praise to God continually, that is, the fruit of our lips giving thanks to his name."
v.5              | ?           | ?       | ?                  | Psalm 136 — Every verse: "for his mercy endureth for ever." The fullest expansion of Ps 100:5.
v.5              | Lamentations | 3       | 22-23              | "The LORD's mercies... are new every morning: great is thy faithfulness."

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.1              | KJV         | "all ye lands" / ESV: "all the earth" — Hebrew "kol ha-aretz" = all the earth/land. The scope is universal — not just Israel but every land, every nation.
v.3              | KJV         | "it is he that hath made us, and not we ourselves" / ESV: "It is he who made us, and we are his" — The Hebrew has a textual variant: the written text (Ketiv) reads "lo" (not) = "we did not make ourselves." The read text (Qere) reads "lo" (to him) = "we are his." Both are theologically true. ESV follows the Qere; KJV follows the Ketiv.
v.5              | KJV         | "his mercy is everlasting" / ESV: "his steadfast love endures forever" — Hebrew "hesed" again. KJV's "mercy" and ESV's "steadfast love" each capture one dimension of this rich term. It is covenant love that is faithful, loyal, generous, and enduring.

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. Serve with gladness (v.2). If your service to God feels like a burden,
   something is wrong — not with the service but with the heart. Gladness is
   the oxygen of true service. Serve because you WANT to, not because you
   HAVE to.

2. Know three things (v.3). WHO: the LORD is God. WHERE: He made you (you
   didn't make yourself). WHOSE: you belong to Him. These three truths
   resolve all identity crises. You are created, owned, and shepherded by
   the living God.

3. Enter with thanksgiving (v.4). Don't come to God listing complaints. Come
   first with gratitude. Thanksgiving is the gate; praise is the courtyard.
   Gratitude opens the door to everything else in worship.

4. God is good, loving, and faithful — always (v.5). Not sometimes. Not to
   some people. His goodness is His nature. His hesed is everlasting. His
   faithfulness spans ALL generations — including yours. These three truths
   never change.

5. This psalm is the summary of all worship. Five verses. The whole theology
   of praise distilled: who God is, who we are, how to come, what He's like.
   If you memorize one psalm about worship, let it be this one.

RESOURCES
------------------------------------
The Bible Project — Psalms Overview:
  https://bibleproject.com/explore/video/psalms/

David Guzik — Psalms 100 Written Commentary:
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/psalms-100/

BibleProject Podcast:
  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/
