120.notes:
Psalms - Chapter 120
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
A Song of Ascents — a lament from one dwelling among hostile liars and warmongers: 'Woe is me, that I sojourn in Mesech'; the psalmist is for peace, but they are for war.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Multiple authors — primarily David (73 psalms attributed),
plus Asaph (12), Sons of Korah (11), Solomon (2), Moses (1),
Heman (1), Ethan (1), and anonymous. The Psalter was compiled over
approximately 1000 years and served as Israel's hymnal and prayer
book. The book is divided into five 'books' (1-41, 42-72, 73-89,
90-106, 107-150), paralleling the five books of Moses. Key themes:
worship, lament, praise, trust, kingship, creation, wisdom,
Messianic prophecy, and the full range of human emotion brought
before God.

Classification: Song of Ascents / Individual Lament
Attributed Author: Anonymous (superscription: "A Song of
degrees/ascents")
Key Themes: Deceitful speech, longing for peace among warlike
people, the pain of living among the ungodly, crying to God in
distress, spiritual exile

Historical Context: Psalm 120 is the first of fifteen "Songs of
Ascents" (Psalms 120-134), sung by pilgrims traveling UP to
Jerusalem for the three annual festivals (Passover, Pentecost,
Tabernacles). "Ascents" refers to going UP to Jerusalem (which
sits on a hill) or possibly the fifteen steps between temple
courts. The collection moves thematically from distress (120)
toward joy in God's house (134). Psalm 120 begins the journey in
a place of exile — among liars and those who hate peace. The
pilgrim longs for Jerusalem because his current dwelling (Meshech/
Kedar — representing distant, hostile lands) is filled with
conflict. The journey to God's house begins with the recognition:
I don't belong here.

Structure:
- Cry to God in Distress (v.1)
- Prayer Against Deceitful Speech (v.2)
- Judgment on the False Tongue (vv.3-4)
- Lament Over Living Among the Hostile (vv.5-7)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.1              | Psalm       | 18      | 6                  | "In my distress I called upon the LORD."
v.2              | Psalm       | 52      | 2-4                | The deceitful tongue as a weapon
v.4              | Proverbs    | 25      | 18                 | "A man that beareth false witness... is a sharp arrow."
v.5              | Genesis     | 25      | 13                 | Kedar was Ishmael's son; represents Arab/nomadic hostility
v.7              | Matthew     | 5       | 9                  | "Blessed are the peacemakers."
v.7              | Romans      | 12      | 18                 | "If it be possible... live peaceably with all men."

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.4              | KJV         | "Sharp arrows of the mighty, with coals of juniper" — Hebrew "rethem" = broom plant (not juniper). Broom coals burn extremely hot and long. The punishment fits the crime: the false tongue (which burns like fire) receives burning coals; the sharp words receive sharp arrows.
v.5              | KJV         | "Mesech" (Meshech) = a people near the Black Sea region (Gen 10:2). "Kedar" = Arabian desert tribe. Both represent "far from Jerusalem" and "far from peace." The psalmist may not literally dwell in both — they represent the hostility of his environment.

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. The journey to God begins in distress (v.1). You don't start the
   pilgrimage from a place of comfort — you start because where you are is
   unbearable. Dissatisfaction with the present drives you toward God's
   presence.

2. Deceitful speech wounds deeply (v.2). Living among liars and slanderers
   is soul-destroying. The tongue's damage is compared to arrows and burning
   coals. Guard against both being the target AND being the source.

3. You may be for peace while others are for war (v.7). Being a peacemaker
   doesn't guarantee peace. Some people are committed to conflict. Your role
   is to BE peace, not to guarantee others will receive it.

4. Not every environment is where you belong (v.5). If you feel like a
   stranger — surrounded by values, speech, and actions contrary to God —
   that discomfort is right. You're on pilgrimage. You haven't arrived yet.

RESOURCES
------------------------------------
The Bible Project — Psalms Overview:
  https://bibleproject.com/explore/video/psalms/

David Guzik — Psalms 120 Written Commentary:
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/psalms-120/

BibleProject Podcast:
  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

========================================
Study Notes generated for personal Bible study use.
Translations referenced: KJV, ESV, ASV, NET, WEB
Commentary sources: Enduring Word (enduringword.com), Charles
Spurgeon (Public Domain)

121.notes:
Psalms - Chapter 121
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
A Song of Ascents (pilgrimage psalm of trust) — 'I will lift up mine eyes unto the hills; from whence cometh my help? My help cometh from the LORD'; He who keeps Israel neither slumbers nor sleeps.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Multiple authors — primarily David (73 psalms attributed),
plus Asaph (12), Sons of Korah (11), Solomon (2), Moses (1),
Heman (1), Ethan (1), and anonymous. The Psalter was compiled over
approximately 1000 years and served as Israel's hymnal and prayer
book. The book is divided into five 'books' (1-41, 42-72, 73-89,
90-106, 107-150), paralleling the five books of Moses. Key themes:
worship, lament, praise, trust, kingship, creation, wisdom,
Messianic prophecy, and the full range of human emotion brought
before God.

Classification: Song of Ascents / Psalm of Trust
Attributed Author: Anonymous (superscription: "A Song of
degrees/ascents")
Key Themes: God as keeper/protector, divine watchfulness that
never sleeps, help from the Creator, protection from all evil,
preservation in all circumstances, total security in God

Historical Context: Psalm 121 is the second Song of Ascents and
one of the most beloved psalms in all of Scripture. It was sung
by pilgrims approaching Jerusalem, looking up at the hills ahead.
The "hills" likely refer to the mountains surrounding Jerusalem
(or possibly the hills where pagan shrines were located — in which
case the answer is: help comes NOT from the hills/shrines but from
the LORD). The psalm uses the word "keep/preserve" (Hebrew
"shamar") six times in eight verses — the most concentrated
expression of divine protection in the Psalter. It likely
functioned as a blessing pronounced by a priest over departing
pilgrims — a divine guarantee for the journey home.

Structure:
- The Pilgrim's Question (v.1)
- The Pilgrim's Answer (v.2)
- The Keeper Who Never Sleeps (vv.3-4)
- Complete Protection (vv.5-6)
- Preservation Always (vv.7-8)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.1              | Psalm       | 87      | 1                  | "His foundation is in the holy mountains."
v.2              | Psalm       | 124     | 8                  | "Our help is in the name of the LORD, who made heaven and earth."
v.3              | 1 Samuel    | 2       | 9                  | "He will keep the feet of his saints."
v.4              | 1 Kings     | 18      | 27                 | Elijah mocked Baal: "Perhaps he is sleeping." Israel's God does NOT sleep
v.7              | Romans      | 8       | 28                 | "All things work together for good."
v.8              | Deuteronomy | 28      | 6                  | "Blessed shalt thou be when thou comest in, and blessed shalt thou be when thou goest out."

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.1              | KJV         | The Hebrew can be read as a statement ("from whence my help comes") or a question ("from where does my help come?"). If a question, it heightens the drama: the pilgrim asks, then answers. ESV reads it as a question: "from where does my help come?"
v.3              | KJV         | "keeper" (shamar) = to guard, watch over, keep safe. Used six times in this psalm (vv.3, 4, 5, 7[x2], 8). Massive emphasis on God's guardian role. He is THE keeper.
v.6              | KJV         | "sun shall not smite thee by day, nor the moon by night" — Protection from heatstroke by day and harmful night exposure. Also possibly spiritual: protection from all celestial influences, both seen and unseen dangers.

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. Lift your eyes (v.1). When overwhelmed, look UP. Not at the problem, not
   at the obstacle — up to the hills where God dwells. Direction of gaze
   determines direction of hope.

2. Your help is from the MAKER (v.2). The One who made heaven and earth is
   your helper. He is not inadequate to your problem. Whatever you face, He
   made something bigger.

3. He never sleeps (vv.3-4). When you're awake at 3 AM with anxiety, God is
   awake too — not anxious but watching, keeping, guarding. You can sleep
   BECAUSE He doesn't.

4. All evil, all time (vv.7-8). Every exit and entrance. Every journey and
   return. From now until forever. There is no gap in God's protective care.
   No situation where He is absent.

5. This psalm is the ultimate travel blessing. Whether you're walking to
   Jerusalem or walking through life — the Keeper of Israel watches over
   your every step.

RESOURCES
------------------------------------
The Bible Project — Psalms Overview:
  https://bibleproject.com/explore/video/psalms/

David Guzik — Psalms 121 Written Commentary:
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/psalms-121/

BibleProject Podcast:
  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

Study Notes generated for personal Bible study use.
Translations referenced: KJV, ESV, ASV, NET, WEB
Commentary sources: Enduring Word (enduringword.com), Charles
Spurgeon (Public Domain)

122.notes:
Psalms - Chapter 122
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
A Song of Ascents — 'I was glad when they said unto me, Let us go into the house of the LORD'; a celebration of arriving at Jerusalem, praying for the city's peace and prosperity.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Multiple authors — primarily David (73 psalms attributed),
plus Asaph (12), Sons of Korah (11), Solomon (2), Moses (1),
Heman (1), Ethan (1), and anonymous. The Psalter was compiled over
approximately 1000 years and served as Israel's hymnal and prayer
book. The book is divided into five 'books' (1-41, 42-72, 73-89,
90-106, 107-150), paralleling the five books of Moses. Key themes:
worship, lament, praise, trust, kingship, creation, wisdom,
Messianic prophecy, and the full range of human emotion brought
before God.

Classification: Song of Ascents / Song of Zion
Attributed Author: David (superscription: "A Song of degrees
of David")
Key Themes: Joy in going to God's house, love for Jerusalem,
prayer for peace, unity of God's people, worship as communal
destination

Historical Context: Psalm 122 is the third Song of Ascents and
the pilgrim's song of ARRIVAL. After the distress of 120 and
the journey of 121, the pilgrim reaches Jerusalem and expresses
joy. The psalm celebrates Jerusalem as the place where tribes
gather, where God's throne of justice stands, and where peace
should be prayed for. "Pray for the peace of Jerusalem" (v.6)
remains one of the most quoted phrases in all of Scripture.
Jerusalem means "city of peace" (shalom), making the prayer
for peace a play on the city's own name.

Structure:
- Joy at the Invitation (v.1)
- Arrival in Jerusalem (v.2)
- Description of the City (vv.3-5)
- Prayer for Peace (vv.6-9)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.1              | Hebrews     | 10      | 25                 | "Not forsaking the assembling of ourselves together."
v.3              | Revelation  | 21      | 2                  | The New Jerusalem, compact and complete
v.5              | 2 Samuel    | 8       | 15                 | "David reigned... and David executed judgment and justice."
v.6              | Matthew     | 5       | 9                  | "Blessed are the peacemakers."
v.6              | Galatians   | 6       | 16                 | "Peace be upon... the Israel of God."

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.3              | KJV         | "compact together" / ESV: "bound firmly together" — Hebrew "chubberah" = joined together, compacted. Jerusalem as a unified, cohesive city — architecturally and spiritually. No gaps, no divisions.
v.6              | KJV         | "Peace of Jerusalem" — Hebrew wordplay: "sha'alu shalom Yerushalayim" — pray (sha'al) peace (shalom) for the city whose name contains shalom. The name and the prayer are one.

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. Be glad to worship (v.1). If the invitation to gather with God's people
   produces reluctance instead of gladness, examine your heart. Corporate
   worship is a privilege, not an obligation.

2. Pray for peace (v.6). Pray for the peace of your church, your city, your
   nation. Peace doesn't happen by accident — it comes through prayer.

3. Unity matters (vv.3-4). The tribes go up TOGETHER. Jerusalem is a city
   "compact together." God values the unity of His people gathered in one
   place.

4. For the sake of others (v.8). Pursue peace not just for your own comfort
   but for the sake of your brothers and sisters. Community peace is
   everyone's responsibility.

RESOURCES
------------------------------------
The Bible Project — Psalms Overview:
  https://bibleproject.com/explore/video/psalms/

David Guzik — Psalms 122 Written Commentary:
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/psalms-122/

BibleProject Podcast:
  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

========================================
Study Notes generated for personal Bible study use.
Translations referenced: KJV, ESV, ASV, NET, WEB
Commentary sources: Enduring Word (enduringword.com), Charles
Spurgeon (Public Domain)

3. Have mercy upon us, O LORD, have mercy upon us, for we have had more than enough of contempt.
4. Our soul has had more than enough of the scorn of those who are at ease, of the contempt of the proud.

123.notes:
Psalms - Chapter 123
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
A Song of Ascents — the psalmist lifts his eyes to God in heaven as a servant looks to the hand of his master, pleading for mercy because the people are filled with the contempt of the proud.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Multiple authors — primarily David (73 psalms attributed),
plus Asaph (12), Sons of Korah (11), Solomon (2), Moses (1),
Heman (1), Ethan (1), and anonymous. The Psalter was compiled over
approximately 1000 years and served as Israel's hymnal and prayer
book. The book is divided into five 'books' (1-41, 42-72, 73-89,
90-106, 107-150), paralleling the five books of Moses. Key themes:
worship, lament, praise, trust, kingship, creation, wisdom,
Messianic prophecy, and the full range of human emotion brought
before God.

Classification: Song of Ascents / Communal Lament
Attributed Author: Anonymous (superscription: "A Song of degrees")
Key Themes: Looking to God for mercy, patient waiting as a
servant, contempt from the proud, humble dependence on God

Historical Context: Psalm 123 is the fourth Song of Ascents — a
short prayer of four verses from a people experiencing contempt
and scorn from those "at ease" (the arrogant wealthy). The central
image is stunning: eyes fixed on God's hand like a servant
watching a master for the slightest signal. The psalm likely
reflects post-exilic Israel, despised by surrounding nations.
It teaches that when scorned by the world, the proper response is
not retaliation but looking upward — fixing your gaze on God's
hand, waiting for His mercy to act.

Structure:
- Eyes Lifted to God (v.1)
- The Servant's Gaze (v.2)
- Plea for Mercy (v.3)
- The Weight of Contempt (v.4)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.1              | Psalm       | 121     | 1                  | "I will lift up mine eyes." Same upward gaze
v.2              | Psalm       | 25      | 15                 | "Mine eyes are ever toward the LORD."
v.3              | Nehemiah    | 4       | 4                  | Nehemiah's prayer when scorned
v.4              | 1 Corinthians | 4       | 13                 | "We are made as the filth of the world... the offscouring of all things."

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.2              | KJV         | "the hand of their masters" — In ancient households, masters communicated through hand gestures. Servants watched constantly for signals. This is the posture of faith: attentive, expectant, ready to respond.

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. Fix your eyes on God's hand (v.2). When you face contempt or scorn, don't
   fixate on the scorners. Look at God's hand. Watch for His signal, His
   provision, His timing.

2. "Until" (v.2). Keep looking until mercy comes. Patience is not passive —
   it's active watching. Don't give up before God acts.

3. Being filled with contempt is painful (vv.3-4). Scripture acknowledges
   this pain without minimizing it. Being despised by the comfortable and
   proud is real suffering. Bring it to God.

------------------------------------

124.notes:
Psalms - Chapter 124
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
A Song of Ascents — 'If it had not been the LORD who was on our side,' the enemies would have swallowed them alive; their soul escaped like a bird from a snare; 'our help is in the name of the LORD.'

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Multiple authors — primarily David (73 psalms attributed),
plus Asaph (12), Sons of Korah (11), Solomon (2), Moses (1),
Heman (1), Ethan (1), and anonymous. The Psalter was compiled over
approximately 1000 years and served as Israel's hymnal and prayer
book. The book is divided into five 'books' (1-41, 42-72, 73-89,
90-106, 107-150), paralleling the five books of Moses. Key themes:
worship, lament, praise, trust, kingship, creation, wisdom,
Messianic prophecy, and the full range of human emotion brought
before God.

Classification: Song of Ascents / Communal Thanksgiving
Attributed Author: David (superscription: "A Song of degrees
of David")
Key Themes: What would have happened without God, divine rescue
from overwhelming enemies, escape like a bird from a snare, help
in the name of the LORD

Historical Context: Psalm 124 is the fifth Song of Ascents — a
corporate thanksgiving that uses vivid "what if" imagery. The
psalm asks Israel to imagine what would have happened IF God had
not been on their side: they would have been swallowed alive,
drowned by flood waters, torn by wild beasts. But God DID
intervene — and the soul has escaped like a bird from the fowler's
snare. The final verse is one of the great confessions of faith:
"Our help is in the name of the LORD."

Structure:
- "If the LORD had not been on our side..." (vv.1-5)
- Praise for Escape (vv.6-7)
- Declaration of Trust (v.8)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.1              | Romans      | 8       | 31                 | "If God be for us, who can be against us?"
v.4              | Isaiah      | 43      | 2                  | "When thou passest through the waters, I will be with thee."
v.7              | 2 Timothy   | 2       | 26                 | Escape from the snare of the devil
v.8              | Psalm       | 121     | 2                  | "My help cometh from the LORD, which made heaven and earth."

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.2              | KJV         | "swallowed us up quick" — "Quick" = alive (as in the Apostles' Creed: "the quick and the dead"). They would have been swallowed alive — like Korah's company (Num 16:32).
v.7              | KJV         | "fowlers" = those who trap birds with snares. The enemy is compared to a trapper — subtle, hidden, deadly. But God broke the snare.

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. Imagine life without God's rescue (vv.1-5). What would have happened
   without Him? The flood would have taken you, the enemy swallowed you
   whole. Gratitude grows when you consider what DIDN'T happen because God
   intervened.

2. The snare is broken (v.7). If you've escaped addiction, toxic
   relationships, spiritual death — the snare is BROKEN. You didn't just
   slip out; God broke it. Don't go back to a broken trap.

3. Our help is in the name of the LORD (v.8). Not in our cleverness, not in
   our strength, not in human alliances. In His NAME — His character, His
   nature, His power.

------------------------------------

  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/psalms-124/

BibleProject Podcast:
  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

125.notes:
Psalms - Chapter 125
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
A Song of Ascents (trust) — those who trust in the LORD are like Mount Zion, immovable; the scepter of wickedness shall not rest on the righteous; peace be upon Israel.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Multiple authors — primarily David (73 psalms attributed),
plus Asaph (12), Sons of Korah (11), Solomon (2), Moses (1),
Heman (1), Ethan (1), and anonymous. The Psalter was compiled over
approximately 1000 years and served as Israel's hymnal and prayer
book. The book is divided into five 'books' (1-41, 42-72, 73-89,
90-106, 107-150), paralleling the five books of Moses. Key themes:
worship, lament, praise, trust, kingship, creation, wisdom,
Messianic prophecy, and the full range of human emotion brought
before God.

Classification: Song of Ascents / Psalm of Trust
Attributed Author: Anonymous (superscription: "A Song of degrees")
Key Themes: The immovability of those who trust God, God
surrounding His people like mountains, the scepter of wickedness
will not remain, peace upon Israel

Historical Context: Psalm 125 is the sixth Song of Ascents — a
psalm of quiet confidence. Its central image is geographical:
Jerusalem is surrounded by mountains, and God surrounds His people
just as those mountains surround the city. For a pilgrim who has
walked through the hills to reach Jerusalem, this imagery is
viscerally real — they can SEE the mountains encircling the city
and understand the metaphor with their eyes. The psalm also
addresses a danger: if wickedness rules too long, even the
righteous might be tempted to do wrong (v.3). But God will not
allow it.

Structure:
- Immovability of the Faithful (v.1)
- God as Surrounding Mountains (v.2)
- Wickedness Won't Remain (v.3)
- Prayer for the Good (vv.4-5)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.1              | Psalm       | 46      | 5                  | "God is in the midst of her; she shall not be moved."
v.2              | Zechariah   | 2       | 5                  | "I, saith the LORD, will be unto her a wall of fire round about."
v.3              | 1 Corinthians | 10      | 13                 | "God is faithful, who will not suffer you to be tempted above that ye are able."
v.5              | Galatians   | 6       | 16                 | "Peace be upon... the Israel of God."

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. Trust makes you a mountain (v.1). The trusting person is not shaken by
   circumstances — like Zion, they abide forever. Stability comes from
   trust, not from circumstances.

2. God is around you on every side (v.2). Mountains don't have gaps. God's
   surrounding presence has no openings for the enemy to exploit. You are
   enclosed by His care.

3. Wickedness has an expiration date (v.3). God won't let evil rule over you
   indefinitely. He limits its duration because He cares about your
   integrity, not just your survival.

------------------------------------

David Guzik — Psalms 125 Written Commentary:
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/psalms-125/

BibleProject Podcast:
  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

4. Restore our fortunes, O LORD, like streams in the Negeb!
5. Those who sow in tears shall reap with shouts of joy!
6. He who goes out weeping, bearing the seed for sowing, shall come home with shouts of joy, bringing his sheaves with him.

2. At that time we laughed loudly and shouted for joy. At that time the nations said, “The Lord has accomplished great things for these people.”

4. Restore our fortunes again, Yahweh, like the streams in the Negev.

126.notes:
Psalms - Chapter 126
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
A Song of Ascents — 'When the LORD turned again the captivity of Zion, we were like them that dream'; a joyful remembrance of restoration and prayer for more: 'They that sow in tears shall reap in joy.'

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Multiple authors — primarily David (73 psalms attributed),
plus Asaph (12), Sons of Korah (11), Solomon (2), Moses (1),
Heman (1), Ethan (1), and anonymous. The Psalter was compiled over
approximately 1000 years and served as Israel's hymnal and prayer
book. The book is divided into five 'books' (1-41, 42-72, 73-89,
90-106, 107-150), paralleling the five books of Moses. Key themes:
worship, lament, praise, trust, kingship, creation, wisdom,
Messianic prophecy, and the full range of human emotion brought
before God.

Classification: Song of Ascents / Communal Lament and Hope
Attributed Author: Anonymous (superscription: "A Song of degrees")
Key Themes: The joy of restoration, laughter and singing at
God's deliverance, sowing in tears and reaping in joy, the
dream-like quality of answered prayer

Historical Context: Psalm 126 likely celebrates the return from
Babylonian exile (538 BC) — the restoration of Zion that seemed
like a dream when it happened. The first half recalls past
deliverance with overwhelming joy; the second half prays for
continued/future restoration using agricultural imagery. "Sowing
in tears" and "reaping in joy" has become proverbial. The Negev
streams (v.4) are wadis that are bone-dry in summer but suddenly
rush with water after rain — an image of sudden, dramatic
restoration after long drought.

Structure:
- Remembering Past Restoration (vv.1-3)
- Prayer for Present Restoration (v.4)
- The Principle: Tears to Joy (vv.5-6)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.1              | Ezra        | 1       | 1-4                | Cyrus' decree: the return from exile
v.2              | Psalm       | 98      | 2                  | "The LORD hath made known his salvation: his righteousness hath he openly shewed."
v.4              | Joel        | 2       | 25                 | "I will restore to you the years that the locust hath eaten."
v.5              | John        | 16      | 20                 | "Your sorrow shall be turned into joy."
v.5              | Galatians   | 6       | 9                  | "In due season we shall reap, if we faint not."

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.1              | KJV         | "turned the captivity" — Hebrew "shub shebut" can also mean "restored the fortunes." Not just ending exile but restoring what was lost.
v.4              | KJV         | "streams in the south" / "watercourses in the Negev" — The Negev (dry southland) has wadis that suddenly fill with rushing water. The image: sudden, abundant, dramatic restoration after extended dryness.

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. Restoration feels like a dream (v.1). When God finally moves after long
   waiting, it's almost unbelievable. Trust that God-sized answers will come
   — even if they seem too good to be true.

2. Sow in tears (v.5). Your pain is not wasted if you're sowing. Tears are
   seed. Every faithful act done through suffering is investment in future
   harvest. Keep sowing, even when weeping.

3. "Shall doubtless come again with rejoicing" (v.6). The harvest is
   GUARANTEED. Not "might" — SHALL. Not "possibly" — DOUBTLESS. If you sow
   faithfully, you WILL reap joyfully. The only question is timing.

4. Turn your captivities into testimonies (vv.1-3). When God delivers, don't
   keep it quiet. Even the nations noticed Israel's restoration. Let your
   testimony be heard.

------------------------------------

  https://bibleproject.com/explore/video/psalms/

David Guzik — Psalms 126 Written Commentary:
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/psalms-126/

BibleProject Podcast:
  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

127.notes:
Psalms - Chapter 127
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
A Song of Ascents of Solomon — a wisdom psalm: 'Except the LORD build the house, they labour in vain'; God gives sleep to His beloved; children are a heritage from the LORD, arrows in a warrior's hand.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Multiple authors — primarily David (73 psalms attributed),
plus Asaph (12), Sons of Korah (11), Solomon (2), Moses (1),
Heman (1), Ethan (1), and anonymous. The Psalter was compiled over
approximately 1000 years and served as Israel's hymnal and prayer
book. The book is divided into five 'books' (1-41, 42-72, 73-89,
90-106, 107-150), paralleling the five books of Moses. Key themes:
worship, lament, praise, trust, kingship, creation, wisdom,
Messianic prophecy, and the full range of human emotion brought
before God.

Classification: Song of Ascents / Wisdom Psalm
Attributed Author: Solomon (superscription: "A Song of degrees
for Solomon")
Key Themes: The futility of human effort without God, trust vs.
anxiety, children as God's blessing, the family as divine gift,
rest in God's sovereignty

Historical Context: Psalm 127 is one of only two psalms attributed
to Solomon in the Songs of Ascents (the other being possibly 72,
which is in Book II). As the builder of the temple, Solomon is
uniquely qualified to declare that building without God is useless.
The irony is powerful: the greatest builder in Israel's history
says, "Unless the LORD builds the house, you labor in vain." The
psalm has two halves: vv.1-2 address anxious self-reliance in
work/security; vv.3-5 address the blessing of children. Both
halves make the same point: what matters most comes from God, not
from human striving. This psalm directly challenges the
workaholic, the anxious, and the self-made person.

Structure:
- Without God, Labor is Vain (v.1)
- Anxious Toil vs. God's Gift of Rest (v.2)
- Children as God's Heritage (vv.3-5)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.1              | Psalm       | 121     | 4                  | "He that keepeth Israel shall neither slumber nor sleep." God is the true keeper
v.1              | 2 Chronicles | 2       | 1                  | Solomon building the temple — yet acknowledging God must build it
v.2              | Matthew     | 6       | 25-34              | "Take no thought for the morrow." Jesus against anxiety
v.2              | Psalm       | 4       | 8                  | "I will both lay me down in peace, and sleep: for thou, LORD, only makest me dwell in safety."
v.3              | Genesis     | 33      | 5                  | "The children which God hath graciously given thy servant."

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.2              | KJV         | "he giveth his beloved sleep" / ESV: "he gives to his beloved sleep" — Some translate: "he gives to his beloved WHILE THEY SLEEP" (i.e., God works for you even while you rest). Both readings are powerful: God either gives you the gift of peaceful sleep OR gives blessings while you sleep.
v.4              | KJV         | "arrows in the hand of a mighty man" — Children are compared to ARROWS: pointed, purposeful, launched forward into the future. Parents are the bow; children are the arrows sent where parents cannot go themselves.

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. God must build it (v.1). Whatever you're building — career, ministry,
   family, business — unless God is building it with you, it's empty.
   Acknowledge dependence BEFORE effort.

2. Stop eating the bread of sorrows (v.2). Anxious overwork is not virtue —
   it's unbelief. God gives His beloved sleep. Can you rest? Can you stop?
   Trust is demonstrated by what you're willing to leave undone.

3. Children are heritage, not hassle (v.3). In a culture that often views
   children as inconvenience, Scripture calls them GOD'S REWARD. Reframe
   your view: children are divine gift.

4. Sleep is a spiritual discipline (v.2). Going to bed is an act of faith —
   trusting that God works while you rest. The inability to sleep often
   reveals the inability to trust.

------------------------------------

  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/psalms-127/

  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

128.ESV:

128.notes:
Psalms - Chapter 128
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
A Song of Ascents — a blessing on the family that fears the LORD: the man eats the fruit of his labor, his wife is a fruitful vine, his children like olive plants; 'peace be upon Israel.'

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Multiple authors — primarily David (73 psalms attributed),
plus Asaph (12), Sons of Korah (11), Solomon (2), Moses (1),
Heman (1), Ethan (1), and anonymous. The Psalter was compiled over
approximately 1000 years and served as Israel's hymnal and prayer
book. The book is divided into five 'books' (1-41, 42-72, 73-89,
90-106, 107-150), paralleling the five books of Moses. Key themes:
worship, lament, praise, trust, kingship, creation, wisdom,
Messianic prophecy, and the full range of human emotion brought
before God.

Classification: Song of Ascents / Wisdom/Beatitude Psalm
Attributed Author: Anonymous (superscription: "A Song of degrees")
Key Themes: The blessing of the God-fearing household, fruitful
labor, family as vine and olive shoots, blessing from Zion,
prosperity through fearing God

Historical Context: Psalm 128 is a companion to Psalm 127 — both
address family and home. Psalm 127 warns against building without
God; Psalm 128 describes the blessed household that fears God. It
paints a warm domestic scene: a man eating the fruit of his labor,
a wife like a fruitful vine, children like olive shoots around
the table. This is the biblical vision of the good life — not
wealth or fame but faithful labor, a loving family, and the fear
of God. The psalm serves as a pilgrim's reminder of what awaits
at home and what God blesses.

Structure:
- The Blessed Man Identified (v.1)
- Blessings of Labor and Family (vv.2-4)
- Blessing from Zion (vv.5-6)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.1              | Psalm       | 112     | 1                  | "Blessed is the man that feareth the LORD."
v.2              | Isaiah      | 3       | 10                 | "Say ye to the righteous, that it shall be well with him: for they shall eat the fruit of their doings."
v.3              | Proverbs    | 31      | 28                 | The virtuous wife blessed by her family
v.3              | Psalm       | 52      | 8                  | "I am like a green olive tree in the house of God."

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.2              | KJV         | "thou shalt eat the labour of thine hands" — Literally eating what your own hands produced. In contrast to the curse of Deuteronomy 28:33 where strangers eat your labor. This is the restoration of Eden's purpose: fruitful work.
v.3              | KJV         | "olive plants" — Young olive shoots (Hebrew "shetilim") are carefully cultivated, deeply rooted, long-lived. Olives take years to mature but produce for centuries. Children as long-term investment.

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. The good life is simpler than you think (vv.2-3). Meaningful work, a
   loving spouse, children around the table. This is God's definition of
   blessing — not celebrity or wealth but faithful, fruitful home life.

2. Fear God and walk His ways (v.1). These two things open the door to
   everything else in this psalm. All the domestic blessings flow from this
   starting point.

3. Family is long-term investment (vv.3, 6). Olive plants take years to bear
   fruit. Children require patient cultivation. The reward: seeing
   children's children and peace.

------------------------------------

  https://bibleproject.com/explore/video/psalms/

David Guzik — Psalms 128 Written Commentary:
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/psalms-128/

BibleProject Podcast:
  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

5. May all who hate Zion be put to shame and turned backward!

129.notes:
Psalms - Chapter 129
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
A Song of Ascents — Israel recounts lifelong affliction: 'Many a time have they afflicted me from my youth'; plowers plowed upon their back, yet the LORD cut the cords of the wicked; may Zion's enemies wither.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Multiple authors — primarily David (73 psalms attributed),
plus Asaph (12), Sons of Korah (11), Solomon (2), Moses (1),
Heman (1), Ethan (1), and anonymous. The Psalter was compiled over
approximately 1000 years and served as Israel's hymnal and prayer
book. The book is divided into five 'books' (1-41, 42-72, 73-89,
90-106, 107-150), paralleling the five books of Moses. Key themes:
worship, lament, praise, trust, kingship, creation, wisdom,
Messianic prophecy, and the full range of human emotion brought
before God.

Classification: Song of Ascents / Communal Lament
Attributed Author: Anonymous (superscription: "A Song of degrees")
Key Themes: Israel's history of persecution, survival through
affliction, God cutting the cords of the wicked, judgment on
those who hate Zion, resilience of God's people

Historical Context: Psalm 129 is the tenth Song of Ascents — a
national testimony of survival. Israel has been afflicted "from
my youth" (from Egypt onward), yet the enemy has NOT prevailed.
The vivid image of plowers making long furrows on Israel's back
(v.3) captures centuries of oppression: Egypt, Assyria, Babylon,
and beyond. Yet the defiant refrain: "they have not prevailed
against me." The second half calls for judgment on Zion's enemies,
comparing them to grass on rooftops that withers before being
pulled — fruitless and temporary.

Structure:
- Afflicted but Not Defeated (vv.1-4)
- Prayer Against Zion's Enemies (vv.5-8)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.1              | Hosea       | 11      | 1                  | "When Israel was a child... I called my son out of Egypt." Affliction from youth
v.3              | Isaiah      | 50      | 6                  | "I gave my back to the smiters." Christ fulfilled this imagery
v.4              | Nahum       | 1       | 13                 | "I will break his yoke from off thee, and will burst thy bonds in sunder."
v.6              | Psalm       | 37      | 2                  | "They shall soon be cut down like the grass, and wither as the green herb."

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. Affliction does not mean defeat (v.2). Being attacked doesn't mean you've
   lost. God's people have been afflicted throughout history — and have
   NEVER been prevailed against. Survival is its own testimony.

2. God cuts the cords (v.4). Whatever has been plowing your back —
   oppression, abuse, injustice — God is righteous and He cuts those cords.
   Liberation comes from His hand.

3. The enemies of God's people are temporary (vv.5-8). Like rooftop grass —
   visible but fruitless, temporary, withering. Those who oppose God's
   people have no lasting harvest.

------------------------------------

  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/psalms-129/

BibleProject Podcast:
  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

130.notes:
Psalms - Chapter 130
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
A Song of Ascents (penitential psalm) — 'Out of the depths have I cried unto thee, O LORD'; if He marked iniquities, none could stand; but there is forgiveness with Him; 'I wait for the LORD, my soul doth wait.'

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Multiple authors — primarily David (73 psalms attributed),
plus Asaph (12), Sons of Korah (11), Solomon (2), Moses (1),
Heman (1), Ethan (1), and anonymous. The Psalter was compiled over
approximately 1000 years and served as Israel's hymnal and prayer
book. The book is divided into five 'books' (1-41, 42-72, 73-89,
90-106, 107-150), paralleling the five books of Moses. Key themes:
worship, lament, praise, trust, kingship, creation, wisdom,
Messianic prophecy, and the full range of human emotion brought
before God.

Classification: Song of Ascents / Penitential Psalm (De Profundis)
Attributed Author: Anonymous
Key Themes: Crying from the depths, God's forgiveness, waiting
on God, hope in God's word, plenteous redemption, the fear that
comes from forgiveness

Historical Context: Psalm 130 is the sixth of the seven
Penitential Psalms (6, 32, 38, 51, 102, 130, 143) and one of the
most profound in the Psalter. Known by its Latin name "De
Profundis" ("Out of the depths"), it has been prayed at deathbeds,
funerals, and in times of spiritual anguish for two millennia.
Martin Luther identified this as one of the "Pauline psalms"
(along with 32, 51, and 143) because of its emphasis on
forgiveness by grace, not merit. The psalm moves from the depths
(v.1) upward through forgiveness (vv.3-4), waiting (vv.5-6), to
confident hope for all Israel (vv.7-8). It is a complete gospel
in miniature: sin acknowledged, grace received, hope restored.

Structure:
- Cry from the Depths (vv.1-2)
- Forgiveness as the Basis of Fear (vv.3-4)
- Waiting on God's Word (vv.5-6)
- Hope for Complete Redemption (vv.7-8)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.1              | Jonah       | 2       | 2                  | "Out of the belly of hell cried I." Another cry from the depths
v.3              | Romans      | 3       | 20                 | "By the deeds of the law there shall no flesh be justified."
v.4              | 1 John      | 1       | 9                  | "If we confess our sins, he is faithful and just to forgive us."
v.4              | Romans      | 2       | 4                  | "The goodness of God leadeth thee to repentance." Grace produces reverence
v.6              | Romans      | 8       | 23-25              | Waiting with eager expectation for redemption
v.7              | Ephesians   | 1       | 7                  | "In whom we have redemption through his blood, the forgiveness of sins, according to the riches of his grace."
v.8              | Titus       | 2       | 14                 | Christ "gave himself for us, that he might redeem us from all iniquity."

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.3              | KJV         | "mark iniquities" / ESV: "kept a record of sins" — Hebrew "shamar avonot" = to watch/guard/keep iniquities. If God maintained a permanent record of every sin, prosecution would be absolute and universal. But He doesn't. This is the gospel.
v.4              | KJV         | "that thou mayest be feared" — Counterintuitive. Forgiveness leads to fear? Yes — the proper response to undeserved grace is not casual familiarity but profound reverence. Cheap grace produces presumption; true grace produces holy awe.
v.6              | KJV         | "more than they that watch for the morning" — Night watchmen (soldiers, city guards) long desperately for dawn — for relief, for safety, for the end of darkness. The psalmist's longing for God exceeds even this.

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. Cry from where you are (v.1). You don't need to clean up before praying.
   The depths are a valid starting point. God doesn't require you to climb
   up first — He hears from the abyss.

2. If God kept score, no one would stand (v.3). This levels every person.
   The "good" person and the "bad" person are equally unable to stand before
   God's record-keeping. Only grace differentiates.

3. Forgiveness produces reverence (v.4). If you find yourself taking God's
   grace lightly, you haven't understood it yet. True forgiveness produces
   not carelessness but awe — "that thou mayest be FEARED."

4. Wait like a watchman for morning (v.6). If you're in a dark night of the
   soul, remember: morning WILL come. Wait for it with the certainty of
   dawn. God's faithfulness is as sure as sunrise.

5. Plenteous redemption (v.7). Whatever your sin, God's redemption is more.
   Not barely enough, not reluctantly given — PLENTEOUS. He will redeem
   Israel from ALL iniquities (v.8). All. Not some. All.

RESOURCES
------------------------------------
The Bible Project — Psalms Overview:
  https://bibleproject.com/explore/video/psalms/

David Guzik — Psalms 130 Written Commentary:
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/psalms-130/

BibleProject Podcast:
  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

Commentary sources: Enduring Word (enduringword.com), Charles
Spurgeon (Public Domain)

131.notes:
Psalms - Chapter 131
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
A Song of Ascents of David — the shortest Song of Ascents: David professes childlike humility and quieted contentment before God, like a weaned child with its mother; 'Let Israel hope in the LORD.'

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Multiple authors — primarily David (73 psalms attributed),
plus Asaph (12), Sons of Korah (11), Solomon (2), Moses (1),
Heman (1), Ethan (1), and anonymous. The Psalter was compiled over
approximately 1000 years and served as Israel's hymnal and prayer
book. The book is divided into five 'books' (1-41, 42-72, 73-89,
90-106, 107-150), paralleling the five books of Moses. Key themes:
worship, lament, praise, trust, kingship, creation, wisdom,
Messianic prophecy, and the full range of human emotion brought
before God.

Classification: Song of Ascents / Psalm of Trust
Attributed Author: David (superscription: "A Song of degrees
of David")
Key Themes: Humility before God, contentment like a weaned child,
quieting the soul, not striving beyond one's place, childlike
trust

Historical Context: Psalm 131 is one of the shortest and most
tender psalms — only three verses. David, the king of Israel,
describes himself as a weaned child on its mother's lap. The
weaned child (typically 2-3 years old in ancient Israel) no
longer nurses anxiously but rests contentedly with the mother —
needing nothing but presence. This is extraordinary from a warrior
king: he has deliberately set aside ambition, pride, and
striving for things "too high" for him. Spurgeon called this
psalm "one of the shortest to read but one of the longest to
learn." It is the psalm of spiritual maturity through chosen
humility.

Structure:
- Negation: What David Does NOT Do (v.1)
- Positive Image: A Weaned Child (v.2)
- Call to Israel: Hope (v.3)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.1              | Romans      | 12      | 3                  | "Not to think of himself more highly than he ought to think."
v.2              | Matthew     | 18      | 3-4                | "Except ye be converted, and become as little children."
v.2              | Isaiah      | 30      | 15                 | "In quietness and in confidence shall be your strength."
v.2              | Philippians | 4       | 11                 | "I have learned, in whatsoever state I am, therewith to be content."

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.2              | KJV         | "weaned" (Hebrew "gamul") = completed, matured. The weaned child has moved past infantile need into quiet contentment. This is not regression to infancy but growth into rest.
v.1              | KJV         | "things too high for me" — Hebrew "niphla'ot" = wonderful/ extraordinary things beyond my capacity. David doesn't deny mystery — he accepts what is beyond him without striving.

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. Choose humility (v.1). Humility is not weakness — it's the king of Israel
   refusing to overreach. Don't exercise yourself in matters too great for
   you. Not everything is your burden.

2. Become a weaned child (v.2). Stop screaming for what you don't have.
   Learn contentment. The weaned child has nothing in hand but rests in the
   mother's arms — satisfied with PRESENCE alone. Can you be satisfied with
   God Himself without His gifts?

3. This psalm is the antidote to ambition-anxiety. The constant striving for
   more, higher, greater — David renounces it. Rest. Be content. Not
   everything "high" is yours to reach for.

4. Three verses. The greatest truths sometimes need the fewest words. Entire
   libraries on humility couldn't say it better.

RESOURCES
------------------------------------
The Bible Project — Psalms Overview:

5. until I find a place for the LORD, a dwelling place for the Mighty One of Jacob.”

132.notes:
Psalms - Chapter 132
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
A Song of Ascents — recalls David's vow to find a dwelling for the LORD and God's covenant choice of Zion and David's dynasty: 'The LORD hath sworn in truth unto David; he will not turn from it.'

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Multiple authors — primarily David (73 psalms attributed),
plus Asaph (12), Sons of Korah (11), Solomon (2), Moses (1),
Heman (1), Ethan (1), and anonymous. The Psalter was compiled over
approximately 1000 years and served as Israel's hymnal and prayer
book. The book is divided into five 'books' (1-41, 42-72, 73-89,
90-106, 107-150), paralleling the five books of Moses. Key themes:
worship, lament, praise, trust, kingship, creation, wisdom,
Messianic prophecy, and the full range of human emotion brought
before God.

Classification: Song of Ascents / Royal/Messianic Psalm
Attributed Author: Anonymous (superscription: "A Song of degrees")
Key Themes: David's zeal for God's dwelling, the Davidic covenant,
God choosing Zion, the anointed king's throne, God's faithful
oath, priestly blessing

Historical Context: Psalm 132 is the longest of the Songs of
Ascents (18 verses) and the most theological. It celebrates two
divine elections: God chose DAVID's line (vv.11-12) and God chose
ZION as His dwelling (vv.13-14). It recalls David's passionate
vow to find a dwelling place for God (2 Sam 7; 1 Chr 13-16),
connecting David's devotion to God's covenant promise. The psalm
was likely used at royal coronations or temple dedications. Its
Messianic implications are clear: God swore to David a perpetual
throne — fulfilled ultimately in Christ, the eternal son of David.

Structure:
- David's Zeal for God's House (vv.1-5)
- Finding the Ark (vv.6-9)
- God's Covenant with David (vv.10-12)
- God Chooses Zion (vv.13-18)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.5              | 2 Samuel    | 7       | 2                  | David's desire to build God a house
v.8              | 2 Chronicles | 6       | 41                 | Solomon quotes this at temple dedication
v.11             | 2 Samuel    | 7       | 12-16              | The Davidic covenant
v.11             | Luke        | 1       | 32-33              | "The Lord God shall give unto him the throne of his father David."
v.14             | Revelation  | 21      | 3                  | "The tabernacle of God is with men, and he will dwell with them."
v.17             | Luke        | 1       | 69                 | "He hath raised up an horn of salvation for us in the house of his servant David."

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. David's passion was God's dwelling (v.5). What consumes YOU? David
   wouldn't sleep until God had a place. This zeal for God's presence should
   mark His people.

2. God swears and doesn't change His mind (v.11). The Davidic covenant is
   unconditional. God's promises to you are equally irrevocable — sealed
   with His oath.

3. God desires to dwell with His people (v.14). The infinite God says "here
   will I dwell, for I have DESIRED it." You are desired by God as His
   dwelling place.

4. Blessing flows from God's chosen place (vv.15-18). Where God chooses to
   dwell, provision abounds, priests are clothed in righteousness, and the
   anointed lamp never goes out.

  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/psalms-132/

Spurgeon (Public Domain)

133.notes:
Psalms - Chapter 133
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
A Song of Ascents of David — 'Behold, how good and how pleasant it is for brethren to dwell together in unity!'; like precious oil on Aaron's head, like dew on Hermon — there the LORD commands blessing and life.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Multiple authors — primarily David (73 psalms attributed),
plus Asaph (12), Sons of Korah (11), Solomon (2), Moses (1),
Heman (1), Ethan (1), and anonymous. The Psalter was compiled over
approximately 1000 years and served as Israel's hymnal and prayer
book. The book is divided into five 'books' (1-41, 42-72, 73-89,
90-106, 107-150), paralleling the five books of Moses. Key themes:
worship, lament, praise, trust, kingship, creation, wisdom,
Messianic prophecy, and the full range of human emotion brought
before God.

Classification: Song of Ascents / Wisdom Psalm
Attributed Author: David (superscription: "A Song of degrees
of David")
Key Themes: The goodness of unity, anointing that flows from head
to body, refreshing dew from above, commanded blessing, brethren
dwelling together

Historical Context: Psalm 133 is one of the most beloved short
psalms — only 3 verses celebrating the beauty of UNITY among
God's people. When pilgrims from all twelve tribes gathered in
Jerusalem for festivals, this psalm captured the joy of
togetherness. David (who unified the kingdom after years of civil
war) was uniquely qualified to praise unity. The two images —
anointing oil flowing down Aaron's beard and Hermon's dew
descending on Zion — both move DOWNWARD: blessing flows from above
onto the gathered community. Where unity exists, God COMMANDS
blessing. Unity doesn't earn blessing; it's where God designates
it.

Structure:
- Declaration: How Good Unity Is (v.1)
- Image 1: Anointing Oil (v.2)
- Image 2: Hermon's Dew (v.3a)
- God Commands Blessing There (v.3b)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.1              | John        | 17      | 21                 | Jesus prayed "that they all may be one."
v.1              | Ephesians   | 4       | 3                  | "Endeavouring to keep the unity of the Spirit in the bond of peace."
v.2              | Exodus      | 30      | 25-30              | The holy anointing oil recipe for Aaron's consecration
v.3              | ?           | ?       | ?                  | Hermon — The highest mountain in Israel (9,000+ ft), source of dew and water for the region.

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.1              | KJV         | "dwell together" — Hebrew "shevet achim gam-yachad" = brothers also sitting/dwelling together. Not just proximity but intentional togetherness. "Gam" (also/even) adds emphasis: even TOGETHER, which is remarkable.
v.2              | KJV         | The anointing oil (Exodus 30) contained myrrh, cinnamon, calamus, cassia, and olive oil — extremely precious and aromatic. Unity fills the room like sacred perfume.

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. Unity is both good AND pleasant (v.1). Not just doctrinally correct but
   genuinely enjoyable. If your community has truth without pleasantness,
   something is missing.

2. Unity flows down from the head (v.2). True unity starts with the Head
   (Christ) and flows to the body. It's not manufactured from below but
   received from above.

3. God COMMANDS blessing where there's unity (v.3). Want to know where God
   sends blessing? Where His people dwell together. If you want commanded
   blessing, pursue unity.

4. Only 3 verses — because unity is simple, not complicated. We complicate
   what God made beautiful. Dwell together. That's it.

------------------------------------

  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/psalms-133/

BibleProject Podcast:
  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

Commentary sources: Enduring Word (enduringword.com), Charles
Spurgeon (Public Domain)

134.notes:
Psalms - Chapter 134
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
A Song of Ascents — the final Song of Ascents: a call to the night-watch servants in the temple to bless the LORD, and a benediction: 'The LORD that made heaven and earth bless thee out of Zion.'

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Multiple authors — primarily David (73 psalms attributed),
plus Asaph (12), Sons of Korah (11), Solomon (2), Moses (1),
Heman (1), Ethan (1), and anonymous. The Psalter was compiled over
approximately 1000 years and served as Israel's hymnal and prayer
book. The book is divided into five 'books' (1-41, 42-72, 73-89,
90-106, 107-150), paralleling the five books of Moses. Key themes:
worship, lament, praise, trust, kingship, creation, wisdom,
Messianic prophecy, and the full range of human emotion brought
before God.

Classification: Song of Ascents / Hymn of Praise
Attributed Author: Anonymous (superscription: "A Song of degrees")
Key Themes: Night worship, blessing the LORD, lifting hands in
the sanctuary, priestly blessing, the Creator who blesses

Historical Context: Psalm 134 is the final Song of Ascents (15th
of 15) — a brief evening benediction of 3 verses. The collection
that began in distress and exile (Psalm 120) concludes at the
temple at night, with worshipers calling priests/Levites to bless
the LORD during the night watch, and receiving blessing in return.
The pattern: worshipers bless God (vv.1-2), then God blesses
worshipers (v.3). It is a fitting conclusion to the pilgrim
journey — arrival at the temple, nighttime worship, mutual
blessing between God and His people. The journey is complete.

Structure:
- Call to the Night-Servants to Bless God (vv.1-2)
- God's Blessing in Return (v.3)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.1              | 1 Chronicles | 9       | 33                 | Levites who served day and night in the temple
v.2              | 1 Timothy   | 2       | 8                  | "Lifting up holy hands."
v.3              | Numbers     | 6       | 24-26              | The Aaronic blessing
v.3              | Psalm       | 121     | 2                  | "My help cometh from the LORD, which made heaven and earth."

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. Worship in the night (v.1). Night seasons — both literal and spiritual —
   are times for worship, not just waiting. Bless God in the darkness.

2. Bless God; God blesses you (vv.1-3). The exchange of blessing: you bless
   Him with praise, He blesses you from Zion. Worship is not
   one-directional.

3. The pilgrim journey is complete. From the distress of Psalm 120 to the
   night-rest of Psalm 134, the worshiper has arrived. The Songs of Ascents
   end in the temple, in worship, in blessing. Journey over. Home.

135.ESV:
1. Praise the LORD! Praise the name of the LORD, give praise, O servants of the LORD,
2. who stand in the house of the LORD, in the courts of the house of our God!
3. Praise the LORD, for the LORD is good; sing to his name, for it is pleasant!
4. For the LORD has chosen Jacob for himself, Israel as his own possession.
5. For I know that the LORD is great, and that our Lord is above all gods.
6. Whatever the LORD pleases, he does, in heaven and on earth, in the seas and all deeps.
7. He it is who makes the clouds rise at the end of the earth, who makes lightnings for the rain and brings forth the wind from his storehouses.
8. He it was who struck down the firstborn of Egypt, both of man and of beast;
9. who in your midst, O Egypt, sent signs and wonders against Pharaoh and all his servants;
10. who struck down many nations and killed mighty kings,
11. Sihon, king of the Amorites, and Og, king of Bashan, and all the kingdoms of Canaan,
12. and gave their land as a heritage, a heritage to his people Israel.
13. Your name, O LORD, endures forever, your renown, O LORD, throughout all ages.
14. For the LORD will vindicate his people and have compassion on his servants.
15. The idols of the nations are silver and gold, the work of human hands.
16. They have mouths, but do not speak; they have eyes, but do not see;
17. they have ears, but do not hear, nor is there any breath in their mouths.
18. Those who make them become like them; so do all who trust in them.
19. O house of Israel, bless the LORD! O house of Aaron, bless the LORD!
20. O house of Levi, bless the LORD! You who fear the LORD, bless the LORD!
21. Blessed be the LORD from Zion, he who dwells in Jerusalem! Praise the LORD!

2. who serve in the Lord’s temple, in the courts of the temple of our God.

11. Sihon, king of the Amorites, and Og, king of Bashan, and all the kingdoms of Canaan.
12. He gave their land as an inheritance, as an inheritance to Israel his people.
13. O Lord, your name endures, your reputation, O Lord, lasts.
14. For the Lord vindicates his people and has compassion on his servants.
15. The nations’ idols are made of silver and gold; they are man-made.
16. They have mouths, but cannot speak, eyes, but cannot see,
17. and ears, but cannot hear. Indeed, they cannot breathe.
18. Those who make them will end up like them, as will everyone who trusts in them.
19. O family of Israel, praise the Lord. O family of Aaron, praise the Lord.
20. O family of Levi, praise the Lord. You loyal followers of the Lord, praise the Lord.
21. The Lord deserves praise in Zion— he who dwells in Jerusalem. Praise the Lord.

1. Praise Yah! Praise Yahweh’s name! Praise him, you servants of Yahweh,
2. you who stand in Yahweh’s house, in the courts of our God’s house.
3. Praise Yah, for Yahweh is good. Sing praises to his name, for that is pleasant.
4. For Yah has chosen Jacob for himself; Israel for his own possession.
5. For I know that Yahweh is great, that our Lord is above all gods.
6. Whatever Yahweh pleased, that he has done, in heaven and in earth, in the seas and in all deeps;
7. who causes the clouds to rise from the ends of the earth; who makes lightnings with the rain; who brings the wind out of his treasuries;
8. Who struck the firstborn of Egypt, both of man and animal;
9. Who sent signs and wonders into the middle of you, Egypt, on Pharaoh, and on all his servants;
10. who struck many nations, and killed mighty kings,
11. Sihon king of the Amorites, Og king of Bashan, and all the kingdoms of Canaan,
12. and gave their land for a heritage, a heritage to Israel, his people.
13. Your name, Yahweh, endures forever; your renown, Yahweh, throughout all generations.
14. For Yahweh will judge his people, and have compassion on his servants.
15. The idols of the nations are silver and gold, the work of men’s hands.
16. They have mouths, but they can’t speak. They have eyes, but they can’t see.
17. They have ears, but they can’t hear; neither is there any breath in their mouths.
18. Those who make them will be like them; yes, everyone who trusts in them.
19. House of Israel, praise Yahweh! House of Aaron, praise Yahweh!
20. House of Levi, praise Yahweh! You who fear Yahweh, praise Yahweh!
21. Blessed be Yahweh from Zion, Who dwells at Jerusalem. Praise Yah!
135.notes:
Psalms - Chapter 135
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
A Hallel hymn of praise — 'Praise the LORD, for the LORD is good'; He does whatever pleases Him in heaven and earth; He struck Egypt and Canaan's kings; idols are worthless, but Israel blesses the LORD from Zion.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Multiple authors — primarily David (73 psalms attributed),
plus Asaph (12), Sons of Korah (11), Solomon (2), Moses (1),
Heman (1), Ethan (1), and anonymous. The Psalter was compiled over
approximately 1000 years and served as Israel's hymnal and prayer
book. The book is divided into five 'books' (1-41, 42-72, 73-89,
90-106, 107-150), paralleling the five books of Moses. Key themes:
worship, lament, praise, trust, kingship, creation, wisdom,
Messianic prophecy, and the full range of human emotion brought
before God.

Classification: Hymn of Praise / Liturgical Psalm
Attributed Author: Anonymous
Key Themes: God's sovereignty over nature and nations, the
superiority of the LORD over idols, God's covenant name, praise
as the calling of His servants, the futility of idolatry

Historical Context: Psalm 135 is a compilation psalm — drawing
from many earlier texts (Psalms 113, 115, 134, Exodus, Deuteronomy)
to create a comprehensive liturgical praise. It combines creation
theology, Exodus history, and anti-idol polemic into one hymn for
temple worship. Positioned between the Songs of Ascents (120-134)
and the Great Hallel (136), it serves as a transitional praise
that summarizes major themes: God's election of Israel (v.4), His
power over nature (vv.6-7), His deliverance from Egypt (vv.8-12),
and the emptiness of idols (vv.15-18).

Structure:
- Call to Praise (vv.1-4)
- God's Greatness in Nature (vv.5-7)
- God's Acts in History (vv.8-12)
- God's Eternal Name (vv.13-14)
- Futility of Idols (vv.15-18)
- Call to Bless the LORD (vv.19-21)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.4              | Deuteronomy | 7       | 6                  | "The LORD thy God hath chosen thee."
v.6              | Psalm       | 115     | 3                  | "Our God is in the heavens: he hath done whatsoever he hath pleased."
v.7              | Jeremiah    | 10      | 13                 | "He causeth the vapours to ascend."
v.15-18          | Psalm       | 115     | 4-8                | Nearly identical idol satire
v.14             | Deuteronomy | 32      | 36                 | Source text quoted here

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. God does whatever He pleases (v.6). There is no domain beyond His
   sovereignty. Heaven, earth, seas, depths — all under His will. Rest in a
   God who is truly in control.

2. He chose you (v.4). "The LORD hath chosen Jacob unto himself." Election
   is personal — God chose you for Himself. You are His peculiar treasure.

3. Idols are still empty (vv.15-18). Modern idols don't look like silver
   statues, but they share the same quality: they cannot see, hear, or save.
   Whatever you trust besides God is equally impotent.

4. Praise is pleasant (v.3). Don't approach worship as medicine to endure.
   It is PLEASANT — enjoyable, satisfying, good for you and delightful to
   experience.

RESOURCES
------------------------------------
The Bible Project — Psalms Overview:
  https://bibleproject.com/explore/video/psalms/

David Guzik — Psalms 135 Written Commentary:
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/psalms-135/

BibleProject Podcast:
  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

========================================
Study Notes generated for personal Bible study use.
Translations referenced: KJV, ESV, ASV, NET, WEB
Commentary sources: Enduring Word (enduringword.com), Charles
Spurgeon (Public Domain)

15. But overthrew Pharaoh and his host in the Red sea: for his mercy endureth for ever.

136.notes:
Psalms - Chapter 136
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
The Great Hallel — a litany of thanksgiving with the refrain 'for his mercy endureth for ever' repeated in all 26 verses, recounting creation, the Exodus, wilderness provision, and the conquest.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Multiple authors — primarily David (73 psalms attributed),
plus Asaph (12), Sons of Korah (11), Solomon (2), Moses (1),
Heman (1), Ethan (1), and anonymous. The Psalter was compiled over
approximately 1000 years and served as Israel's hymnal and prayer
book. The book is divided into five 'books' (1-41, 42-72, 73-89,
90-106, 107-150), paralleling the five books of Moses. Key themes:
worship, lament, praise, trust, kingship, creation, wisdom,
Messianic prophecy, and the full range of human emotion brought
before God.

Classification: Historical Hymn / Responsive Liturgical Psalm
(The Great Hallel)
Attributed Author: Anonymous (known as "The Great Hallel" in
Jewish tradition)
Key Themes: God's mercy (hesed) enduring forever — repeated 26
times, God as Creator, God as Redeemer from Egypt, God as Provider,
the connection between mercy and every divine act

Historical Context: Psalm 136 is unique in the Psalter — every
single verse (all 26) ends with the same refrain: "for his mercy
endureth for ever" (Hebrew: "ki le-olam hasdo"). This was sung
antiphonally: a leader sang the first half, and the congregation
responded with the refrain. It was sung at temple dedications
(2 Chr 7:3), before battles (2 Chr 20:21), and at Passover. Known
in Jewish tradition as "The Great Hallel," it surveys all of
salvation history — creation (vv.4-9), Exodus (vv.10-22), and
ongoing providence (vv.23-25) — declaring that God's hesed
(covenant love/mercy) underlies EVERY act. The relentless
repetition is not laziness but theology: mercy is not occasional
but CONSTANT in everything God does.

Structure:
- Call to Thanksgiving (vv.1-3)
- God's Mercy in Creation (vv.4-9)
- God's Mercy in the Exodus (vv.10-22)
- God's Mercy in Providence (vv.23-26)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.1              | Psalm       | 100     | 5                  | "His mercy is everlasting."
v.1              | 2 Chronicles | 7       | 3                  | Sung at Solomon's temple dedication
v.1              | 2 Chronicles | 20      | 21                 | Jehoshaphat sent singers before the army with this refrain
v.4              | Psalm       | 72      | 18                 | "God... who only doeth wondrous things."
v.5-9            | ?           | ?       | ?                  | Genesis 1 — Creation narrative paralleled.
v.23             | 1 Samuel    | 2       | 8                  | "He raiseth up the poor out of the dust."
v.25             | Matthew     | 5       | 45                 | "He maketh his sun to rise on the evil and on the good."

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
"his mercy endureth for ever" — Hebrew "ki le-olam hasdo" =
literally "for to eternity [is] His hesed." Hesed encompasses
mercy, lovingkindness, steadfast love, covenant faithfulness,
loyalty, and grace. No single English word captures it. The KJV's
"mercy" and ESV's "steadfast love" each catch one facet of this
diamond.

The 26-fold repetition: In Jewish numerology, 26 is the numerical
value of God's name YHVH (Yod=10, He=5, Vav=6, He=5). Whether
intentional or not, the number of repetitions matches the number
of God's Name.

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. His mercy is in EVERYTHING (all 26 verses). Creation — mercy. Judgment on
   Egypt — mercy. Wilderness leading — mercy. Daily food — mercy. There is
   no act of God that is divorced from His hesed. Look for mercy in
   everything He does.

2. Repetition is not laziness but emphasis. Saying it once is a statement.
   Saying it 26 times is a conviction, a theology, a worldview. Let "His
   mercy endures forever" become your refrain — the lens through which you
   see everything.

3. Even in "low estate" — mercy (v.23). Your lowest point is not outside
   God's mercy. He REMEMBERS you there. Low estate is where mercy meets you.

4. Sing this psalm antiphonally. This psalm was meant to be ANSWERED —
   leader and congregation. Find someone and practice responsive worship:
   they sing God's acts, you respond with "His mercy endures forever."

5. All flesh receives His provision (v.25). God's mercy is not exclusive. He
   feeds everyone. Common grace flows to all creation from His everlasting
   love.

RESOURCES
------------------------------------
The Bible Project — Psalms Overview:
  https://bibleproject.com/explore/video/psalms/

David Guzik — Psalms 136 Written Commentary:
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/psalms-136/

BibleProject Podcast:
  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

========================================
Study Notes generated for personal Bible study use.
Translations referenced: KJV, ESV, ASV, NET, WEB
Commentary sources: Enduring Word (enduringword.com), Charles
Spurgeon (Public Domain)

4. How shall we sing the LORD’s song in a foreign land?

6. Let my tongue stick to the roof of my mouth, if I do not remember you, if I do not set Jerusalem above my highest joy!
